Personal Claude Skills (43)

  • Content Creation Engine 📊 ★6-phase BOFU research + multi-platform scripting pipeline. Replaces Pantana's BOFU Video Engine + BOFU Blogger.

AI Library

AI Library

Complete reference archive captured May 13, 2026. 43 personal Claude skills, 72 library items, 20 community prompts, 12 lectures, 9 lessons. Personal skills are cross-referenced with library items — items with a green "Enhanced By" badge already have a personal-skill equivalent. Items with a "⚠️ Gap — Build" badge are flagged as candidates for new skills. Click any item to jump, or use the search box to filter.

🆕 2026-06 Additions (6)

New June 2026 items — ChatGPT Ads troubleshooting + the Jason Pantana coaching-folder ingest. (The private PropIQ 1:1 call is intentionally kept out of the library; it lives in the vault + PropIQ audit.)

Troubleshooting ChatGPT Ad Campaigns [Lessons]

Round-two ChatGPT Ads lesson — fixing live campaigns (impressions but no clicks).

  • Budget is a ceiling, not a charge; bid ~$7–8 (the ~$4 floor under-serves).
  • Decision tree: flatline→rebuild/raise budget; impressions-no-clicks→raise bid+fix offer; clicks-no-conversions→fix the landing page.
  • Landing page = 'catcher's mitt' (BOFU, one CTA, headshot, no forced reg).
  • New: no start/end dates; Business account only; financial-services copy gets flagged; Yelp×ChatGPT data deal now surfaces Yelp in most threads.

Source: https://aimarketingacademy.com/lessons/troubleshooting-chatgpt-ad-campaigns/ · Backup: vault 06 Coaching & Training/Jason Pantana/

AI Recommendation Engine (AEO) [Lectures]

How to get recommended by ChatGPT / Perplexity / Grok / Google AI Overviews.

  • YouTube = #1 most-cited domain in AI search; Reddit = #2.
  • AI can't read review content, but Zillow review COUNTS are now readable — spell awards/accolades out in your bio.
  • Win citations via proof pages / listicles using best/top/versus language; seed Reddit via happy clients (site:reddit.com).
  • Rotating citation sources: FastExpert, Yelp 10Best, RealTrends, HomeLight→US News, Zillow, RateMyAgent, Reddit.

Source: Pantana coaching folder (no public URL) · Backup: vault 06 Coaching & Training/Jason Pantana/

Teach Claude Your Business [Lectures]

The canonical Claude Skills teaching session (transcript behind the Claude Skills Agent Starter Pack).

  • GPTs / Gems / Projects / Spaces taxonomy; 80/20 across LLMs; start free, upgrade when the business needs it.
  • Why Skills beat GPTs/Gems on tokens: system prompts re-read every message; skills load on-demand.
  • Clean plain-English tokens / context-window / chat-compression explainer.
  • The 9-skill starter pack (Graeham already covers all nine).

Source: Pantana coaching folder (no public URL) · Backup: vault 06 Coaching & Training/Jason Pantana/

Sharran — Profitable Content Machine (REVB) [Guides]

Sharran Srivatsaa's lead-gen + nurture system (REVB decks, Parts 1+2).

  • Money Map: Channel → Hook → Conversion Mechanism → Lifetime Nurture ('find the gap, fill the gap').
  • Email Sprinkler System: ~3x/wk cadence, avoid Mon-AM/Fri-PM, super-signature, full nurture swipe-file.
  • 9-Point Opt-In Scorecard; 10-day Crash Course series.
  • Social Funnel: Tide Riser / Over Achiever / Hand Raiser + 9 engagement scripts; Creator's Advantage (paid amplifies proven organic).

Source: Pantana coaching folder (no public URL) · Backup: vault 06 Coaching & Training/Jason Pantana/

Automate & Convert (ManyChat IG DM) [Guides]

Instagram keyword→DM automation for lead capture (Katie Day & Tim Macy, REVB).

  • Keyword triggers (HOMES / MOVE / SELL) → automated DM flow, 80%+ DM open rates, no landing-page friction.
  • Flow design: delays, {{first_name}}, behavior tags; promote the keyword via bio / stories / Reels / pinned posts.
  • ManyChat free to 1,000 contacts — a distinct lead channel not otherwise covered.

Source: Pantana coaching folder (no public URL) · Backup: vault 06 Coaching & Training/Jason Pantana/

99 Video Scripts for Realtors (REVB) [Guides]

Swipe-file of 99 short-form video scripts — a hook/idea bank.

  • Buyers / Sellers / Investors / Personal Brand / General, each ~20–30s.
  • Reusable hook+body templates (rent-vs-buy, house hacking, VA grants, luxury showcases).
  • Localize before use — San Diego-specific in places. Feeds content-creation-engine / concept-forge / cinematic-hooks.

Source: Pantana coaching folder (no public URL) · Backup: vault 06 Coaching & Training/Jason Pantana/

Bots (22)

Bots

15 Custom GPTs and Gems Every Real Estate Agent Should Build

Copy, customize, and deploy 15 ready-made Custom GPTs, Gemini Gems, and/or Claude Projects/Skills.

View source →
A complete playbook for turning your 15 most repetitive marketing tasks into Custom GPTs (ChatGPT) and Gems (Gemini) that run on repeat. Each one produces a specific, finished asset — not brainstorming, not ideas, but deliverables you'd otherwise spend hours creating from scratch.

15 Custom GPTs and Gems Every Real Estate Agent Should Build

What This Is

A complete playbook for turning your 15 most repetitive marketing tasks into Custom GPTs (ChatGPT) and Gems (Gemini) that run on repeat. Each one produces a specific, finished asset — not brainstorming, not ideas, but deliverables you’d otherwise spend hours creating from scratch.

Every recurring marketing task you do can become a Custom GPT or Gem that performs that task to your exact spec, every single time. Set it up once. It remembers how you want it done — your brand, your voice, your formatting preferences, your compliance requirements. No more re-explaining yourself in every chat.

Custom GPTsGemsPlatformChatGPTGeminiRequiresChatGPT Plus ($20/mo) or higherFree Google account (Gemini Advanced recommended)Image generationImage 1.5Nano Banana 2Video generationSora – coming soonVeo 3Search/Deep researchModerateStrongHow to buildWrite instructions + upload files → save as Custom GPTWrite instructions + upload files → save as Gemini Gem

Which one should you use? Depends on the task. Some features are platform-exclusive — image generation works differently on each, Gemini has Veo for video and stronger web search. Each entry below notes which platform works best. When both work equally well, pick whichever you’re more comfortable with.

New to building these? These AiM resources will get you started:

How to Create a ChatGPT Custom GPT — step-by-step setup from scratch

Custom GPTs vs. Projects — which format fits which task

GPTs for Everyday Marketing — the Ai Rookie lecture that covers what custom GPTs are and why they matter for your marketing

Quick Reference

Use this table to find the GPT or Gem you need and see which platforms support it. Scroll down for full details, system instructions, and linked resources.

#GPT / GemChatGPTGeminiOther1Email Newsletters✅—Claude (recommended for HTML)2Blog Posts✅ (recommended)✅Claude3Social Captions✅✅Claude4Carousel Design✅ (image 1.5)✅ (Nano Banana 2)—5Thumbnails & Covers—✅ (Nano Banana 2)—6Branded Stats Graphics—✅ (Nano Banana 2)—7Property Flyers✅ (image 1.5)✅ (Nano Banana 2)—8Listing Remarks✅ (recommended)✅Claude9Photo Captions✅ (required)——10Listing Motion—✅ (Veo 3 only)—11Video Scripts✅ (recommended)✅Claude12Ad Creative (Meta)✅✅Claude12Ad Creative (Google)✅✅Claude13Market Analysis✅ (recommended)—Claude14Area Research✅✅Perplexity, Grok15Property Intel✅✅Perplexity, Grok + NotebookLM

  1. Email Newsletter Generator

Writes your weekly or monthly email campaigns start to finish — layout, copy, subject lines, and platform-ready code or text you can paste directly into your email provider. Works on ChatGPT or Gemini.

Note: Claude is the strongest option for coding HTML emails — but since this guide focuses on Custom GPTs and Gemini Gems specifically, the resources below are built for those platforms. If you use Claude, the same system instructions work in a Claude Project or Skill.

AiM resources cover this one completely. Pick the one that fits your email style:

HTML/CSS Email Newsletter Generator — Fully designed, branded HTML newsletters with your colors, fonts, images, and buttons. Adapts to your email platform (Mailchimp, Follow Up Boss, Flodesk, etc.).

Plain-Text Email Newsletter Generator — Conversational, no-design-needed newsletters. Handles merge tags, spam filter avoidance, and platform formatting.

HTML Market Update Email Newsletter — Market update emails specifically. Upload MLS data, get a designed HTML email with charts and data narrative.

Personalized Market Update Email Built from MLS Data — Builds a personalized market update email from your MLS export. More text-forward than the HTML version above.

Listing Announcement Email Blast — A one-off branded HTML email for a new listing announcement (not a recurring newsletter — a single campaign).

Each resource page includes complete system instructions you can copy and paste into a Custom GPT, Gem, or Claude Project.

  1. Blog Post Writer

Turns any topic, transcript, or keyword data into a publish-ready blog post — SEO-optimized, locally relevant, and written in your voice. Best on ChatGPT (recommended for web research capability) or Claude.

AiM resources cover this one completely:

Custom GPT That Writes Your Blogs — The primary tool. Handles five blog types (local area, market insights, buyer/seller advice, listing spotlights, repurposed video), includes compliance guardrails, and walks you through every decision. Available as a pre-built GPT you can launch instantly or system instructions you can customize.

Automated BOFU Blogger — System Setup — The full setup guide for building an automated bottom-of-funnel blog system. This connects your keyword data to an AI writing pipeline that produces hyper-local, search-optimized blog posts on repeat.

Blog Posts from Seller Search Trends — Two-prompt system: generates 50 localized blog titles from keyword data, then expands any title into a full post.

  1. Social Caption Writer

Writes post copy matched to any platform — Instagram, Facebook, LinkedIn, or X. Adjusts length, tone, hashtag strategy, and formatting for each one. Works on ChatGPT or Gemini.

This one is new. No existing AiM resource covers a general-purpose social caption GPT, so here are the system instructions to build one. Upload 5–10 of your best past captions or transcripts to the GPT’s Knowledge section — that’s how it learns your voice.

Prompt

Copy

## PurposeYou write platform-optimized social media captions for a real estate professional. Every caption is on-brand, engagement-driven, and ready to paste. You adapt length, tone, structure, and formatting to the specific platform and post format.—## Behavior Overview### TriggerWhen the user says something like:– “Write a caption for [topic]”– “Post this on Instagram”– “I need social copy for [listing / stat / event]”– “Caption for all platforms”Begin the caption workflow. If no platform is specified, ask which one(s) they want before writing.### Voice MatchingIf writing samples have been uploaded to the Knowledge section, analyze them and match the user’s unique voice, tone, and diction as closely as possible. All captions must reflect this style consistently. If no samples are uploaded, write in a conversational, confident tone — professional but not corporate. The user’s explicit style requests always take priority over uploaded samples.—## Intake FlowAsk the user for the following in one message:> To write your caption, I need a few quick details. Reply with whatever you’ve got:>> – Topic or content idea (listing, market stat, event, tip, behind-the-scenes, etc.)> – Platform(s): Instagram, Facebook, LinkedIn, X, or “all”> – Tone preference (optional): educational, conversational, bold, luxury, casual> – Call to action (optional): comment, DM, link in bio, share, save> – Any hashtags, keywords, or phrases to include> – What’s the visual? (photo, carousel, Reel, text graphic — helps me match the copy to the format)If some details are missing, proceed with what is provided — just acknowledge anything that’s unclear or skipped.CRITICAL — If the user provides all the info upfront in a single message, do NOT ask them to repeat it. Acknowledge what they gave you and go straight to writing.—## Writing the Opening LineThe opening line is the most important line in every caption on every platform. It’s what shows before the truncation (“…more” on Instagram, “see more” on LinkedIn). Bad opens = nobody reads the rest. Get this wrong and nothing else matters.Strong opens:– Lead with a benefit or insight, not a setup (“Email outperforms every social platform you’re on” not “I wanted to share something about email”)– State something bold or contrarian (“Strategy beats spend” not “Strategy can sometimes be important”)– Ask a real question that makes them think (“What would it look like if you never wrote a listing description from scratch again?” not “Have you ever thought about using AI?”)– Reference something timely — a tool update, market shift, or behavioral insightWeak opens to avoid:

Bots

Automated BOFU Blogger — Cowork Agent

Research, score, and write bottom-of-funnel blog posts automatically — with deeper topic discovery than the original Zapier version and zero external tools to manage.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your Content Creation Engine — same blog-writing capability as Phase 5 output, integrated with your richer research pipeline.
A ground-up revision of the Automated BOFU Blogger — rebuilt for Claude Cowork. If you set up the original version with Zapier, Google Sheets, Apps Script, and API keys for OpenAI and Perplexity, that system still works. But this isn't just the same workflow on a different platform.

Automated BOFU Blogger — Cowork Agent

What This Is

This is a ground-up revision of the Automated BOFU Blogger — rebuilt for Claude Cowork. If you set up the original version with Zapier, Google Sheets, Apps Script, and API keys for OpenAI and Perplexity, that system still works. But this isn’t just the same workflow on a different platform. The research mechanism is fundamentally different, the writing quality is higher, and the entire multi-tool stack has been replaced by a single desktop agent.

What changed — and why it matters: The original BOFU Blogger asked Perplexity for 5 topic ideas in a single API call. That was the entire research step. One prompt, one response, five suggestions. This version replaces that with the same research engine that powers the BOFU Video Engine — it generates 230+ localized search queries tailored to your market and runs them across Google, YouTube, Reddit, Zillow, Realtor.com, Redfin, BiggerPockets, City-Data, Nextdoor, and NerdWallet. It deep-dives into Reddit threads and YouTube comments to extract real questions from real buyers and sellers. Then it scores every discovered topic on five dimensions — inquiry type, decision-stage intent, source confirmation, emotional urgency, and local relevance — before selecting the strongest one to write about. That’s a categorically different level of research.

No Zapier subscription. No OpenAI API key. No Perplexity API key. No Google Sheet. No Apps Script. No RSS feed. No XML export. Everything runs inside Claude — one tool, one subscription.

The BOFU Blogger is a Cowork agent that researches bottom-of-funnel topics in your market, picks the strongest one, checks it against your existing blog, writes a complete SEO/AEO-optimized post, and delivers it — on a schedule, without you lifting a finger.

Here’s what it does on every run:

Refreshes your blog library by scanning your live site — so it always knows what you’ve already published before it does anything else

Generates 230+ localized search queries tailored to your city, neighborhoods, audience, and your state/province’s specific real estate process terminology

Searches 9+ platforms — Google People Also Ask, YouTube, Reddit, Zillow, Realtor.com, Redfin, BiggerPockets, City-Data, Nextdoor, NerdWallet — pulling real questions from real buyers and sellers. The focus is on two types of questions: process questions (“how much are closing costs,” “what happens after the inspection,” “should I sell before buying”) and property questions (“new construction in [neighborhood],” “what does flood zone X mean,” “what’s being built near [development]”). These are the questions people ask when they’re approaching a decision — not casually browsing.

Deep-dives into Reddit threads and YouTube comments to find the exact questions, frustrations, and concerns people express when they’re approaching a real estate decision or navigating one

Scans for current local news — new construction, zoning changes, employer relocations, development activity in your market

Scores every discovered topic on five dimensions — inquiry type, decision-stage intent, how many platforms confirmed it, emotional urgency, and local relevance — then ranks the top 7–10

Checks for duplicates against your existing blog and prior runs — if the top pick overlaps with something you recently published, it logs a recommendation to refresh that existing post (with specific suggestions), skips writing a competing piece, and moves to the next-best topic instead

Writes a complete blog post — SEO title, meta description, an AI snippet summary at the top designed to be pulled by Google AI Overview and other AI search engines, 1,200–1,800 word body, FAQ section, JSON-LD schema markup, internal links to your existing content, CTAs, and author attribution

Delivers it as an HTML file, a Markdown file, and optionally as a Google Doc, a draft on your blog via API connector, or a draft via Chrome — your choice

Notifies you when it’s done — via iMessage, Slack, Zapier, or a file in the folder

You review. You publish. The system handles everything else.

This isn’t a prompt you paste into ChatGPT. It’s a desktop agent — it plans the work, executes every step, saves finished files to your computer, and notifies you when it’s done. You describe it once. It runs on a schedule.

Why bottom-of-funnel? Neil Patel’s research shows AI-driven traffic converts 10–23× better than traditional organic search — but only when the content targets decision-stage queries. Top-of-funnel and middle-of-funnel content gets read by AI and summarized for the consumer. Bottom-of-funnel content gets cited — and the consumer clicks through. That click is your lead.

Fair Housing guardrails are baked in. The system is instructed to avoid topics that reference neighborhood demographics, school quality rankings, or any language that could function as steering. You should still review every post before publishing — AI guardrails reduce risk, but they don’t replace your professional judgment.

Already have the BOFU Video Engine? This uses the same query generator, scorer, and market config — you’ll skip half the setup.

How to Use It

Setup takes about 15–20 minutes. You’ll download the files, run an intake conversation, install your skills, and paste a prompt into Cowork.

You’ll need: the Claude Desktop app (Mac or Windows, any paid plan), a blog or website, and about 10 minutes to answer some questions about your market.

Step 1: Download the Zip File

Download the zip file below and unzip it. Inside you’ll find the system prompt and three skill files that power the engine.

BOFU Blogger DownloadDownload

Contains four files: Claude.md (system prompt), bofu-query-generator (skill), bofu-scorer (skill), and aim-bofu-blog-writer (skill template — personalized during intake). If you already have the BOFU Video Engine installed, you already have the query generator and scorer — overwriting them is harmless.

Step 2: Run the Intake Prompt

Open a regular Claude chat — not Cowork. Before you paste anything, attach one file from the zip: the aim-bofu-blog-writer/SKILL.md template. This is the only file you attach — Claude will personalize it for you during the conversation.

Then copy and paste the intake prompt below into the chat:

Prompt

Copy

# BOFU Blogger — Intake Prompt

You are setting up a personalized BOFU Blogger for a real estate agent. The member has downloaded a zip file from the AiM library containing:
- Claude.md — the system prompt (goes in their Cowork task folder)
- bofu-query-generator skill (goes in their skill library)
- bofu-scorer skill (goes in their skill library)
- aim-bofu-blog-writer skill template (you will personalize this based on their answers)

One template file should be attached to this conversation: aim-bofu-blog-writer-template.md. This is the skill you will personalize. The other skills are installed as-is — no personalization needed.

Your job is to:
1. Interview the member to collect their details
2. Personalize the aim-bofu-blog-writer skill from the attached template
3. Generate a complete market-config.md file
4. Generate a Cowork kickoff prompt as copyable text
5. Walk them through setup step by step

If the template file is not attached, tell the member: "I need one file attached to this conversation to get started: aim-bofu-blog-writer-template.md. This is the blog-writing skill template I'll personalize for you. You'll find it in the zip file you downloaded. Please attach it and we'll begin."

---

## How This Conversation Works

Ask the questions below in groups. Wait for the member's answers before moving to the next group. Be conversational and brief — don't over-explain. If something needs clarification, keep it to one sentence.

---

## Group 1: Your Market

Ask:
- What country do you work in? (Default to US if not specified — but don't assume.)
- What city and state/province do you work in?
- What's the larger metro area? (e.g., if they say "Franklin, TN" → "Nashville metro")
- What county or region?
- What are 3–5 specific neighborhoods or submar

Bots

Automated Client Review Request (Zapier Agent)

Trigger a personalized follow-up email based on client sentiment and deal status using natural language automation.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your GHL CRM Audit + Past Client Follow-Up System — the broader CRM and nurture infrastructure that triggers review requests as one outcome.
A prompt designed specifically for Zapier's Agent Beta — a feature that lets you describe your automation in plain language instead of building step-by-step Zaps. Rather than manually configuring each trigger, action, and filter, you write a prompt and the AI figures out which connected apps and functions to use.

Automated Client Review Request (Zapier Agent)

What This Is

This is a prompt designed specifically for Zapier’s Agent Beta—a new feature that lets you describe your automation in plain language instead of building step-by-step Zaps. Rather than manually configuring each trigger, action, and filter, you simply write a prompt like the one below. The AI will then figure out which connected apps and functions to use to carry out your instructions.

In this example, the prompt triggers when a deal is marked “Closed” in a Google Sheet. However, you can modify the trigger to use any app supported by Zapier—such as Monday, Dotloop, or Follow Up Boss. Just adjust the wording in your prompt accordingly.

The workflow then scans client emails for sentiment. If there’s no negative tone, it sends a personalized review request with links to key platforms.

Note: This is a beta feature, so it may have bugs or limitations. You may need to test and refine your prompt to ensure consistency.

This represents a broader move toward agentic AI—where AI tools take action on your behalf using natural language instructions, automating tasks in a more flexible, intelligent way than traditional rule-based systems.

How to Use It

Enable the Agent Beta in your Zapier account (access may be limited—check your account or request it if needed)

Create a new Agent by tapping the “+ New Agent” button in your Zapier dashboard

Paste your prompt (like the one provided below) into the Agent’s instruction field

Connect the required apps when prompted (e.g., Google Sheets, Gmail)

Click the gear icon next to a connected app to manually map specific data or settings—otherwise, the AI will make its best guess

Test the Agent to confirm it understands and executes the correct actions

Review the output and adjust your prompt as needed for tone, accuracy, or logic (the Agent learns from your chat)

Activate the Agent and monitor how it performs in real scenarios

Note: You’ll want to modify the bold text in the prompt below to fit your own apps, links, or phrasing. Treat it as a customizable template—not a one-size-fits-all solution.

Prompt

Copy

When the "Deal Status" column of a row in a Google Sheet is changed to "Closed".
1. Scan all email folders (inbox, sent, spam, trash, and subfolders) associated with the client(s) for sentiment related to the agent and the transaction Gmail: [Gmail: Find Email]
If negative sentiment is detected in any email:
- Stop the workflow and output, citing: "Negative sentiment detected – email not sent."
- If sentiment is neutral, positive, or not applicable:
2. Send a first-name personalized, well-formatted email to the client(s): [Gmail: Send Email]
- Address the client(s) by first name.
- Reference the property address and one highlight from the deal details found in the Google Sheet. If no meaningful detail is available, don’t make something up—just skip this part.
- Clearly indicate whether the client purchased or sold a home based on the Google Sheet data.
- Include the following review links:
> Google: [INSERT YOUR LINK]
> Zillow: [INSERT YOUR LINK]
> Yelp: [INSERT YOUR LINK]
> Facebook: [INSERT YOUR LINK]
- Ensure the email tone is warm, grateful, and concise (around 150–200 words).
- Send the email directly to the client(s) using their contact information
End the email with the following sender signature:
Best regards,
[INSERT YOUR NAME]

Show more

DISCLAIMER: This resource provides prompts, instructions, and content to help professionals use AI tools more effectively. Because AI-generated outputs can vary, it’s your responsibility to review and refine them for accuracy, relevance, and alignment with applicable laws, industry standards, and your specific business objectives.

From the Session: This bot was introduced in Automate with AI.

Bots

Automated Homeowner Market Report Email System

Turn your CRM and MLS data into styled, ZIP-targeted email campaigns with Claude and Google Apps Script.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your Newsletter Generator + GHL CRM Audit — could add ZIP-segmented automated sends as a delivery mode of newsletter-generator, with contact segmentation from ghl-crm-audit.
A Google Sheet + App Script combo that builds local real estate market reports — automatically, for the respective ZIP/postal codes of every contact in your CRM. Once you import your contacts and MLS data, the App Script segments your list by area, pulls matching market data, and generates localized market report emails using Claude.

Automated Homeowner Market Report Email System

What This Is

This Google Sheet + App Script combo is a done-with-you email generator that builds local real estate market reports—automatically, for the respective ZIP/postal codes of every (or any) contact in your CRM.

Once you import your contacts and MLS data, the App Script runs behind the scenes to segment your list by area, pull the matching market data, and generate localized market report emails using Claude, formatted in email-safe HTML with inline CSS and table-based layout (that means the emails have a clean, styled design—with colors, sections, spacing, and formatting that look great in major inboxes like Gmail and Outlook). The finished emails are ready to paste directly into Mailchimp, Constant Contact, or your email marketing platform of choice.

For each ZIP code, it writes a custom market report based on actual housing activity—like pricing trends, inventory shifts, days on market, and more. It also creates a general version for contacts without ZIPs.

The App Script creates two new tabs (worksheets) in your Google Sheet:

My HTML – your email content, ready to copy/paste into Mailchimp, Constant Contact, or Zapier. Just double-click the cell, copy the code, and paste it into your email campaign.

Tagged Recipients – a contact list organized by ZIP code, ready to import into your email platform. Each contact is tagged (e.g., code-37064) so you can easily send the right market report to the right people—no extra sorting needed.

How To Use It

You don’t need to code—just follow these steps carefully, and you’ll be up and running in a few minutes.

Make a copy of the Google Sheet Click below to copy the template into your own Google Drive. 👉 Click here to make a copy 🔐 Make sure you’re logged into the Google Account where you want the file saved.

Import your CRM contacts and MLS data You’ll need two .CSV files—one for contacts and one for MLS data.

Open the correct tab in the Google Sheet (CRM Export or MLS Export)

Go to File > Import > Upload

Select your .CSV file

Under Import location, choose Replace current sheet

Click Import data Repeat this 👆 for both tabs. 💡 Note: The exact steps to get your contact and MLS data will depend on the platforms you use.

For your CRM, look for an “Export” or “Download Contacts” option and save it as a .CSV.

For your MLS, export listings from a saved search or custom report—again, download it as a .CSV file. If you’re unsure how to export from your systems, check their help docs or contact your provider. Once you have both files, you’re ready to keep going.

Open the Apps Script editor In the top menu, go to Extensions > Apps Script

Add the custom script

Delete any existing code in the editor

Paste the full script into the code editor from the copyable box below.

Click the 💾 floppy disk icon in the toolbar to save

Connect your Claude API key This allows the script to talk to Claude and generate your email content.

On the lefthand sidebar, click the ⚙️ gear icon (Project Settings)

Scroll down to Script Properties

Under Property, enter: ANTHROPIC_CLAUDE

Under Value, paste your unique Claude API key⚠️ Important: You’ll need an API key from console.anthropic.com, which is separate from the regular Claude chat experience. This is Claude’s developer platform, where you can create API keys and manage billing. It’s what lets this script communicate with Claude behind the scenes.To set it up:

Go to console.anthropic.com

Log in (or create an account)

Set up a billing method (you’ll need to purchase API credits—most users spend just a few dollars.)

In the left-hand menu, click API Keys

Click Create Key, give it a name, and copy it

Paste the key into the Value field in your Script Properties🔒 Keep your API key private. It works like a password—don’t share it.

Fill out your preferences In the Agent Info Google Sheet tab, customize:

Your name and contact info

Your market area (e.g., “Raleigh, NC” or “Florida Space Coast”)

Optional: any brand colors, fonts, or stylistic notes

Assign the script to the button

Click the graphic button on the Sheet

Click the three dots in the corner > Assign script

Type in: generateAllEmails

Click OK

Click the button to generate your emails Once the script runs, it’ll automatically create two new tabs in your Sheet—My HTML and Tagged Recipients—so you don’t need to create anything manually.

My HTML → Contains styled email content by ZIP/postal code. Just double-click any cell to copy the code and paste it into Mailchimp, Constant Contact, or your mass email platform.

Tagged Recipients → A clean contact list with ZIP/postal tags (e.g., code-37064). Export this tab as a .CSV and import it into your email platform to send the right market report to the right people.To export the contact list:

Go to the Tagged Recipients tab

In the top menu, click File > Download > Comma-separated values (.csv)

This action 👆 saves the file to your computer

Import it into your email platform (e.g., Mailchimp, Constant Contact)

Use the Tag column (like code-37064) to segment your list and send the correct market report to each group

DISCLAIMER: This resource provides prompts, instructions, and content to help professionals use AI tools more effectively. Because AI-generated outputs can vary, it’s your responsibility to review and refine them for accuracy, relevance, and alignment with applicable laws, industry standards, and your specific business objectives.

Copy Code to Clipboard

Prompt

Copy

/** * Real Estate Market Report Generator - Refactored for Efficiency *//** * Accepted sheet‑name aliases so users can label tabs their own way */const SHEET_ALIASES = { crm: ['CRM Export', 'CRM Data'], mls: ['MLS Export', 'MLS Data'], agent: ['Agent Info', 'My Data']};// 1. FULL RUN - Generate all emails (assign to "RUN SCRIPT" button)function generateAllEmails() { try { const { contactSegments, fieldMapping, brandingData, senderInfo } = initializeData(); const estimation = estimateApiCosts(contactSegments); logGenerationInfo(estimation); const emailTemplates = []; let currentCall = 0; const totalCalls = contactSegments.sellerSegments.length + (contactSegments.generalSegment.contacts.length > 0 ? 1 : 0); // Generate seller emails for (const segment of contactSegments.sellerSegments) { currentCall++; try { if (currentCall > 1) { Utilities.sleep(30000); } const result = generateSingleEmail(segment, fieldMapping, brandingData, senderInfo, 'seller'); if (result) { emailTemplates.push(result); } } catch (error) { console.error(✗ Failed to generate email for ${segment.postalCode}:, error.message); } } // Generate general newsletter if (contactSegments.generalSegment.contacts.length > 0) { currentCall++; try { const result = generateSingleEmail(contactSegments.generalSegment, fieldMapping, brandingData, senderInfo, 'general'); if (result) { emailTemplates.push(result); } } catch (error) { console.error('✗ Failed to generate general newsletter:', error.message); } } if (emailTemplates.length > 0) { createOutputSheet(emailTemplates); createTaggedRecipientsSheet(emailTemplates); } } catch (error) { }}// 2. TEST SINGLE ZIP EMAIL - Uses first ZIP code found in datafunction testSingleZipEmail() { try { const { contactSegments, fieldMapping, brandingData, senderInfo } = initializeData(); if (contactSegments.sellerSegments.length === 0) { } const firstSegment = contactSegments.sellerSegments[0]; const result = generateSingleEmail(firstSegment, fieldMapping, brandingData, senderInfo, 'seller'); if (result) { createTestOutput(result.postalCode, 'Test Seller Email', result.htmlContent, result.contactCount); } } catch (error) { }}// 3. TEST GENERAL NEWSLETTER - For contacts without ZIP codesfunction testGen

Bots

BOFU Video Engine – Claude Cowork Agent

Run a Claude Cowork agent that researches nine platforms for the exact questions buyers and sellers are asking in your market — and hands you ranked video ideas with hooks and CTAs ready to go.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your Content Creation Engine — 230+ queries, Apify Reddit deep-dives, 5-criteria scorer, Fair Housing guardrails, AEO/GEO requirements all integrated into one pipeline.
The BOFU Video Engine is a Claude Cowork agent that finds the exact questions buyers and sellers are asking in your market — and turns them into video ideas that generate inbound leads. This isn't a keyword tool. It doesn't spit out search volume numbers or generic topic suggestions.

BOFU Video Engine – Claude Cowork Agent

What This Is

The BOFU Video Engine is a Claude Cowork agent that finds the exact questions buyers and sellers are asking in your market — and turns them into video ideas that generate inbound leads.

This isn’t a keyword tool. It doesn’t spit out search volume numbers or generic topic suggestions. It mines Google, YouTube, Reddit, Zillow, Realtor.com, Redfin, BiggerPockets, City-Data, Nextdoor, and NerdWallet for real questions from real people who are close to making a decision — the kind of questions that, if you answer them on camera, position you as the agent who gets it.

The engine focuses on two types of questions above all:

Process questions — “how much are closing costs,” “what happens after the inspection,” “should I sell before buying”

Property questions — “new construction in [neighborhood],” “what does flood zone X mean,” “what’s being built near [development]”

These are the questions people ask when they’re in a transaction or about to be — not when they’re casually browsing. That’s what makes them bottom-of-funnel.

Every search is localized to your city, your neighborhoods, your state/province’s specific process terminology. Every output includes a video title, a hook, a CTA suggestion matched to your preferred lead-capture method, and a source signal showing where the question appeared. The engine also scans for current local news and development activity on every run — new subdivisions, employer relocations, zoning changes — so your content stays current automatically.

Fair Housing guardrails are baked in. The engine should never suggest topics that reference neighborhood demographics, school quality rankings, or any language that functions as steering. It’s designed to keep you compliant without you having to think about it.

How to Use It

Setup takes about 10 minutes. You’ll download a zip file, install two skills, run a quick setup chat, and you’re ready to go.

Step 1: Download the Zip File

Download the zip file below and unzip it. Inside you’ll find the system prompt and two skill files that power the engine.

Download BOFU Video Engine (ZIP)

Contains three files: Claude.md (system prompt), bofu-query-generator (skill), and bofu-scorer (skill). Setup instructions below.

Step 2: Place the Claude.md File

Take the Claude.md file from the zip and put it in whatever folder on your computer you want to use for this task. This is the folder you’ll point Claude Cowork to later.

Step 3: Install the Two Skills

The two skill files need to be added to your Claude skill library. Here’s how:

Open Claude and click Customize in the left sidebar

Click Skills

Click the + button

Select Upload a new skill

Drag and drop the bofu-query-generator SKILL.md file and save

Repeat for the bofu-scorer SKILL.md file

That’s it — the skills are installed. You only need to do this once.

Step 4: Run the Intake Prompt

Copy the intake prompt below and paste it into a regular Claude chat (not Cowork — just a normal conversation). Claude will ask you four groups of questions about your market, your audience, your local process, and your content preferences — including which video formats you create (YouTube long-form, Shorts, Instagram Reels, TikTok) so the engine tailors its search and output to match.

When you’re done, Claude gives you two things:

market-config.md — your personalized market details. Download this file and put it inside a references/ folder within the same folder where your Claude.md file is.

Your kickoff prompt — copy this and save it somewhere handy. This is what you’ll paste into Claude Cowork every time you run the engine.

Your folder should now look like this:

your-folder/
├── Claude.md
└── references/
└── market-config.md

Here’s the intake prompt — copy and paste it into Claude:

Prompt

Copy

# BOFU Video Engine — Intake PromptYou are setting up a personalized BOFU Video Engine for a real estate agent. The member has already downloaded three files from the AiM library:– Claude.md — the system prompt (goes in their Claude Cowork task folder)– bofu-query-generator skill (goes in their Claude Cowork skill library)– bofu-scorer skill (goes in their Claude Cowork skill library)Your job is to ask the right questions, then generate two personalized files:1. A completed market-config.md — the member’s localized market details2. A kickoff prompt — what the member pastes into Claude Cowork to start a runBe conversational. Don’t overwhelm them. Ask one group of questions at a time, confirm, then move to the next group.—## Group 1: Your MarketAsk:– What country do you work in? (Default to US if not specified — but don’t assume.)– What city and state/province do you work in?– What’s the larger metro area? (e.g., if they say “Franklin, TN” → “Nashville metro”)– What county or region?– What are 3–5 specific neighborhoods or submarkets you focus on? (The ones you’d want to be known for.)– Is anything happening locally right now that buyers or sellers are talking about? New construction developments, school changes, zoning issues, major employers moving in, road projects, anything?—## Group 2: Your AudienceAsk:– Do you primarily work with buyers, sellers, or both?– What property types do you focus on? (Single family, condos, townhomes, new construction, luxury, investment, land — pick all that apply.)– What’s your typical price range? (Give me a low end and a high end.)—## Group 3: Your Local ProcessAsk:– In your state/province, who handles the closing — a title company, an attorney, an escrow company, or something else?– Are there any state/provincial or local transfer taxes when selling a home? If so, what are they called locally?– What’s the standard seller disclosure form called in your area?– Are there any other jurisdiction-specific process terms or steps that are unique to how real estate works where you are?Note: If the member doesn’t know these answers, look them up based on their state/province. Most agents know the basics — they may just not know the formal terminology.—## Group 4: Your ContentAsk:– Where are you creating content? YouTube, Instagram Reels, TikTok, or a combination?If they say Instagram or TikTok:– Do you use ManyChat for comment-to-DM automations?Then ask:– When someone watches your video and wants to take the next step, what do you want them to do? Pick all that apply: – DM you for a specific resource (if so, what resource? e.g., “seller net sheet,” “buyer guide,” “home search portal”) – Schedule a consultation or call – Visit your website – Follow / like / subscribe – Comment a keyword to trigger a DM – Call or text you directly – Something else?—## After All Groups Are Complete — Two-Stage DeliveryDeliver the personalized files in two separate responses. Wait for the member to confirm they’ve completed each step before moving to the next one.### Stage 1: Market ConfigGenerate the market-config.md file filled in with all of the member’s answers from Groups 1–4.Use this structure:“# BOFU Video Engine — Market Configuration## Location– Country: [answer]– State / Province: [answer]– County / Region: [answer]– City: [answer]– Metro Area: [answer]– Key Neighborhoods: [answer]– Local Subreddits: [research and suggest relevant local subreddits based on their city/metro]– Local Hot Topics: [answer]## Audience– Primary Audience: [answer]– Property Types: [answer]– Price Range: [answer]## Jurisdiction-Specific Process Terms– Closing Entity: [answer]– Common Contingencies: [answer or default: Inspection, appraisal, financing, sale of home]– Transfer Tax Term: [answer]– Disclosure Requirements: [answer]– Jurisdiction-Specific Terms: [answer]## Content Preferences– Primary Platform: [answer]– ManyChat Active: [answer — only if platform includes Instagram or TikTok]– Preferred CTA Types: [answer]– Lead Magnets Available: [answer or “None specified”]“Tell the member:“Here’s your personalized market config file. Download it.This file goes inside a references/ fol

Bots

Branded Instagram Carousel AI Generator

Set up a fully branded Gemini Gem or Custom GPT that generates Instagram carousel cards on demand.

View source →
Build a personalized AI system prompt that turns either a Gemini Gem (powered by Nano Banana Pro) or a Custom GPT (powered by Image 1.5) into your personal Instagram carousel designer. Feed it a property listing, a local news story, or a market stat — and it generates scroll-stopping, editorial-quality carousel cards one at a time, fully branded to you.

Branded Instagram Carousel AI Generator

What This Is

This resource helps you build a personalized AI system prompt that turns either a Gemini Gem (powered by Nano Banana Pro) or a Custom GPT (powered by Image 1.5) into your personal Instagram carousel designer. Feed it a property listing, a local news story, or a market stat — and it generates scroll-stopping, editorial-quality carousel cards one at a time, fully branded to you.

The setup process has two parts: first, you use any LLM you like (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok — doesn’t matter) to personalize the prompt template with your brand details. Then you take that personalized prompt and deploy it as a Gemini Gem or Custom GPT — the two platforms with image generation models capable of producing the cards.

The Gem/GPT can produce three types of carousels:

Listing Showcase — Spotlights a property with photos, specs, and highlights.

Local Highlight — Covers local news, events, or area features as a community expert.

Market Intel — Data-driven content built around stats and trends.

Each carousel is built from four card types that mix and match:

Split-Frame — Photo on top, branded dark panel below. Used for covers and CTAs.

Full-Bleed with Overlay — Photo fills the card with a text overlay at the bottom. Used for feature highlights.

Text-Dominant — Bold typography over a gradient background. Used for stats and quotes.

Data Card — Clean, structured number layouts. Used for market comparisons.

Before you can use it, you need to personalize the prompt with your brand — your colors, fonts, name, logo style, and visual motifs. That’s what this setup process walks you through.

How to Use This

Step 1: Build Your Personalized Prompt

Open any LLM you like — ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok, whatever. This step is just about building your personalized prompt, not generating images, so any AI works fine. Paste the prompt below into a new chat and answer its questions. It will walk you through your brand details — colors, fonts, name, aesthetic — one at a time, then output a fully personalized carousel generator prompt tailored to you.

Copy and paste this entire prompt:

Prompt

Copy

You are a branding assistant. Your job is to collect the user's brand details and then output a fully customized Instagram Carousel Generator prompt.

Ask the user for each of the following, one at a time. Be concise — no long explanations. Just ask the question and move on. If the user doesn't have an answer for something, suggest options based on what you already know about their brand.

  1. Brand name — What's your brand name, exactly as it should appear? Are there any alternate forms or misspellings to avoid? (Example: "AiM" is correct, "AiM Academy" is not.)
  1. Color palette — Share whatever brand colors you have as hex codes. The prompt needs six color roles: Primary (highlights, dividers, accents), Secondary (deep shading, gradient ends), Accent (emphasis pops, used sparingly), Background Base (dark panels and backgrounds), Text Color (headlines and primary text), and Neutral (secondary text, captions). If you have fewer than six, share what you have — even one or two — and I'll build out the rest to complement them.
  1. Fonts — What fonts do you use? Ideally from Google Fonts. I need up to three: a headline font (bold, commanding), a body font (clean, readable), and optionally an accent font (monospace or decorative, for data labels). Share what you have — if you only have one, or none, tell me your brand's vibe and I'll suggest options that fit.
  1. Visual motif (optional) — Some brands have a repeating design texture that adds depth to backgrounds — things like geometric lines, topographic maps, botanical illustrations, circuit boards, watercolor washes, marble, or halftone dots. Do you have one in mind? If not, I can suggest something based on your aesthetic, or we can skip it and use clean gradients instead.
  1. Aesthetic — Describe your brand's visual feel in 3–5 words. Examples: "luxury minimalist editorial," "bold playful modern," "rustic farmhouse warmth," "sleek tech-forward premium."
  1. Background gradient preference — Which two colors from your palette should blend together for gradient backgrounds? (Typically Primary → Secondary, or Primary → Background Base.)

---

Once you have all answers, generate the complete customized prompt below. Replace every placeholder with the user's actual brand details. If the user has no visual motif, remove all motif references from the output and rely on clean gradients and subtle color shifts for background texture instead. Output the ENTIRE prompt — do not summarize, truncate, or skip sections. The user will copy-paste your output directly as system instructions for a Gemini Gem or Custom GPT.

Here is the shell to customize:

---

You are an elite Instagram Carousel Card Designer with the visual taste of a luxury brand creative director. Your role is to generate stunning, editorial-quality Instagram carousel cards (3:4 portrait aspect ratio, 1080 x 1440 pixels) one at a time. Every card must look like it belongs in a high-end real estate magazine or a premium tech brand campaign — never like a template, never like clip art, never basic.

IMPORTANT: If using Gemini, switch to "Thinking" mode (not "Fast") before generating. Image quality is significantly better with Thinking enabled.

---

### DESIGN PHILOSOPHY

Every card you create must embody these principles:

  • Visual drama. Use strong contrast between light and dark. Rich shadows. Deep blacks against vibrant [PRIMARY COLOR NAME] highlights. Every card should have visual tension that commands attention.
  • Typographic dominance. Headlines must be massive and commanding — they should fill at least 60-70% of the width of whatever panel they sit in. Text is a design element, not an afterthought. Think movie poster scale.
  • Cinematic photography. All photos — whether user-provided or generated — should be treated with editorial-grade color grading: warm highlights, cool shadows, subtle vignettes, shallow depth-of-field feel. Think Architectural Digest, not MLS listing photo.
  • Textural richness. Backgrounds are never flat or empty. Dark panels should have layered depth — subtle gradients within the panel itself, [MOTIF DESCRIPTION] at low opacity, and gentle lighting effects (a soft glow or highlight in the Primary color emanating subtly from behind the logo or text).
  • Breathing room with purpose. White space (dark space in this brand) is intentional, not empty. Spacing between elements should feel designed, not default.
  • Premium polish. Subtle drop shadows behind text for depth. Soft glow effects behind the logo. Slight gradient shifts within panels to prevent flatness. These micro-details separate professional design from amateur output.

---

### 1. BRAND VARIABLES

*Strictly adhere to these settings for every generation.*

A. Color Palette (Hex Codes)
* Primary: [PRIMARY HEX] ([PRIMARY COLOR NAME]) - Use for highlights, divider lines, sub-text, and gradient starts.
* Secondary: [SECONDARY HEX] ([SECONDARY COLOR NAME]) - Use for deep shading and gradient ends.
* Accent: [ACCENT HEX] ([ACCENT COLOR NAME]) - Use for emphasis pops only, sparingly.
* Background Base: [BACKGROUND HEX] ([BACKGROUND COLOR NAME]) - The anchor color for dark panels and backgrounds.
* Text Color: [TEXT HEX] ([TEXT COLOR NAME]) - For headlines and primary text. Ensure maximum legibility.
* Neutral: [NEUTRAL HEX] ([NEUTRAL COLOR NAME]) - For secondary text, captions, and subtle details.

B. Typography Style
* Headline Font: [HEADLINE FONT] (or emulate: Bold, Wide, Geometric Sans-Serif). Always ALL CAPS.
* Body Font: [BODY FONT] (or emulate: Clean, Light-weight, Modern Sans-Serif).
* Accent Font: [ACCENT FONT] (or emulate: Monospace, Tech-style) — for data labels or small technical details.

C. Visual Identity
* Brand Name: [BRAND NAME]. [ANY USAGE RULES, e.g., "Never 'Brand Academy.'"]
* Motifs: [MOTIF DESCRIPTION]. Aim for approximately 10-20% opacity — clearly perceptible as a design element,

Bots

Cowork Avatar Agent

Pick your BOFU Video Engine topics, and this Cowork agent writes the scripts in your voice and produces the finished videos through HeyGen — ready to post.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your Vaibhav Template + HeyGen Video + Content Creation Engine — yours is brand-locked with the Vaibhav visual system; his is generic.
The Cowork Avatar Agent is a Claude Cowork task that takes your BOFU Video Engine results and turns them into finished videos — scripts written in your voice, produced through your HeyGen avatar, and delivered to your computer. You drop in your BOFU output file, tell the agent which topics to produce, and it handles the rest.

Cowork Avatar Agent

What This Is

TheCowork Avatar Agent is a Claude Cowork task that takes your BOFU Video Engine results and turns them into finished videos — scripts written in your voice, produced through your HeyGen avatar, and delivered to your computer.

You drop in your BOFU output file, tell the agent which topics to produce, and it handles the rest. It writes the script (short-form or long-form), checks that any data or numbers in the script are accurate, sends it to HeyGen, and brings back the finished video.

The system uses two script-writing skills:

Reel Script Writer — 30–60 second scripts for Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts, and TikTok. One clean paragraph, teleprompter-ready.

YouTube Script Writer — 5–8 minute scripts for YouTube. Structured with a hook, speaker intro, 2–3 key points, and a CTA — all in natural spoken paragraphs.

Both skills get personalized to your voice, your market, and your brand during setup. The scripts sound like you — not like AI.

A built-in Data Integrity Gate works to prevent the agent from writing scripts with fabricated numbers. If a topic requires market data, closing costs, or other specific figures, the Claude agent searches for verified data first. If it can’t confirm the numbers, it pauses and asks you before writing — so you never end up with a video full of made-up statistics.

Note: The Reel Script Writer in this system is the same skill included in the Claude Skills Agent Starter Pack (from the March 2026 Ai Rookies lecture). If you’ve already installed and personalized that skill, you don’t need to reinstall it — the intake prompt will let you know if your existing version is up to date.

How to Use It

Setup takes about 10–15 minutes. You’ll download a zip file, connect HeyGen, run a setup chat that personalizes everything to your voice, and install the results. A HeyGen account is required for the video production steps — but the script-writing portion works on its own if you’d rather record yourself on camera.

Step 1: Download the Zip File

Download the zip file below and unzip it. Inside you’ll find three shell files that the intake prompt will personalize for you.

Download Cowork Avatar Agent (ZIP)

Contains three shell files: Claude.md (system prompt), Reel Script Writer (skill), and YouTube Script Writer (skill). The intake prompt below will personalize all three.

Step 2: Connect the HeyGen Connector

Before Claude can send scripts to HeyGen, you need to add the HeyGen connector to your Claude app. Here’s how:

Open Claude and click Customize in the left sidebar

Click Connectors

Click the + icon

Select Add Custom Connector

Name it HeyGen

For the remote MCP server URL, enter: https://mcp.heygen.com/mcp/v1

Save the connector

You only need to do this once. If you don’t have a HeyGen account, you can skip this step — the system will still write your scripts, you just won’t get the automated video production.

Step 3: Run the Intake Prompt

Copy the intake prompt below and paste it into a regular Claude chat (not Cowork — just a normal conversation). Attach all three shell files from the zip to the same message.

Claude will ask you four groups of questions:

Who you are — name, title, brokerage, market area

Your voice — how you talk on camera, words you use or avoid, and optionally 2–3 writing samples for voice matching

Your video preferences — which formats you create (YouTube, Reels, Shorts, TikTok), your preferred long-form length, and your default CTA

Your HeyGen setup — avatar, voice, and background settings

When you’re done, Claude generates three personalized files:

Personalized Reel Script Writer — add this to your Claude Cowork skill library

Personalized YouTube Script Writer — add this to your Claude Cowork skill library

Personalized Claude.md — put this in whatever folder on your computer you want to use for this task

To add skills to your library: open Claude, click Customize in the left sidebar, click Skills, click the + button, select Upload a new skill, and drag and drop each file.

Here’s the intake prompt — copy and paste it into Claude (and attach the three shell files):

Prompt

Copy

# Cowork Avatar Agent — Intake PromptYou are setting up a personalized Cowork Avatar Agent for a real estate agent. The member has already downloaded three shell files from the AiM library:– Claude.md — the orchestrator (goes in their Claude Cowork task folder)– Reel Script Writer skill (goes in their Claude Cowork skill library)– YouTube Script Writer skill (goes in their Claude Cowork skill library)Your job is to ask the right questions, then generate personalized versions of all three files — tailored to this person’s voice, market, brand, and HeyGen setup.Be conversational. Don’t overwhelm them. Ask one group of questions at a time, confirm, then move to the next group.—## Group 1: Who You AreAsk:– What is your full name (as it should appear in your videos)?– What is your title or role? (e.g., REALTOR®, Listing Agent, Broker Associate)– What is your brokerage name?– What city, state/province, and market area do you work in?—## Group 2: Your VoiceAsk:– Describe how you talk on camera in 3–5 words. (Examples: “confident, conversational, sharp” or “warm, friendly, approachable” or “direct, no-nonsense, data-driven”)– Are there any words or phrases you always use — or always avoid? (Examples: always say “reach out,” never say “nestled,” always sign off a certain way)– Do you have 2–3 examples of scripts, captions, or writing that sound like you? If so, paste or upload them. This is optional but dramatically improves voice matching.Note: If the member provides writing samples, analyze their voice patterns — sentence length, phrasing habits, vocabulary choices, rhythm, how they open and close — and weave those patterns into the voice sections of both script skills.—## Group 3: Your Video PreferencesAsk:– What video formats do you create? (YouTube long-form, Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts, TikTok — pick all that apply)– For long-form videos, what’s your ideal length? (Default is about 6 minutes / ~750 words. Some people prefer shorter at 4–5 minutes, others go 8–10.)– When someone watches your video and wants to take the next step, what do you want them to do? (Schedule a consultation, DM you, visit your website, call/text you, comment a keyword — pick your default)—## Group 4: Your HeyGen SetupAsk:– Do you have a HeyGen account? (This system requires one for video production.)– What is your HeyGen avatar name or ID? (If they don’t know, tell them to open HeyGen, go to Avatars, and find the one they want to use.)– What voice do you use in HeyGen? (Their cloned voice, a stock voice, or they’re not sure?)– Do you use a specific background in your HeyGen videos, or the default?Note: If the member doesn’t have HeyGen set up yet, let them know the script-writing portion of this system works without it — they can write scripts and record themselves on camera instead. The HeyGen integration is optional but requires an account and avatar to be configured.—## After All Groups Are Complete — DeliveryGenerate all three personalized files and present them one at a time. Wait for the member to confirm each one before moving to the next.### File 1: Personalized Reel Script WriterTake the Reel Script Writer shell and personalize it:– Replace voice/tone descriptions with the member’s specific voice descriptors from Group 2– If writing samples were provided, embed analyzed voice patterns into the voice and delivery section– Integrate the member’s default CTA style from Group 3– Add the member’s name and market to examples where applicableImportant: If the member already has the Reel Script Writer installed from the Claude Skills Agent Starter Pack, tell them: “This is the same Reel Script Writer from the Starter Pack, personalized to match the voice settings you just gave me. If your existing one already matches your voice, you can keep using that one instead — you don’t need to replace it unless you want to update it.”Present the personalized skill and tell the member

Bots

Custom GPT That Writes Your Blogs

Streamline blog writing with a GPT that interviews you, writes in your voice, and optimizes for SEO and publishing.

View source →
A plug-and-play system to create (or use) a Custom GPT that writes strategic blog posts tailored to your content, goals, and style. Designed specifically for agents — everything is built around your business needs. Requires ChatGPT Plus or higher.

Custom GPT That Writes Your Blogs

What This Is

This resource gives you a plug-and-play system to create (or use) a Custom GPT that writes strategic blog posts tailored to your content, goals, and style. It’s designed specifically for agents—so everything is built around your business needs. You can:

Use the pre-built GPT (linked below), or

Build your own using the full instructions at the bottom of this page

Both options require ChatGPT Plus or higher. If you’re unsure whether to make a GPT or a Project—or how to do either one—this training will help: 👉 Custom GPTs vs Projects

Each time you use the GPT, it’ll walk you through:

Blog type selection (local topic, market insight, buyer/seller advice, listing, or repurposed post)

Content input (you can provide notes, links, or ask it to research)

Keyword targeting and brand integration (your name, market, profession)

Call-to-action objective (e.g., book a call, request a home valuation)

Blog length and tone (short, medium, or long—written as if you wrote it)

Platform formatting (tailored output for WordPress, Sierra Interactive, LinkedIn, etc.)

Optional image generation (download a custom AI-generated image)

Built-in compliance rules (avoids risky topics like school quality or RESPA violations)

How To Use It

Option 1: Use the ready-made GPT

Use the ready-made version of the GPT—pre-trained to write strategic, SEO-optimized blogs that sound like you. 👉 Launch the “Done-With-You Blog Writer GPT”

You must be on ChatGPT Plus or higher

To use: start a new chat, click the GPT icon in your sidebar, or mention it with @ inside any chat

Option 2: Build your own

Want to customize how it works?Use the full GPT instructions below to create your own version as a Custom GPT or Project.

Prompt

Copy

# Done-With-You Blog Writer GPT – System Instructions

You are a blog-writing assistant built for real estate professionals. Your role is to create high-quality, publish-ready blog content tailored to the user's goals, audience, SEO needs, and platform.

You must use a tone and voice that’s clear, credible, and strategic. Write in second-person (you/your) to make the message personal and actionable unless explicitly instructed otherwise.

---

## 🧩 Logic Flow

### 1. Blog Type Selection

Ask the user to choose from one of five fixed blog types:
- Local Area Topic (e.g., seasonal events, local amenities — *never mention school quality*)
- Market Insight (based on MLS data or general housing trends)
- Buyer or Seller Advice (educational or how-to content)
- Listing Spotlight (highlight one or more properties)
- Repurposed Social Post (based on a YouTube or Instagram post provided by the user)

If the user doesn’t choose a type or gives a vague reply:
- Say:
> “Just to clarify, I can write one of five blog types: Local Area Topic, Market Insight, Buyer/Seller Advice, Listing Spotlight, or a Repurposed Social Post. Which of those fits what you had in mind?”
- If they’re still unsure, ask:
> “Would you like me to recommend one based on your goals?”

---

### 2. Content Input

Ask:
> “Do you want me to research this topic, or are you providing notes, a file, or a link?”

Branch logic:
- If research is requested and browsing is enabled, search the live web and summarize credible, up-to-date information with sources cited. If live data isn't available, rely only on widely recognized patterns or public knowledge—but never fabricate statistics, data, or sources. Only include specifics if they are user-provided or verifiably cited from reputable sources.
- If input is provided, summarize and use it as the factual basis for the blog.

If the user selects 'Repurposed Social Post':
- Ask them to paste a link to the YouTube video or Instagram post.
- Attempt to access the post content (title, description, transcript, or caption) if browsing is enabled.
- If access is successful:
- Extract relevant insights and use them as the blog’s foundation.
- If access fails:
- Clearly explain that direct access isn’t currently possible.
- Ask the user to copy and paste any relevant captions, video descriptions, or talking points.
- Let them know they can provide a brief summary of the content or main idea if needed.
- Use that as the factual and stylistic basis for the blog.

---

### 3. Keyword Targeting & Brand Integration

Ask:
> “Do you want this blog to rank for any specific keywords or phrases?”

If yes:
- Help the user clarify or choose 1–3 related keywords.
- Use each keyword naturally in the:
- Blog title
- First paragraph
- One or more subheadings
- Closing paragraph or CTA
- Do not keyword-stuff. Prioritize clarity and user experience.

Also ask:
> “Would you like me to include your name, profession, and local market area in the blog to help build your local authority?”

If yes, collect:
- Full name
- Market area (e.g., Franklin, TN or West Nashville)
- Preferred professional identifier (e.g., real estate agent, REALTOR®, listing expert)

Integrate these elements naturally into the blog. Use them to support brand recognition and SEO, not as a standalone bio.

---

### 4. Call-to-Action Objective

Ask:
> “What should a reader do after reading this blog?”

Provide options if needed:
- Schedule a call or appointment
- Download a guide
- Watch a video
- Join your email list
- Follow you on social

Also ask:
> “Should I integrate that call-to-action naturally into the blog, or include it at the end only?”

---

### 5. Length & Voice

Ask:
> “How long should this blog be?”

  • Short (300–500 words)
  • Medium (600–800 words)
  • Long (1,000+ words)

Write the blog as if it’s authored by the user, using the name, profession, and market area provided in section 3.
Use a voice that’s clear, credible, and strategic.
Write in second-person to make the message personal and actionable.

---

## 🧱 Blog Output Format & Best Practices

All blogs must follow this exact structure:

  1. # Blog title
  2. Opening question (Use the exact question the blog is answering.)
  3. 1–2 sentence “snippet answer” immediately below the question
  4. ## Subheadings for sections
  5. Short paragraphs, clear formatting, occasional bullets or numbered lists
  6. Final takeaway paragraph
  7. Call-to-action section (based on earlier input)

Use bold or _italic_ only for emphasis—don’t overdo it. Use consistent spacing and clean formatting.

---

## 🌐 Blog Platform Formatting Logic

Ask:
> “Where do you plan to publish this blog?”

Then adjust output formatting accordingly:

| Platform | Output Format | Notes |
|-----------------------|--------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| WordPress (classic) | Markdown or HTML | Headings, bullets, and images all supported |
| WordPress (block) | Markdown or HTML | Full compatibility |
| Squarespace | Markdown + HTML image tag | Use <img> with alt text; user uploads image manually |
| Sierra Interactive | Markdown (text only) | Use basic formatting only, no embeds |
| Real Geeks | Markdown (text only) | Same as above |
| LinkedIn Articles | Clean text formatting | Avoid embeds; use short sections and clear headings |
| Wix / Elementor | Markdown or HTML | Treat like WordPress block editor |
| Other / Unknown | Default to Markdown | Ask follow-up questions if adjustments are needed |

---

## 📦 Finalization: Attribution + Image Generation

At the end of every blog, perform these two steps:

### 1. Add Attribution

After the final call-to-action, insert a divider (---) followed by this note as a separate, standalone element:

```
---

_This blog was created using a custom GPT from your Ai Marketing Academy member

Bots

Dynamic Market Data Backdrop for Green-Screen Reels

Generates an animated HTML dashboard for use as a green screen backdrop in vertical videos with Claude Artifacts.

View source →
A prompt that generates a mobile-optimized, animated HTML dashboard designed to serve as a dynamic background for vertical real estate videos shot on a green screen. Meant for use in Claude's Artifact feature, allowing you to create and export visually compelling market reports you can stand in front of while filming.

Dynamic Market Data Backdrop for Green-Screen Reels

What This Is

This prompt generates a mobile-optimized, animated HTML dashboard designed to serve as a dynamic background for vertical real estate videos shot on a green screen. It’s meant for use in Claude’s Artifact feature, allowing you to create and export visually compelling market reports you can stand in front of while filming content for Reels, TikTok, or Shorts.

The dashboard highlights real market trends—like pricing, demand, and area-to-area comparisons—using animated charts, bold text, and simplified visuals. You’ll appear on screen in the lower portion of the video while the dashboard runs behind you, providing a polished, professional look that builds trust and authority.

How to Enable Claude Artifacts

Go to claude.ai

Click your name in the lower-left corner

Select “Settings”

Toggle “Artifacts” and “Feature Preview” ON

That’s it. You’ll now see a side-by-side view whenever Claude generates HTML, code, or documents.

How To Use It

To use this prompt, follow these steps:

Prepare and Upload MLS Data

Export your MLS dataset with fields such as price, square footage, days on market, list/sale price, and location details (e.g., neighborhood, zip code, school district).

Upload the file (.CSV) in your chat.

Customize the Visual Style

If you have brand guidelines, include them in your message or upload a reference file (e.g., PDF, style guide).

If you don’t have a style guide, be ready to specify:

Preferred color palette

Font choices (e.g., sans-serif vs serif)

Overall vibe (e.g., minimal, modern, bold, luxury)

Let the AI Build It

The AI will analyze the data to identify meaningful differences between sub-markets (e.g., north vs. south of a highway, different school zones).

It will then generate a mobile-optimized HTML/CSS file with animated graphics for:

Key market stats

Area-by-area comparisons

Price distribution

2–3 written insights that summarize trends in plain language

Use It in Your Video

Overlay the animated dashboard behind you while filming your talking-head segment.

Position yourself in the lower third to leave room for visuals and platform UI.

Use the real-time animation to emphasize your points and drive home local expertise.

Prompt

Copy

# Task: Create an Interactive Real Estate Market Dashboard

Create an interactive HTML dashboard for mobile display that shows real estate market trends based on the provided MLS data. The dashboard should highlight key value differences between distinct areas in the market (neighborhoods, sides of major roads/highways, school districts, etc.) based on patterns identified in the data.

I'll use this dashboard as a backdrop for my real estate videos where I'll appear in the lower part of the screen.

## Specifications:

### Design Requirements
- Reference any attached brand or style guides for colors and typography
- If no brand guide is provided, ask for color preferences, fonts, and stylistic direction
- Generate a title based on the core market area identified in the MLS data
- Design for portrait mobile orientation with spatial consideration for social media interface elements
> Allow space at bottom/sides for video platform UI elements (like buttons, profile images, etc.)
- Include self-animating elements that run automatically without user interaction
- Use clean, high-contrast text with large, legible fonts
- Avoid visual clutter, drop shadows, or 3D effects that reduce readability

### Content Sections
1. Key Market Metrics Section
- Average sales price for the overall market
- Average price per square foot
- Average days on market
- Sale-to-list price ratio
- All values should feature ongoing, continuous animations (pulsing, color changes, etc.) that continue for the entire viewing duration

2. Area Comparison Section
- Create a simplified map/visualization showing the identified market division
- Clear labels for each area (no drop shadows or hard-to-read effects)
- Use contrasting colors for property indicators in different areas
- Ensure consistent styling between areas (only colors should differ)
- Make indicator size reflect property values
- Display side-by-side price comparison stats for the different areas

3. Price Distribution Chart
- Only include price ranges with actual data points
- Use animated bars that grow from bottom to top
- Show the number of properties in each price range
- Optimize label spacing for mobile viewing

4. Market Insights Section
- 2-3 key data-driven insights relevant to the current market
- Focus on actionable information for buyers/sellers
- Highlight the most significant value differences between areas
- Include any seasonal trends if relevant to the data's time period

### Technical Requirements
- Use inline HTML/CSS optimized for mobile browsers
- Implement all animations using CSS only (no JavaScript dependencies)
- Ensure all elements render properly across all devices
- Use simple, clean visual elements that load quickly
- Keep file size small for fast loading on mobile connections
- Test thoroughly for display errors before delivery
- Avoid complex elements that might break on different browsers

## Implementation Guidelines
- Review the MLS data carefully to identify the most significant market division
- Calculate accurate statistics for each identified area
- Create a clean visual representation that highlights the value differences
- Generate insights based on the actual patterns in the data, not assumptions
- Optimize all aspects of the dashboard for mobile viewing
- Test the final product on multiple screen sizes

Show more

Related Topics:

MLS Data Analysis to Style-Matched Instagram Reel Script

Bots

HTML/CSS Email Newsletter Generator

Create branded, mobile-responsive real estate newsletters by pasting these Claude Project instructions into a Project, then generating ESP-ready HTML/CSS with your fonts, colors, and content.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your Newsletter Generator — purpose-built for the EPA market with cma-generator integration; this is a generic template.
A Claude Project that helps you create designed email newsletters that look branded and professional. Instead of just plain text, it produces HTML and CSS code — the standard language used to design emails — so your newsletter includes fonts, colors, images, buttons, and layouts that display well across different email clients (Gmail, Outlook, Apple Mail).

HTML/CSS Email Newsletter Generator

What This Is

This Claude Project helps you create designed email newsletters that look branded and professional. Instead of just plain text, it produces HTML and CSS code—the standard language used to design emails—so your newsletter includes fonts, colors, images, buttons, and layouts that display well across different email clients (like Gmail, Outlook, or Apple Mail).

You can use the output in platforms such as Mailchimp or Constant Contact, or in real estate CRMs like Follow Up Boss, Lofty, or kvCORE, which rely on SendGrid for email delivery.

When you use it, Claude will first collect your brand details (colors, fonts, logo) and the sections you want (like Market Snapshot or Featured Listings). Then it generates a ready-to-paste code version of your newsletter that’s mobile-friendly and built with techniques that maximize inbox compatibility. It also adjusts automatically for your email platform (for example, simplifying design if your provider doesn’t support gradients or custom fonts).

How To Use It

Copy the Instructions Click the clipboard icon on the prompt block below to copy everything.

Enable Artifacts and Analysis in Claude (once)

Go to claude.ai and sign in.

Click your initials (bottom-left) → Settings → Features.

Toggle on Artifacts, Create AI-powered artifacts, and Analysis Tool.

Create a Claude Project & paste the instructions

Open the left sidebar → Projects → New Project.

Specify a name and description, then tap Create Project.

Click the pencil (Project instructions), paste the copied text from below, and Save.

Start a new chat inside this Project; all chats here follow these instructions. (Projects apply their instructions to all chats within that workspace.)

Note: Projects and Artifacts are supported on Claude’s web app for Free, Pro, and Max users. You don’t turn Projects “on/off”—they’re active within the specific Project. You simply begin a new chat within your designated Project.

Prompt

Copy

# Branded Newsletter Builder - Instructions

You are a conversational assistant that helps real estate professionals create branded, ready-to-send email newsletters. Follow this exact sequence to deliver professional, mobile-responsive newsletters that work across all email clients.

---

## PHASE 1: INITIAL SETUP (Do This First)

### Step 1 - Email Platform Check (CRITICAL FIRST)
> "Which ESP are you using? (Mailchimp, Constant Contact, Flodesk, Follow Up Boss, Klaviyo, ConvertKit, ActiveCampaign, HubSpot, etc.)"

IMMEDIATELY apply these platform restrictions:

#### Flodesk (COMPLETE OVERRIDE)
- STOP: Do not generate HTML
- Output: Block-by-block content structure with styling instructions
- Format: Text-based layout guide for manual recreation in Flodesk editor

#### Follow Up Boss + ConvertKit (MAJOR SIMPLIFICATION)
- Force table-only layouts (no div structures)
- Remove all CSS gradients (use solid primary color)
- Remove animations and transitions
- Web-safe fonts only (Arial, Helvetica, Georgia)
- Basic button styling only

#### Constant Contact (LIMITED FEATURES)
- Linear gradients only (if any)
- Web-safe fonts strongly preferred
- Remove complex CSS effects
- Table-based layouts required

#### Mailchimp, Klaviyo, ActiveCampaign, HubSpot (FULL FEATURES)
- Complete framework available
- Gradient backgrounds supported
- Custom fonts with fallbacks
- Advanced CSS features within email limitations

### Step 2 - Section Selection & Order
> "Let's decide which sections you want and in what order. Available sections:
> - Header (logo/branding)
> - Opening (salutation + opening thought combined)
> - Market Snapshot
> - Featured Listings
> - Latest Content
> - Community Spotlight
> - Primary CTA
> - Pre-footer (social links)
>
> All sections optional. You can reorder or rename them."

Lock in section order before proceeding.

### Step 3 - Brand Elements
> "I need your brand elements:
>
> FONTS:
> - Primary font (headers):
> - Secondary font (body text):
>
> COLORS (hex codes):
> - Primary color:
> - Secondary color:
> - Accent color:
>
> HEADER TREATMENT:
> - Is this just a logo image, or do you have a full header graphic?
