This system turns Claude Cowork into your video editor. Here’s what’s happening under the hood: Claude connects to Descript — a video editing app that lets you edit video by editing text — and talks directly to Descript’s built-in AI editor called Underlord. Underlord is a chatbot inside Descript that has full editing permissions — it can cut, rearrange, add captions, reframe, and export your video. What this system does is put Claude in charge of that conversation. Two AI bots, chatting edits back and forth, while you stay in the loop on the decisions that matter.

You paste a Descript project link into Cowork, answer a few quick questions, and Claude handles the rest:

Rough-cuts the video — finds the best takes, removes the bad ones

Verifies every line against your script so nothing important gets cut

Cleans up filler words and dead air

Adds branded captions with active word highlighting

Applies zoom cuts and intro/outro fades

Reframes for vertical or horizontal

Exports an MP4 to your Downloads folder

It gets you 85–90% of the way to a finished edit. Not perfect — you still review the framing, eyeball the captions, and make final tweaks in Descript. But it compresses what used to be 30–60 minutes of manual editing into a few minutes of AI-assisted work. Claude asks you to verify visuals before exporting, but the tedious part — finding best takes, removing bad ones, syncing captions — is automated.

This is built for talking head videos — one speaker, one camera, scripted content recorded off a teleprompter. It could work for multi-speaker recordings like interviews too, but it’s optimized for the solo talking head workflow.

If you’ve used the Video Rough-Cut Editor Prompt, you already know the concept — paste a prompt into Underlord, get a rough cut back. This workspace takes it further. Instead of stopping at the rough cut, Claude runs the entire pipeline: rough cut, script verification, cleanup, captions, zoom cuts, reframe, and export. And instead of you driving the conversation with Underlord, Claude handles that for you.

Tip: For the best results, open Descript’s settings and set Underlord’s model to Claude Opus 4.6. This gives Underlord the strongest reasoning for complex editing decisions like selecting the best take from multiple options.

What You’ll Need

Claude Pro or Team plan with the Claude desktop app — Cowork is a mode inside the desktop app that lets Claude work on files and folders on your computer. It only runs onthe desktop app (not the website or mobile app) because it needs access to your local machine. You’ll point it at a folder, and Claude can read and work with anything inside it.

Descript account with AI credits — any paid plan works (Hobbyist, Creator, or Business). The free plan includes Underlord access too, but AI credits are limited. Each edit uses roughly 11–14 credits, so check your plan’s monthly allowance and plan accordingly.

How to Use It

Setup takes about 10 minutes. After that, every edit starts with a single link.

Step 1: Connect Descript to Claude

This step connects Claude to Descript so the two can talk to each other. You only need to do this once.

Open the Claude desktop app.

In the left sidebar, click Customize.

Click Connectors.

Click the + button to add a connector. Descript isn’t in the directory yet, so choose Add Custom Connector.

For the name, type Descript. For the Remote MCP Server URL, paste: https://api.descript.com/v2/mcp

Click Add, then click Connect. You’ll be prompted to sign into Descript and click “Allow” to give Claude permission to edit on your behalf.

Once connected, you should see Descript listed under your connectors. That’s it — Claude can now send editing instructions to Descript’s Underlord.

Step 2: Install the Two Skills

Skills are instruction files that teach Claude how to do specific jobs. This system uses two of them — one that contains the editing pipeline and one that teaches Claude how to talk to Underlord. They’re bundled together in a single download.

Download Skills (ZIP)

Contains two files: talking-head-editor and underlord-capabilities. Install each one separately — instructions below.

Unzip the download. You’ll see two markdown files. Install each one:

Open the Claude desktop app.

In the left sidebar, click Customize.

Click Skills.

Click the + button, then choose Create a Skill.

Click Upload a Skill, then drag and drop the markdown file.

Click Save.

Do this for both files. Once saved, these skills are permanent — they’ll be there every time you use Claude.

Step 3: Generate Your Personal Settings File

The connector tells Claude how to reach Descript. The skills tell Claude what to do. This step tells Claude who you are — your brand colors, fonts, and editing preferences so every video comes out looking like yours.

You’re going to generate a file called CLAUDE.md. Think of it like a settings file — Claude reads it at the start of every editing session to know your brand kit, your default recording style, your pacing preference, and your preferred output format. You create it once, and it applies to every video you edit.

Copy the intake prompt below and paste it into a new Claude chat (not Cowork — just a regular chat). Claude will ask you five quick questions, then generate your personalized CLAUDE.md file. The whole thing takes about two minutes.

Prompt

Copy

I need you to help me set up a Cowork workspace for automated talking head video editing. You’re going to ask me a few quick questions, then generate a personalized CLAUDE.md file I’ll put in my workspace.Here’s the template with placeholders. Your job is to ask me the questions below, then output the completed file with my answers filled in.TEMPLATE:# Talking Head Video Editor — Cowork WorkspaceYou are a talking head video editor. This workspace edits talking head videos in Descript using the Underlord AI agent via the Descript MCP connector.—## TriggerWhen the user provides a Descript project link (e.g., https://web.descript.com/…), start the talking head editing workflow using the talking-head-editor skill.Before starting the edit, ask these quick questions (skip any the user already answered):1. “Was this recorded {{DEFAULT_RECORDING_STYLE}}? Or did you do it differently this time?”2. “Do you have the script you were reading? Pasting it here helps me verify the edit doesn’t accidentally cut real content.”3. “Horizontal (16:9), vertical (9:16), or both?” (Skip if the user’s default covers it and they didn’t say otherwise.)Then run the full pipeline from the talking-head-editor skill.If the user comes back with another video in the same session, repeat the questions above. All brand preferences carry over automatically.—## Brand Kit### Colors| Name | Hex | Usage ||——|—–|——-|| Primary | {{PRIMARY_COLOR}} | Active word highlight in captions, accent elements || Secondary | {{SECONDARY_COLOR}} | Caption background, secondary accents || Tertiary | {{TERTIARY_COLOR}} | Call-to-action emphasis (optional) || Text Dark | {{TEXT_DARK_COLOR}} | Dark text, dark backgrounds || Text Light | {{TEXT_LIGHT_COLOR}} | Light text, caption text on dark backgrounds |### Fonts| Font | Usage ||——|——-|| {{FONT_PRIMARY}} | Headings, captions, on-screen text || {{FONT_SECONDARY}} | Body text, secondary text, fallback |### Caption Style– Font: {{FONT_PRIMARY}} Bold– Text color: {{TEXT_LIGHT_COLOR}}– Active word highlight: {{PRIMARY_COLOR}}– Background: Semi-transparent {{TEXT_DARK_COLOR}} at 80% opacity, rounded corners– Position: Bottom center for horizontal; lower third for vertical (roughly 70% from top — above the IG/YT Shorts/TikTok UI zone but low enough to look natural)– Animation: Word-by-word highlight (active word changes color as spoken)—## Editing Preferences– Default recording style: {{DEFAULT_RECORDING_STYLE}}– Dead air threshold: {{DEAD_AIR_THRESHOLD}

Bots

Title Influencer Instagram Reel Script Generator

You know you should be posting more short-form video. You know what it would do for your visibility with agents. The thing holding you back…

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your Vaibhav Template + Content Creation Engine — different target audience (this is for title execs targeting agents, yours is agent → consumer) but same script-engineering pattern, more rigorous.
Short-form vertical video is the most effective medium for building trust, authority, and visibility with real estate agents. This tool — a Custom GPT or a Gem — is designed to help title sales executives generate high-retention Instagram Reel scripts that speak directly to real estate agents.

Title Influencer Instagram Reel Script Generator

What This Is

Short-form vertical video is the most effective medium for building trust, authority, and visibility with real estate agents. Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts, and TikToks outperform every other format for reach, frequency, and memorability.

This tool—a Custom GPT (ChatGPT) or a Gem (Google Gemini)—is designed to help title sales executives generate high-retention Instagram Reel scripts that speak directly to real estate agents.

It produces content that:

Creates urgency and relevance around title, escrow, and closings.

Positions you as a knowledgeable resource with broad visibility across transactions.

Surfaces topics agents often overlook but should care about.

Generates hooks, angles, and scripts that perform well on social.

In simple terms:

It helps you create short, smart, compelling video scripts that make agents see title as essential—not an afterthought.

How to Use This

Step 1 — Decide where you want to build your tool

You have two choices:A) Build a Custom GPT inside ChatGPTB) Build a Gem inside Google Gemini

The remaining steps apply to both, with A/B options where needed.

Step 2 — Log into your platform

A) ChatGPT: Go to chatgpt.com and sign in.

B) Gemini: Go to gemini.google.com and sign in.

Step 3 — Create your custom GPT/GEM

A) In ChatGPT:

Look at the left sidebar.

Click “GPTs.”

In the top-right corner, click “Create.”

B) In Gemini:

Look at the left sidebar.

Click “Gems.”

Select “New Gem.”

Step 4 — Name and describe your GPT/GEM

For both platforms, you can name it anything you want (e.g., Title Reel Script Generator).

The description is optional—keep it short and clear.

Step 5 — Insert the instructions

Both ChatGPT and Gemini will give you a field for “instructions.”

Paste the provided instructions below into that field.

Prompt

Copy

# Custom AI Instructions — Instagram Reels Script Generator for Title Sales Executives

## Purpose
You create 45–60 second Instagram Reels scripts for title sales executives working within the Fidelity National Financial family of brands (Fidelity Title, Chicago Title, Lawyers Title, Ticor, Commonwealth, Alamo, and others).
Your audience for every script is real estate agents.

Your job is to generate short-form video scripts that:
- Create urgency around title, escrow, and closing issues.
- Highlight unseen risks or deal patterns agents often overlook.
- Deliver useful, practical insights based on real transaction visibility.
- Produce strong hooks with natural spoken cadence.
- Are optimized for high retention on Instagram Reels.

---

# Overall Workflow

You must begin every session by asking the user a structured set of questions and waiting for all answers before continuing.

---

# Required Questions You Must Ask the User

### 1. Title Brand
“Which title brand are you creating this as—Fidelity, Chicago Title, Lawyers Title, Ticor, Commonwealth, Alamo, or another FNF brand?”

### 2. Your Role
“What’s your role so I can match the voice and perspective? (For example: Title Sales Executive, Sales Manager, Escrow Officer, Branch Manager, etc.)”

### 3. Local Market
“Which market or region are you in? This helps me tailor topics, examples, and research to your territory.”

### 4. Audience
“Is your audience primarily listing agents, buyer agents, or both?”

### 5. Script Style / Personality Fit
**“How should the script sound to match your personality and communication style?
For example: direct, punchy, conversational, polished, authoritative, more playful, or more serious?”**

### 6. Video Style Preference
“Do you prefer a fast, punchy short-form feel or a slightly more explanatory 45–60 second style?”

### 7. Existing Topic or Data
“Do you already have a specific topic, trend, update, defect pattern, fraud alert, or piece of data you want this script to be based on?”

- If YES:
Ask them to share the details, then skip the research phase and proceed directly to scriptwriting.

- If NO:
Move to the next question.

### 8. Research Option
“Would you like me to conduct fresh research and return five trending, high-impact topic options for you to choose from?”

### 9. CTA Preference
**“Do you want the script to include a call to action?
If yes, what type? (Examples: follow for more insights, DM for support, ask for a checklist, or no CTA.)”**

---

# Research Phase (ONLY if the user does NOT provide a topic)

If the user wants research, you must perform a fresh, up-to-date web search to identify current, relevant, and trending issues affecting real estate transactions, title, escrow, and closings.

You should consider:
- Regulatory or compliance changes
- Wire fraud and cyber fraud patterns
- Recording delays or county procedure changes
- Underwriting shifts
- Title defect patterns
- Market conditions affecting timelines
- Delays caused by estates, trusts, divorces, HOAs, payoffs, investor entities
- NAR settlement implications
- Anything affecting closing timelines, risk, or agent experience

You must incorporate the user’s local market into your search logic.

Use this FNF Direct Operations page as brand context:
https://fnf.com/companies/title-insurance/#Direct%20Operations

Your priority is to surface topics that create:
- Urgency
- Relevance
- Exclusivity
- Authority
- Meaningful consequences for agents

---

# Returning Topic Options
After research, you must present exactly five strong topic options.

Each option must:
- Be concise
- Include a strong potential hook
- State why agents should care
- Tie directly to closings, timelines, risk, or client experience
- Be something a title rep can credibly talk about

If the user says “Search again,” you must redo the research and return five new topics.

Once the user chooses a topic, you must begin scriptwriting.

---

# Scriptwriting Rules

## Length
Write approximately 120–160 words, equal to 45–60 seconds spoken.

## Tone & Personality
You must:
- Match the user’s selected personality/style
- Write conversationally and confidently
- Use contractions and short sentences
- Avoid filler, hype, clichés, buzzwords, or vague generalities
- Speak directly to real estate agents, never consumers
- Focus on implications for deals, client prep, communication, and timelines

## Hooks
You must begin every script with a strong, agent-centered hook that:
- Creates urgency or curiosity
- Addresses a real risk or timeline issue
- Promises fast payoff

You must not use generic openers like:
“Today I want to talk about title insurance.”

## Agent Value Requirements
You must deliver one or more of the following:
- A risk agents underestimate
- A pattern you see across many files
- A shift affecting closings
- A practical step agents should take
- Something that protects clients or prevents delays

## Credibility Requirements
You must:
- Position the speaker as a knowledgeable title professional
- Avoid legal advice
- Avoid guaranteeing underwriting outcomes
- Ensure all information is accurate, safe, and non-speculative

## CTA Rules
If the user requested a CTA, you must include it at the end in one clean, natural sentence.
If they said “no CTA,” you must not include one.

## Delivery Format
You must return the final script inside one single preformatted text block (triple-backtick fenced) with no commentary.

Example:

[script only]

## Teleprompter Optimization
You must:
- Write with natural spoken rhythm
- Avoid long paragraphs
- Avoid stage directions unless requested
- Write the script as if the speaker is talking directly to camera

---

# Chain of Verification
Before delivering the script, you must run an internal self-check (not shown to the user).
If anything fails verification, you must correct it before returning the final script.

You must verify:

### 1. Topic Alignment
- Does the script match the chosen topic or data?
- Is it relevant to real est

Prompts (29)

Prompts

Agent Appointment Intelligence Dossier

Research any real estate agent before your first meeting and turn the dossier into an audio briefing for the drive over.

View source →
A complete workflow for researching a real estate agent before a meeting. You'll use the deep-research tool of your preferred LLM to generate a detailed "agent dossier," then feed that dossier into NotebookLM to produce a structured audio overview (like a podcast) you can listen to before an appointment, call, or first contact.

Agent Appointment Intelligence Dossier

What This Is

This resource gives you a complete workflow for researching a real estate agent before a meeting. You’ll use the deep-research tool of your preferred LLM (e.g. Gemini, Perplexity, ChatGPT, et al.) to generate a detailed “agent dossier,” then feed that dossier into NotebookLM to produce a structured audio overview (like a podcast) you can listen to before an appointment, call, or first contact.

The workflow is designed for vendors, service partners, and business-development professionals who want to understand:

Who the agent is (professionally and personally, based on public info),

How they work and make decisions,

Where they might need support,

How to approach them with the right message and tone.

How To Use This

Copy and Run the Full Research PromptScroll down to PROMPT 1 and copy it. Open your chosen LLM (Perplexity, Gemini, Grok, or ChatGPT), in the message box, enable its deep-research mode, paste the prompt in. Before submitting the prompt, replace the placeholders for the agent’s name, market/city, and brokerage, then submit it to generate the full agent dossier in a markdown format.

Copy the LLM Output (the “Dossier”) When the model finishes its response, copy the entire output it produces (all sections, top to bottom). Tip: Look for the icon of two overlapping, rounded-edge squares to copy the full response.

Open NotebookLM Go to https://notebooklm.google.com and sign in with your Google account.

Create a New Notebook Create a notebook dedicated to this agent (for example: “Agent Dossier – [Agent Name]”).

Add the Dossier as a Source In NotebookLM, click Add Source → Copied text. Paste the full dossier you copied from the LLM’s response and save it as a source.

Add Any Extra Sources (Optional) If you have them, add additional sources as separate items, such as:Crystal Knows profile, transaction or production reports, public bios or articles, any other relevant text documents.NotebookLM will use all of these together when generating the audio overview.

Generate the Audio Overview In the right-hand sidebar of the notebook—after you review the next step—click Audio Overview to create a spoken briefing based on your sources.

Customize the Audio Overview

Click the pencil icon (edit) on the Audio Overview panel.

Choose a Deep Dive style (or equivalent long-form option).

Paste the short ordering prompt from PROMPT 2 into the instructions box.

Save and generate the Audio Overview.

Listen or Download Play the Audio Overview directly inside NotebookLM, or download it so you can listen on the way to your meeting or before sending outreach.

PROMPT 1 — Full Agent Research Prompt

Prompt

Copy

## Role
You are a Strategic Intelligence Analyst for a Title Company tasked with preparing a comprehensive relationship and business-development briefing on a Real Estate Agent.

Your job is to research the agent’s professional footprint, public presence, and operating style to help a Title Sales Executive build rapport, time outreach correctly, and position the title company as a long-term partner.

—

## Custom Inputs
– Agent Name: [AGENT NAME]
– Market/City: [CITY / MARKET]
– Brokerage: [BROKERAGE]

—

## Research Scope

### 1. Professional Profile
– Brokerage and team affiliation
– Years in business
– Primary markets served
– Buyer vs seller mix (if evident)
– Production indicators (reviews, transaction counts, team size, rankings)
– Specialties (luxury, relocation, investors, new construction, distressed, etc.)

---

### 2. Transaction Behavior Signals
– Typical price bands operated in
– New construction vs resale mix
– Investor vs retail client mix
– Volume vs boutique service positioning
– Frequency of fast-close or multi-offer scenarios

---

### 3. Digital & Brand Presence
Research and summarize insights from:
– Zillow
– Realtor.com
– LinkedIn
– Facebook
– X (Twitter)
– Instagram / TikTok (if public)
– Personal or team website
– Google results and public articles

Include:
– Brand tone and positioning
– Posting frequency
– Topics emphasized
– Messaging themes (education, lifestyle, speed, trust, volume, etc.)
– Visual style and professionalism

---

### 4. Awards & Credentials
– Designations (CRS, GRI, ABR, SFR, CDPE, etc.)
– Brokerage or industry awards
– Leadership roles
– Teaching, coaching, or speaking involvement

---

### 5. Personal & Lifestyle Signals (public only)
– Hobbies or interests mentioned
– Community involvement
– Family mentions (high level only)
– Causes, charities, or events supported
– Personality style inferred from content (analytical, relational, energetic, structured, etc.)

---

### 6. Process & Pain-Point Clues
– Evidence of centralized vs decentralized transaction coordination
– Mentions of timelines, stress-free closings, or process
– Public frustrations or problem themes (lenders, inspections, delays, appraisals, etc.)
– Complexity of deals handled (relocations, estates, foreclosures, multi-state, etc.)

---

### 7. Decision-Making Style
– Data-driven vs relationship-driven
– Systems-focused vs personality-led
– Brand-first vs volume-first
– Leader, rainmaker, or hybrid role

---

### 8. Timing & Trigger Events
– Recent brokerage changes
– Team expansion or recruiting signals
– Major awards or rankings
– Market expansion
– Public growth language (“we’re growing,” “welcome to the team,” etc.)

---

### 9. Vendor & Partner Loyalty Signals
– Mentions or tags of title companies or escrow partners
– Patterns of repeated vendor use
– Language indicating exclusive or preferred partners
– Co-branded content with service providers

---

### 10. Compliance & Risk Sensitivity (public only)
– Emphasis on disclosures, ethics, or consumer education
– Any public litigation, complaints, or regulatory issues
– Tone around protection, transparency, or caution

---

### 11. Influence & Relationship Map
– Who appears to control vendor decisions (agent, team lead, TC, ops)
– Senior or anchor agents on team
– Visibility of operations or admin staff

—

## Output Format (Markdown)

### Agent Overview
– Name:
– Brokerage:
– Market:
– Years Active:
– Primary Focus:

---

### Business Model & Production
– Team or solo:
– Production indicators:
– Client type mix:
– Price band tendencies:

---

### Brand & Online Presence
– Zillow/Realtor.com positioning:
– Social media themes:
– Website messaging:
– Brand personality:

---

### Awards & Credentials
– Designations:
– Awards:
– Leadership roles:

---

### Personal & Lifestyle Insights
– Interests:
– Community involvement:
– Personality style:

---

### Process & Operational Signals
– Transaction coordination style:
– Complexity of deals:
– Public pain-point clues:

---

### Decision & Growth Signals
– Decision-making style:
– Growth trajectory:
– Trigger events:

---

### Partner & Loyalty Indicators
– Current partner signals:
– Openness to new partners:

---

### Conversation Starters
Provide:
– 3 professional talking points
– 3 personal rapport builders
– 2 value-add angles for a title partner

---

### Executive Summary
– 3 personalization strategies
– 3 strategic notes for relationship building
– 1 recommended outreach angle (warm, consultative, or strategic)

—

## Important Instructions
– Use only publicly available information
– No speculation or invasive details
– Clearly note when data is unavailable
– Write in professional, client-ready Markdown
– Prioritize insights useful for relationship building and business development
– Cite sources when possible

Show more

PROMPT 2 — Audio Ordering Prompt

Prompt

Copy

Provide a thorough briefing for a potential business partner preparing to meet this real estate agent. Start with who they are (market, brokerage, years active, team/solo, production). Then outline their brand tone, online presence, and personal signals they’ve p

Prompts

Blog Posts from Seller Search Trends

Create blog posts using trending seller keyword data, optimized for modern search and AI results.

View source →
A two-prompt system for generating locally optimized blog content using real keyword data. Designed for professionals who want to boost local SEO by publishing blog posts that match what customers are actually searching for in their area. Works in any large language model (LLM) that supports lengthy prompt inputs and document uploads.

Blog Posts from Seller Search Trends

What This Is

This is a two-prompt system for generating locally optimized blog content using real keyword data. It’s designed for professionals who want to boost local SEO by publishing blog posts that match what customers are actually searching for in their area.

You can run these prompts in any large language model (LLM) that supports lengthy prompt inputs and document uploads, including: ChatGPT, Claude, Grok, Gemini, et al.

Here’s how it works:

Prompt 1 helps you generate a list of SEO-optimized blog titles based on long-tail keyword questions. These titles are customized to your location and your business or brand name.

Prompt 2 expands any of those titles into a full, localized blog post — written in a clear, conversational style and optimized for voice search, Google, and AI-based searches.

You’ll provide some basic info (like your location and the name of your business), and attach a keyword data file. The prompts do the rest.

How to Use It

Step 1: Prepare Your Info

Before you run the first prompt, gather the following:

A keyword research spreadsheet (from AnswerThePublic or a similar tool)

Your Primary Keyword (broad topic, like “sell home”)

Your Specified Location (city, neighborhood, or region)

Your Business or Brand Name (agent, team, or company name)

Step 2: Run Prompt 1 – Blog Title Generation

This prompt uses your keyword data to generate blog titles that:

Reflect real search behavior using long-tail keyword questions

Are optimized for your specific location

Include your brand or business name naturally

Follow best practices for local SEO and AI-driven search tools

📎 Important: When you paste in Prompt 1, be sure to:

Attach your keyword research file

Fill in your info under the ## Key Data Points section of the prompt

Replace the highlighted sample text inside the lines like this:

Keyword Research Tool: AnswerThePublic (“Research Tool”)

Adjust the number of blog titles if you want more or fewer than 50

After Prompt 1 runs, you’ll get a customized list of blog post titles tailored to your local market.

Prompt

Copy

# Prompt 1: Writing the Blog Titles
You are an experienced SEO content strategist specializing in real estate.

## Key Data Points

Consider the following key data points in the completion and customization of this task:

  • Keyword Research Tool: AnswerThePublic ("Research Tool")
  • Primary Keyword (broad): Sell Home ("Primary Keyword")
  • Data Source: Google ("Data Source")
  • Language: English ("Language")
  • Country or Region: USA ("Region")
  • Specific Location (city, region, neighborhood, etc.): Location ("Specified Location")
  • Real Estate Agent or Team Name: Agent or Team Name ("Agent or Team")

## Task Instructions

Review the attached spreadsheet containing keyword data from the Research Tool. The data includes searches related to the Primary Keyword, sourced from the Data Source in the specified Language across the Region. Follow this two-step process:

### Step 1: Nationwide Keyword Analysis

  • Analyze the provided nationwide data.
  • Prioritize long-tail keywords based on search volume, competitiveness, and relevance to natural, conversational queries potential home sellers commonly ask.
  • Specifically target long-tail keywords formatted as complete, natural-language questions optimized for AI-driven and voice searches.
  • Dynamically infer home sellers’ likely concerns, motivations, or related topics based on the selected long-tail queries.

### Step 2: Localized Blog Title Creation

  • Using the long-tail queries previously identified in Step 1, tailor and localize each query specifically to the Specified Location. Prominently feature the Specified Location keyword and relevant variations (e.g., "selling a home in [Specified Location]," "[Specified Location] real estate agent," etc.) to maximize local SEO relevance.
  • Incorporate specific neighborhood or landmark names within the broader Specified Location when contextually appropriate, further enhancing local search visibility.
  • Explicitly reference the Agent or Team name, naturally integrating it to personalize the titles (e.g., "[Agent or Team]'s Guide to Selling a Home in [Specified Location]").
  • Ensure all titles reflect conversational, exact, or closely matching natural-language questions to directly address and capture search intent, maximizing visibility in AI-driven search engines like ChatGPT, Grok, and Perplexity AI.

### Final Output: 50 SEO-Focused Blog Post Titles

Create 50 conversational blog post titles clearly reflecting optimized, localized, and personalized long-tail queries.

#### SEO Best Practices for AI Search

##### Demonstrate E-E-A-T Compliance
Clearly demonstrate Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, and Trustworthiness (E-E-A-T) by:

  • Incorporating authoritative language and terms such as "Expert Guide," "Trusted Advice," or "Proven Strategies."
  • Conducting additional research on the specified real estate agent or team to authentically reflect their genuine expertise, achievements, and local authority.
  • Conducting supplementary research on the Specified Location, including relevant neighborhoods, landmarks, and local real estate market trends, ensuring titles accurately capture local context and resonate with the target audience.

##### Optimize for Exact-Match Queries
- Include exact-match conversational queries within blog post titles whenever possible to align closely with AI-generated search result criteria.

##### Compliance and Regulatory Adherence
Ensure strict adherence to all relevant real estate laws, regulations, and ethical standards, including:

  • Federal Regulations:
  • The Fair Housing Act
  • RESPA (Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act)
  • Regulations prohibiting steering and commission fixing
  • Recent NAR (National Association of REALTORS®) settlements
  • Professional and Ethical Standards:
  • NAR Code of Ethics
  • State Real Estate Commission advertising policies

##### Originality and Content Integrity
- Explicitly confirm that all generated content is original, plagiarism-free, and uniquely tailored to the agent or team.

##### Professional Consultation Disclaimer
- Refrain from providing specialized advice (tax, legal, accounting, or financial). Instead, clearly recommend that readers consult licensed professionals for such matters.

Show more

Step 3: Run Prompt 2 – Blog Writing (One Title At A Time)

Once you have your list of blog titles, move on to Prompt 2 to write full blog posts.

How it works:

Choose a blog title from your list

Paste in Prompt 2

Replace the phrase in the prompt that refers to the “first” (or “second” or “third”) of the “50” generated blog titles with the exact blog title number you want to use

Keep your Primary Keyword, Specified Location, and Agent or Team Name consistent throughout

Run Prompt 2 to generate a complete blog post (1,200–1,800 words)

The post will:

Start with the blog title as a header

Restate the title as a natural-language question

Provide a short answer up top (for AI/voice search)

Include local references, insights, and compliance language

End with a strong call to action and a short meta description

👉 Repeat this step for each blog title in your list.

Prompt

Copy

# Prompt 2: Writing the Blog Post

You are an experienced SEO-focused real estate content writer.

## Key Data Points
*(Restated from Prompt #1 for clarity and ease of reference.)*

  • Keyword Research Tool: AnswerThePublic ("Research Tool")
  • Primary Keyword (broad): Sell Home ("Primary Keyword")
  • Data Source: Google ("Data Source")
  • Language: English ("Language")
  • Country or Region: USA ("Region")
  • Specific Location (city, region, neighborhood, etc.): Location ("Specified Location")
  • Real Estate Agent or Team Name: Agent or Team Name ("Agent or Team")

## Task Overview

Your tas

Prompts

CRM Database Health Audit - Roadblock Report

A Claude prompt that reads your CRM export and tells you exactly which leads to call today — plus personalized scripts based on real data.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your GHL CRM Audit — GoHighLevel-specific with behavioral bucketing, Pipeline Health Score, Pipeline Mismatch Detection, and optional GitHub Action automation.
A Claude prompt that reads your CRM export and produces a one-page report on where an agent is stuck in their pipeline — plus the exact leads they should be calling today. No more staring at a database wondering who to call first. This surfaces the broken metrics, identifies the hottest leads, and writes personalized scripts based on each lead's actual history.

CRM Database Health Audit - Roadblock Report

What This Is

A Claude prompt that reads your CRM export and produces a one-page report on where an agent is stuck in their pipeline — plus the exact leads they should be calling today. No more staring at a database wondering who to call first. This surfaces the broken metrics (dormancy rate, missed follow-ups, dialer activity), identifies the hottest leads, and writes personalized scripts based on each lead’s actual history.

Agents get decision fatigue from overwhelming databases. Team leaders coach on vibes instead of data. This cuts through both problems with a performance scorecard and action plan that takes 5 minutes to run.

How to Use It

Log into your CRM (Sierra Interactive, Follow Up Boss, kvCORE, etc.).

Pull a full CSV export of the agent’s leads. Include every column your CRM gives you — status, source, last activity, history, visits, properties viewed, tags, notes, dialer activity, all of it. Don’t clean or trim anything.

Open a fresh Claude Cowork chat (one agent per chat).

Drag the CSV file into the chat.

Paste this prompt:

Prompt

Copy

You are my real estate operations analyst. I just uploaded a CSV. It is a full lead export from Sierra Interactive for one of my agents. I have not cleaned or filtered it. Read every row. Cross-reference activity, history, visits, tags, status, and notes on your own.

Produce an Agent Roadblock Report. Use plain English, short sentences, hyphens instead of em-dashes, and a confident, no-fluff tone. Pull every number, name, date, and address straight from the CSV - do not invent anything. If a field is missing, mark it as Unknown rather than skipping it.

The report should be organized into three parts. You can adjust formatting (tables, bullets, paragraphs) to whatever reads cleanest, but make sure all of the following information is captured and clearly presented.

PART 1 - PERFORMANCE SCORECARD
A snapshot of the agent's activity inside Sierra. Include the metrics that are useful for spotting dormancy, missed follow-up, and overall pipeline health. At minimum cover: total database size, dormancy rate (leads with no activity in 90+ days), cold or lost active leads, year-to-date closed transactions and volume, recent session activity, last login, new leads in the last several weeks, leads not yet viewed, time to first email, time to first call, dialer activity, outbound call/text/email counts in the last several weeks, and tasks sitting with no action. Flag each metric as OK, Warning, or Critical based on whether it is healthy, slipping, or genuinely broken. Use your judgment on thresholds based on what looks normal vs. neglected in the data.

PART 2 - HOTTEST LEADS RIGHT NOW
The top handful of leads (5 is a good default) most likely to convert in the near term. For each one, capture name, status, days since last touch, and source. Pick based on a combination of recent engagement signals (visits, properties viewed, replies, lead score, recent activity) and viable status. Rank them in priority order.

PART 3 - PERSONALIZED FOLLOW-UP SCRIPTS
For each of the leads listed in Part 2, give the agent everything they need to make the call. Capture:
Lead name, type (buyer, seller, nurture), and source.
- Phone, email, and location.
- Price band, visit count, and properties viewed.
- Key Intel: a short paragraph pulling specific facts from the CSV - what they clicked, what the agent has already done, dates of last touches, referral source, lead score, anything that explains why this lead is hot or stuck. Use real dates and real properties from the data.
- A recommended script or approach the agent can read off their phone. Reference the last specific interaction. Sound human, not corporate. If the lead has been called multiple times with no callback, pivot to text or email and write the actual message to send.

ASK ME FIRST
Before producing the report, ask:
1. Agent's first and last name.
2. Their role (listing, buyer, dual).
3. Any context I want factoredin (new agent, slump, just back from leave, personal stuff, etc.).

Then read the CSV and produce the report.

Show more

Answer the clarifying questions about the agent.

Claude reads the entire export and produces your report.

Using This with a Different CRM: This prompt is built for Sierra Interactive, but works with any CRM. Just swap “Sierra Interactive” for your platform name and adjust the metrics in Part 1 to match what your system exports. The structure (scorecard, hottest leads, scripts) works the same way.

Tips: Run one agent per chat for clean results. Don’t pre-filter the data — let Claude spot the patterns. Use the performance flags (OK/Warning/Critical) to prioritize coaching conversations. The personalized scripts reference real interactions from the CRM, so they sound natural instead of generic.

Attribution: The Wolfe Team, Jupiter Florida (Erica Wolfe & Alexandra Simon)

Prompts

CRM Database Health Audit Report Generator

Paste the prompt into Claude Cowork with any CRM export and get back a branded Word document with charts, per-agent accountability tables, and 5 ready-to-read action items for your next team meeting.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your GHL CRM Audit — produces the same Word output, plus executes cleanup, plus optional scheduled automation.
A reusable prompt designed for Claude (Cowork mode) that analyzes any real estate CRM export and produces a fully formatted Database Audit Report as a Word document — complete with embedded charts, tables, and data visualizations. Built for team leaders who want to hold agents accountable, identify revenue at risk, and surface forgotten leads buried in their CRM.

CRM Database Health Audit Report Generator

What This Is

A reusable prompt designed for Claude (Cowork mode) that analyzes any real estate CRM export and produces a fully formatted Database Audit Report as a Word document — complete with embedded charts, tables, and data visualizations.

This tool is built for real estate team leaders who want to hold their agents accountable, identify revenue at risk, and surface forgotten leads buried in their CRM. It works with any CRM that can export to CSV — Follow Up Boss, KVCore, Sierra Interactive, Cinc, BoomTown, LionDesk, Chime, Real Geeks, and others.

It turns a raw CRM export into a boardroom-ready audit with charts, tables, and specific next steps — in minutes instead of hours. Here’s what you get:

A clear picture of your database health — total contacts, stage breakdown, and inactivity gaps.

A “Lost Profits” flag that catches active pipeline leads that have gone cold.

Per-agent accountability breakdowns showing activity, dormancy, and engagement.

A “Sleeping Database” report surfacing forgotten leads sitting untouched for months.

Five plain-language action items you can read directly in your next team meeting.

What you’ll need:

Claude desktop app with Cowork mode enabled.

A CSV export from your CRM containing your full contact database.

A folder on your computer where you save the CSV and where Claude will output the report.

How to Use It

Export your data from your CRM. Pull a full CSV of your contacts. The export should include as much data as possible — contact names, pipeline stages, assigned agents, lead sources, tags, and any activity or communication history (notes, calls, texts, emails). The more columns you include, the better the analysis.

Open Claude in Cowork mode. Launch the Claude desktop app and enter Cowork mode. Select the folder where your CSV export is saved.

Paste the prompt below into the Claude chat. Claude will scan your CSV, figure out which columns map to the data it needs, run the full analysis, generate charts, and produce the Word document — all automatically.

Prompt

Copy

# Real Estate CRM Database Audit Report

## Purpose
You are analyzing a real estate CRM export (CSV or spreadsheet file) located in this folder. Your job is to produce a professional Word document (.docx) called "Database Audit Report" saved to this same folder. The report must include embedded charts and data visualizations generated as PNG images and placed directly inside the document.

The audience is a real estate team leader who will use this report in 1-on-1 meetings and team accountability sessions. Write in direct, plain language. Name names. Use specific numbers. Do not hedge or generalize.

---

## Step 1: Data Discovery and Column Mapping

Before running any analysis, you must first scan the CSV file and map its columns to the data fields below. Different CRMs use different column names (e.g., Follow Up Boss uses "Stage" while others may use "Status" or "Pipeline Stage"). Read the header row, sample a few data rows, and identify which columns best match each required field.

Required fields to map:

  1. CONTACT NAME - The person's name. Look for columns like: Name, Full Name, First Name + Last Name, Contact Name, Person.
  1. DATE CREATED - When the lead entered the system. Look for: Date Added, Created Date, Date Created, Registration Date, Created At, Add Date.
  1. PIPELINE STAGE - The lead's current stage or status. Look for: Stage, Status, Pipeline Stage, Lead Status, Contact Status, Deal Stage. Note the exact stage names used in this CRM, as they vary (e.g., "Showing Homes" vs. "Active Buyer" vs. "Touring").
  1. ASSIGNED AGENT - Who owns the lead. Look for: Assigned To, Agent, Assigned Agent, Owner, Lead Owner, Assigned User. If a value appears to be a team name or company name rather than an individual person, treat it as an "unassigned pool."
  1. LEAD SOURCE - Where the lead came from. Look for: Lead Source, Source, Origin, Lead Type, Marketing Source, Campaign.
  1. TAGS - Any labels or categories. Look for: Tags, Labels, Categories, Groups.
  1. LAST ACTIVITY DATE - The most recent interaction. This may be a single column (Last Activity, Last Contact, Last Touch, Last Communication Date) OR it may need to be derived from multiple activity columns (Notes, Calls, Texts, Emails, Messages, Activity Log). If activity is stored as text entries with embedded dates (e.g., "Agent texted Contact on 2/26/2026"), parse the most recent date from each activity column and use the latest one. Also check for columns like Last Assigned, Last Updated, or Last Modified. Use whichever date is most recent. If no activity date can be determined, fall back to the Date Created.
  1. ACTIVE PIPELINE STAGES - After scanning the Stage/Status column, identify which stages represent leads who are actively working toward a transaction. Common examples: "Showing Homes," "Submitting Offers," "Active Buyer," "Touring," "Under Contract," "Listing Agreement," "Active Listing." These are the stages used for the Lost Profits analysis. If you are unsure which stages qualify, use any stage that implies the lead is past initial contact and actively engaged in buying or selling.
  1. EARLY/DORMANT STAGES - Identify which stages represent leads still in the earliest part of the pipeline. Common examples: "Lead," "New," "Attempted Contact," "Uncontacted," "New Lead," "Inquiry." These are the stages used for the Sleeping Database analysis.

After mapping, silently proceed to analysis. Do not ask me to confirm the mapping. Use your best judgment.

---

## Step 2: Analysis and Report Structure

The report must contain exactly these sections in this order:

### Title Page Area
- Report title: "DATABASE AUDIT REPORT"
- Subtitle: "[Team/Brokerage Name] - CRM Database Analysis" (infer the team name from the data, such as the most common value in the Assigned Agent pool, the filename, or any team-level identifier you find)
- Report date (use today's date)

### Executive Summary
One paragraph, 4-6 sentences. State the total database size, the percentage inactive for 90+ days, the number of active pipeline leads going cold, the number of sleeping/forgotten leads, and the single biggest operational issue you found. This paragraph should be blunt and specific enough that the team leader can read it out loud and the team immediately understands the problem.

### Section 1: Database Health Overview
Contents:
- Total contact count (bold)
- Table: Stage breakdown with columns: Stage | Count | % of Total. Sort by count descending.
- Chart: Horizontal bar chart showing contacts by pipeline stage. Color: cyan (#4ECDC1).
- Subheading: "Inactivity Summary"
- Table: Three rows showing contacts with zero activity in 30+ days, 60+ days, and 90+ days. Columns: Inactivity Window | Contacts | % of Total. Highlight the 90+ row in red.
- Chart: Bar chart showing the three inactivity buckets (30/60/90 days) with counts and percentages labeled on each bar. Colors: orange for 30+, red for 60+, dark red for 90+.

### Section 2: Lost Profits Flag
Section heading should be styled in red to signal urgency.

Definition: Leads in active pipeline stages (identified in Step 1, field #8) with no activity in 30+ days. These are leads who were close to a transaction and are now going cold.

Contents:
- Introductory paragraph explaining which stages you identified as active pipeline stages, why these leads matter, and the total count of cold leads.
- Chart: Bar chart showing cold lead count per agent (exclude the unassigned pool from this chart). Color: red. Label each bar with the count.
- Note (bold, red text) stating how many of these leads are sitting in the unassigned pool and need immediate reassignment.
- For each agent who has cold leads: a subheading with the agent's name, followed by a table with columns: Lead Name | Stage | Days Inactive | Lead Source. Sort by days inactive ascending. Color-code the Days Inactive values: 30-59 days = default color, 60-89 days = orange, 90+ days = red and bold.

### Section 3: Agent Accountability Breakdown
Contents:
- Introductory paragraph noting how many leads are in the unassigned

Prompts

Community Happenings Research Prompt

Discover timely local events, updates, and developments you can repurpose into a community newsletter.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your Local News Scraper — automated production version feeding newsletter-generator + content-creation-engine.
A deep research prompt designed to help you gather a reliable, scannable synopsis of community happenings in a specific local area. Built for real estate professionals who want a quick way to source relevant local content — new businesses, zoning updates, infrastructure projects, community events, or lifestyle highlights.

Community Happenings Research Prompt

What This Is

This resource is a deep research prompt designed to help you gather a reliable, scannable synopsis of community happenings in a specific local area. It’s built for real estate professionals who want a quick way to source relevant local content—things like new businesses, zoning updates, infrastructure projects, community events, or lifestyle highlights.

The generated output of this prompt isn’t meant to be finished content. Instead, it gives you the raw research you can repurpose into whatever format you choose—whether that’s an email, a video, a blog, or another type of update.

How To Use It

Copy the Prompt Tap the clipboard icon to copy the entire prompt provided on this page.

Paste into Your AI Research Tool Paste the prompt into your AI of choice—such as Perplexity, Grok, or ChatGPT. (These three offer deep research functionality.)

Fill in the Placeholders (within the Prompt) Edit the fields for:

Area/Location (e.g., Richmond, VA)

Past Timeframe (e.g., past 7 days, past month)

Upcoming Timeframe (e.g., this weekend, next 10 days)

Enable Deep Research Most tools can auto-detect, but explicitly enabling research is safest.

Perplexity: In the composer/search bar, open the mode selector and switch from Search (magnifying glass) to Deep Research (often shown with an atom icon) before submitting.

Grok : Research is typically Auto in the composer; availability depends on your plan (e.g., Premium+ or SuperGrok). Verify that real-time search/tool use is active.

ChatGPT: Click Tools in the composer and choose Deep research (plan-dependent). You can also use Agent mode from the tools dropdown; the original Deep research option remains in Tools. Quotas and availability vary by plan.

Submit the Prompt Run the prompt. The AI will generate a categorized synopsis of what’s been happening and what’s coming up in the selected area.

Repurpose the Results Use the synopsis as the foundation for your next piece of content. Adapt it into an email, video script, blog, social post, or any other format you want.

Prompt

Copy

You are tasked with conducting deep research to create a synopsis of recent and upcoming community happenings in a specific area.

### Customization Fields:
- Area / Location: {{Insert city, town, or neighborhood}}
- Timeframe - Past: {{Insert how far back to review, e.g., past 7 days, past month}}
- Timeframe - Upcoming: {{Insert forward-looking window, e.g., this weekend, next 10 days}}

### Instructions:
1. Confirm today’s current date so your analysis references the correct timeframe.
2. Research and summarize findings across the following categories:
- Local Events: Festivals, concerts, parades, farmers markets, sports, etc.
- Community & Lifestyle: School events, non-profits, fundraisers, neighborhood gatherings.
- Business Openings/Closures: Restaurants, shops, gyms, entertainment venues.
- Government & Policy: Zoning changes, city council updates, new regulations, ordinances.
- Development & Infrastructure: New construction projects, housing developments, road work, transit updates, city planning.
- Historical/Recent Recap: Notable events or community highlights from the past {{timeframe - past}}.
- Upcoming Highlights: What’s happening in the next {{timeframe - upcoming}}.

  1. Provide your output as a clear, scannable synopsis with short sections under each category.
  2. Include dates, names, and sources/links wherever possible so the information can be verified and cited in a newsletter.
  3. If certain categories have no available updates, note “No significant updates found.”

### Output Format:
- Use Markdown with headers for each category.
- Keep tone neutral, informative, and community-focused.
- Make the synopsis ready to adapt directly into a real estate agent’s newsletter.

Show more

Prompts

Deep Market Insight for Video Scripts

A structured AI prompt that analyzes a loaded data source to generate an in-depth market insight for a video script.

View source →
This prompt turns raw MLS data into compelling, 60-second Instagram Reel scripts that speak directly to home sellers. Ideal for agents who want to consistently publish market-driven content without sounding generic, robotic, or off-topic. Unlike other tools that rely on preset styles or uploaded samples, this prompt works on demand.

Deep Market Insight for Video Scripts

What This Is

This prompt is designed to turn raw MLS data into compelling, 60-second Instagram Reel scripts that speak directly to home sellers. It’s ideal for agents who want to consistently publish market-driven content without sounding generic, robotic, or off-topic.

Unlike other tools that rely on preset styles or uploaded samples, this prompt works on demand. It’s data-first, meaning the script is always based on a real insight pulled from your dataset. The tone stays true to how a savvy, approachable agent would actually speak on video—no filler, no fluff.

Use this prompt when you want a fast, clean, well-paced video script that helps sellers understand what’s really going on in the market—based on actual numbers, not guesswork.

How To Use It

Follow these steps to generate a script that’s ready to record:

  1. Upload your MLS data as a CSV or spreadsheet file.Make sure it includes recent stats like list price, sold price, DOM (days on market), and active/expired listings. The more complete your data, the better the insight.
  1. Copy and paste the full prompt (in Markdown format) into ChatGPT.Paste it exactly as-is. It’s already formatted with instructions, tone guidance, and performance rules built in.
  1. Let the AI analyze the file and surface one meaningful insight.It will find a single trend, shift, or data point that sellers need to hear about—something that goes beyond “inventory is up” or “prices are stable.”
  1. Review the script it generates.The result should be:

130–160 words

Written in a single paragraph (no labels, bullets, or directions)

In your natural voice—not overly formal, not “AI-speak”

Featuring a strong hook, useful insight, and soft CTA

Pro Tip: If the script feels slightly off-tone, you can add a note like“Make it punchier” or “Lean more into urgency” and re-run it using the same insight.

  1. Record your video and post it to Instagram.You’ve now got a powerful, data-backed script designed to stop scrolls, educate sellers, and position you as the market expert.

Use this prompt weekly or monthly as part of your content rhythm. It helps you stay top-of-mind while delivering real value—and that’s how you get more listings.

Prompt

Copy

## 🎯 Objective
You’ve been given an MLS dataset. Your job is to analyze the data and identify one clear, meaningful insight that would matter to prospective home sellers. This should reveal a trend, shift, or unexpected detail—not surface-level. Focus on things like:

  • Pricing changes
  • Inventory shifts
  • Buyer demand
  • Days on market

## ⚠️ Data Integrity
Use only the data provided. Do not fabricate, infer, or extrapolate numbers that aren’t in the file.

---

## 🎬 Script Instructions

Write a script for a 60-second Instagram Reel based on the insight. Follow these rules:

  • ✍️ Tone: Conversational, confident, and natural—like a trusted local expert speaking directly to camera.
  • 🧠 Hook: Start with a bold statement, intriguing question, or surprising fact that relates to the insight.
  • 📊 Explain: Present the data clearly and simply. Use everyday language. Space out stats for readability and natural pacing.
  • 📣 CTA: End with a subtle, seller-focused call to action (e.g., “Thinking about selling? Let’s talk.”)
  • ⏱️ Length: Keep it between 130–160 words—well-paced for 60 seconds of spoken delivery.
  • ✅ Format: Output the script as one clean paragraph. No section headers, no bullet points, no stage directions.
  • 🤖 Avoid AI-sounding phrasing. It should feel like a human—me—talking naturally on camera.

---

## 🧪 Example Hook Formats (for inspiration)

  • “Most homeowners have no idea this just happened in our market.”
  • “Thinking of waiting until fall to sell? You might want to see this first.”
  • “Here’s what buyers are doing differently right now—and how it could affect your home sale.”

Show more

Because AI-generated outputs can vary, it’s your responsibility to review and refine them for accuracy, relevance, and alignment with applicable laws, industry standards, and your specific business objectives.

Prompts

Google Sheet Marketing KPI Dashboard

Use Claude to generate a Google Sheet and Apps Script that track, organize, and analyze your marketing metrics.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your Content Calendar — yours produces the 7-tab V12 dashboard with live data validation, week-over-week trending, and CRM intelligence.
Companion to the Marketing Plan KPI Grid lecture. Walks you through how to use the prompt and style guide to generate your own Google Sheet Marketing KPI Dashboard using Claude. You'll copy both files — the prompt and the style guide — and submit them together in a single message to Claude.

Google Sheet Marketing KPI Dashboard

What This Is

This is the companion to the Marketing Plan KPI Grid lecture (Ai Rockstars). It walks you through how to use the prompt and style guide provided below to generate your own Google Sheet Marketing KPI Dashboard using Claude.

You’ll copy both files—the prompt and the style guide—and submit them together in a single message to Claude. Because of their length, they’ll appear as attachments, which is exactly what you want. Claude will then:

Ask a few intake questions to personalize the dashboard (which marketing channels, how data will be provided, reporting cadence, etc.).

Build a Google Sheet dashboard with cards for each marketing channel you selected.

Create a corresponding Google Apps Script that crunches your numbers and updates the dashboard based on your chosen data method.

This process turns your marketing plan into a living, trackable dashboard—without needing any coding experience.

How to Use This

Follow these steps to set everything up.

Start a new chat in ClaudeOpen Claude in a new chat window. You’ll be adding both the prompt and the style guide into this same message.

Copy and paste the promptScroll down to the Prompt box on this page and click the clipboard icon to copy it. Then paste it into your new Claude message.

Copy and paste the style guideNext, scroll to the Style Guide box and click its clipboard icon to copy it. (This will be the second prompt box below.) Paste it directly to the same Claude message.Because of their length, Claude will automatically treat both as attachments—this is correct and expected.

Add one short messageUnder the two attachments, type this single line before sending:“Follow the instructions in the attached prompt and companion style guide.”

Send everything togetherYou should now have one Claude message containing both attachments and your one-line instruction. Send it. Claude will recognize both documents and begin by walking you through a short intake to customize your dashboard.

Answer Claude’s intake questionsYour answers tell Claude how to structure your dashboard and script. It will ask for:

Which digital marketing channels you want to track (e.g., Instagram, Facebook Page, Meta Ads, YouTube, Google Search Console, email, etc.)

How you’ll provide the data — CSV exports or API connections

Your brand preferences (fonts, colors, and overall styling) so the dashboard matches your look

Let Claude build everythingAfter the intake, Claude automatically designs:

A Google Sheet dashboard with cards for each channel you selected

A matching Apps Script that processes your data

If you chose CSVs, a recommended Drive folder setup for your exported files (i.e., the data you’ll download from your marketing channels like Meta and Google)

Review Claude’s follow-up stepsAs one of its outputs, Claude will include detailed, step-by-step instructions specific to your setup. These steps explain how to:Import or export your data from each platformConnect API keys or CSV filesSet up your Google Drive folders and permissionsRefresh and maintain your dashboard over timeWhen it comes to platform-specific or technical details, defer to Claude’s own instructions—they’re tailored to your answers.

Finish setup in Google SheetsOnce Claude gives you your files (the Google Sheet and Apps Script), follow the steps it provides to open, convert, and connect them in your Google account.

When Claude gives you an XLSX file, download it to your computer. Then, in Google Drive, create a new Google Sheet and go to File → Import → Upload. Click Browse Files and attach the XLSX file you just downloaded. When prompted, choose to replace the current sheet with that file.If you’ve enabled MCP with Google Drive in Claude, you can also click the Drive icon to open the file directly in Drive. Just remember to go to File → Save as Google Sheets, or you won’t be able to use the Apps Script extension.

Then add the Apps Script via Extensions → Apps Script as directed.

Update your dashboard regularlyYou can refresh it monthly (CSV) or let the Apps Script run automatically (API). The script will handle calculations, comparisons, and card updates.⚠️ Important: Before using any API connections, double-check that the Apps Script is configured as read-only. It should only pull data from your marketing platforms—not edit or post anything back to them. Claude’s setup instructions should also remind you never to paste an API key directly into the Apps Script code. Instead, store it as a Script Property in your Apps Script project settings for security. This keeps your credentials hidden and protected.

Use it to measure your planYour finished dashboard gives you one place to track results across every marketing channel—so you can see what’s working, what’s not, and where to focus next.

In short: Start a new Claude chat → paste the prompt → paste the style guide → add the line “Follow the instructions in the attached prompt and companion style guide” → hit send. Claude will take it from there, guiding you through setup and building your personalized Google Sheet Marketing KPI Dashboard.

Prompt: Build Your Google Sheet Marketing KPI Dashboard

Prompt

Copy

# Marketing Dashboard Builder - Prompt

Instructions for users:
1. Upload STYLE_GUIDE.md to this conversation
2. Paste this entire prompt
3. Answer Claude's questions about your platforms and preferences
4. Claude will build your custom dashboard

---

You are an expert marketing analytics consultant specializing in building custom Google Sheets dashboards. Your job is to interview the user about their marketing technology stack and create a personalized, automated dashboard that consolidates their data.

You have access to STYLE_GUIDE.md which contains:
- Complete platform specifications and CSV structures
- Dashboard design standards (colors, fonts, layouts)
- Apps Script code templates and patterns
- Sample implementations

## Interview Process

### Question 1: Platform Selection

Ask the user which marketing channels they want to track. Provide clear context and examples:

Your message:

"I'll help you build a custom marketing dashboard that tracks your digital marketing and content performance across multiple platforms.

Which marketing channels do you want to track? (Select all that apply)

Paid Advertising:
- Meta Ads (Facebook/Instagram advertising)
- Google Ads
- TikTok Ads

Organic Social Media & Content:
- Instagram (business/creator account)
- Facebook Business Page
- YouTube Channel
- LinkedIn Profile (creator analytics)
- TikTok (business account)

Search & Website:
- Google Search Console (organic search performance)

Email Marketing:
- Mailchimp
- Constant Contact
- Klaviyo
- Other email service providers

Important: This dashboard is designed for digital marketing performance - things like social media, paid ads, email campaigns, and website traffic. It's not designed for offline marketing activities like direct mail, print advertising, open houses, or postcards.

Just list the platforms you use. For example: 'Meta Ads, Instagram, Facebook Page, YouTube, Mailchimp, Google Search Console'"

---

### Question 2: Data Source Method

After the user lists their platforms, explain the two data source options and provide recommendations:

Your message:

"Perfect! Now, for each platform, I need to know how you'll get data into your dashboard.

Two options:

📁 CSV Exports (Manual but simple)
- You export data files from each platform (I'll show you exactly how)
- Upload the CSV files to a Google Drive folder
- Click 'Refresh' in your dashboard to process the data
- Takes 5-10 minutes per update (weekly or monthly)
- No API keys or technical setup required
- Complete control over what data you import

🔌 API Integration (Automated but requires setup)
- Dashboard automatically fetches fresh data
- Requires API keys from each platform (I'll provide step-by-step setup)
- More technical initial setup
- Data updates automatically when you refresh
- Some platforms may have rate limits

**Here are my recomme

Prompts

HTML Market Update Email Newsletter

Generate a ready-to-send HTML email that analyzes your MLS data and presents it as a market update for homeowners.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your Newsletter Generator — yours covers the market-update section as part of the full multi-section weekly email.
Create a professionally designed, insight-rich real estate market update email — automatically. Using Claude's Artifact feature, transforms MLS data you export into a spreadsheet into a polished HTML email that tells the story of your local market. The prompt analyzes data, identifies noteworthy trends, and wraps everything in a ready-to-send layout.

HTML Market Update Email Newsletter

What this is:

This resource helps you create a professionally designed, insight-rich real estate market update email—automatically. Using Claude’s Artifact feature (or a similar tool), it transforms MLS data you export into a spreadsheet into a polished HTML email that tells the story of your local market.

The prompt doesn’t just format content—it analyzes your MLS data, identifies noteworthy trends, and wraps everything in a layout that’s ready to send to homeowners. It’s perfect for database nurturing, listing lead follow-up, or any moment when a smart market update could spark a conversation.

How To Use It

Review the full prompt below so you understand its key components—personalized greeting, market insight, charts, offline viewing, and PDF support.

Customize the prompt with your local market. Change any references to location (e.g., Franklin, Tennessee) to reflect your target area. Update the date range mentioned in the intro to match your dataset.

Prepare your MLS data in a spreadsheet. You’ll need Closed (sold), Expired, and Withdrawn listings. Label them clearly and define the time period you’re covering in your data. Otherwise, adjust the prompt if you alter your MLS search.

Upload the spreadsheet to Claude when you run the prompt. Attach the spreadsheet directly in Claude and paste in your customized prompt.

Generate the Artifact and review the output. Claude will generate a complete HTML email based on your MLS data. If you want to include your branding (logo, fonts, or colors), just let Claude know in a follow-up prompt and upload a simple brand kit file. You can also ask for layout or content changes, or make manual edits directly in the HTML.

Make any final edits. Ask Claude to make tweaks if you need to adjust layout, style, branding, or tone. You can also edit the HTML manually if needed.

Send it. Copy the final HTML from Claude and paste it into the “Code your own” section of your email service provider (like Mailchimp, Constant Contact, or KVCore). This gives you full control over formatting and ensures the design displays properly in inboxes.

Prompt

Copy

Assume the role of a masterful graphic artist and expert in interactive infographic design. Your task is to create an HTML email newsletter based exclusively on the data in the attached spreadsheet. Do not alter, fabricate, or infer any information beyond what is provided. The spreadsheet contains a list of homes in Franklin, Tennessee, categorized by Closed (sold), Expired (unsold during the listing period), and Withdrawn (removed from the market by the seller). This email newsletter will serve as a Real Estate Market Update, presenting key trends through expertly designed charts, graphs, and interactive visual elements to inform and engage homeowners. Use the date range in the data to generate a relevant title that accurately reflects the time period covered.

• • •

REQUIREMENTS:
*All instructions listed below must be followed:*

• • •

Personalized Greeting:  
– Personalize the greeting by addressing the recipient by their first name if available. If no name is present, default to a friendly salutation like “Friend” or “Homeowner.” Format it according to standard email merge tag syntax.

Compelling Market Insight:  
– Analyze the data to identify one specific and compelling insight that would resonate with homeowners considering selling their property.  
– Write a three- to four-sentence paragraph summarizing this key market trend, highlighting a data point that would make a homeowner stop and think about selling.  
– Ensure the language is persuasive yet factual, without exaggeration.

Data Visualization:  
– Develop interactive charts and graphs that help homeowners visualize key trends and performance metrics year-to-date.  
– Use only the spreadsheet data—do not add or assume any missing information.

Offline Functionality:  
– Ensure the entire infographic, including all charts, graphs, and images, is fully viewable and functional in any web browser without an internet connection.

Expert Design:  
– Apply advanced graphic design principles to create a visually appealing, easy-to-read, and engaging layout.  
– Add section headers and concise explanatory text where appropriate to guide the reader through the data.

PDF Compatibility:  
– Include a “View Full Report & Save as PDF” button that opens the infographic in a web browser, where it is properly formatted for 8.5×11 inch paper (portrait orientation) and includes a built-in “Save to PDF” option for easy downloading.  
– Ensure charts and graphs do not get split across pages—they should be properly formatted to start on a new page if needed.  
– Maintain clean margins and proper spacing to avoid content cutting off at the edges.

Coding Standards:  
– Write clean, structured HTML, CSS, and JavaScript that follows best practices for email design.  
– Ensure cross-device and cross-email client compatibility.

Data Integrity:  
– Use exclusively the data provided in the spreadsheet.  
– Do not generate, infer, or hallucinate additional information.

By following these instructions, you’ll create a highly effective real estate market update email that engages homeowners, provides valuable data-driven insights, and encourages potential sellers to reach out.

Show more

Prompts

Homeowner Sentiment Deep Research

Use AI-powered tools to analyze homeowner sentiment trends across a specific geographic area.

View source →
This prompt instructs Grok, an AI developed by xAI, to conduct a qualitative analysis of homeowner sentiment around listing residential real estate in your chosen geographic area. Grok's DeepSearch feature performs multi-step research using real-time data from social platforms, forums, news sites, and authoritative publications.

Homeowner Sentiment Deep Research

What This Is

This prompt instructs Grok, an AI developed by xAI, to conduct a qualitative analysis of homeowner sentiment around listing residential real estate in your chosen geographic area.

Grok’s DeepSearch feature performs multi-step research using real-time data from social platforms, forums, news sites, and authoritative publications. It’s the only major AI currently connected to a live social feed (i.e., X – formerly Twitter), giving it a unique edge in surfacing raw, unfiltered public sentiment—especially around local events and housing conversations.

This kind of deep research is a growing strength of advanced AI tools (like Grok, ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Gemini). It refers to autonomous exploration and synthesis of live web content into a structured, source-supported report that captures not just what people are saying—but how they feel and why it matters.

Once Grok completes its analysis, you can copy the results into an AI podcasting tool like NotebookLM to enrich content with fresh, location-specific insights.

How To Use It

To get the most relevant and accurate output, you’ll want to tailor the prompt to your specific research goals. You may choose to modify the prompt based on the following preferences:

Research FocusWhat are you trying to uncover? You might focus on homeowner sentiment, perceptions of market conditions, emotional readiness to sell, or the impact of recent events (like a natural disaster, policy change, or media narrative).

Geographic AreaBe specific. Local sentiment varies widely, so name the city, ZIP code, or even the neighborhood you want Grok to analyze.

TimeframeUse the past 30 days as a baseline, or adjust to target specific seasonal trends, recent news cycles, or moments of market activity.

Targeted SourcesIdentify which types of platforms are most likely to hold valuable insights—e.g.,

X (formerly Twitter) for real-time reactions

Reddit for long-form homeowner stories

Facebook Groups or Nextdoor for hyperlocal chatter

YouTube comments or news sites for opinionated takes

Once you’ve updated the prompt with your preferences, run it in Grok. After it completes its DeepSearch analysis, review the findings for accuracy and tone. From there, you can copy the results into a content tool like NotebookLM (or any AI writing assistant) to generate more informed, hyperlocal scripts, summaries, or talking points.

Prompt

Copy

Conduct a comprehensive qualitative analysis to deeply understand current homeowner sentiment, perceptions, and psychological factors specifically related to selling residential real estate in Franklin, Tennessee.

### Sources of Analysis:

Gather and analyze data from discussions published within the past 30 days, drawing insights from:

  • Official news outlets and authoritative real estate sources.
  • Online forums (e.g., Reddit).
  • Social media platforms (e.g., X/Twitter, Instagram, Facebook).
  • Relevant local community groups or discussion channels.

### Analysis Objectives:

Your analysis should precisely identify and summarize:

  • Prevailing emotional sentiments (positive, neutral, negative) expressed by homeowners considering listing their homes.
  • Key psychological motivators or barriers influencing homeowners’ decisions.
  • Dominant themes, trends, and concerns, including emerging anxieties or optimistic attitudes explicitly expressed by potential sellers.
  • Notable shifts or changes in homeowner attitudes compared to previous periods, if discussed.

### Scope:

Exclude general or indirect real estate topics unrelated to homeowner sentiment and intentions regarding listing their homes. Ensure your findings remain directly relevant to homeowners in Manhattan Beach, highlighting nuanced insights that clearly reveal their current mindset, motivations, reservations, and overall emotional atmosphere toward selling residential real estate.

Show more
