Prompts

Listing Announcement Email Blast

Generate a polished, ready-to-send campaign email for your new property listing using Claude.

View source →
Create a ready-to-send announcement email for a new property listing. You'll use the prompt inside Claude, because Claude is generally stronger than other AI tools (ChatGPT, Gemini, Grok) when it comes to writing the code that makes emails display correctly across inboxes.

Listing Announcement Email Blast

What This Is

This tool helps you create a ready-to-send announcement email for a new property listing. You’ll use the prompt below inside of Claude, because Claude is generally stronger than other AI tools (like ChatGPT, Gemini, or Grok) when it comes to writing the “code” that makes emails display correctly across inboxes.

Think of it this way: every email is made of code (1s and 0s). Your email client — whether that’s Gmail, Outlook, or Apple Mail — takes that code and converts it into the text, images, and buttons you see. This prompt tells Claude exactly how to “code” your email so it’s polished, branded, and ready to paste into your email platform.

The final result is a campaign-style email designed to:

Look professional on computers and phones.

Show key details (like price and address) as text, not just inside images.

Feature real property photos with descriptions.

Include one clear main button as the call-to-action.

Load quickly so recipients don’t have to wait.

How to Use It

Copy the prompt. Scroll down, click the clipboard icon on the prompt box, and paste it into Claude.

Answer Claude’s questions. The prompt will trigger Claude to ask you for the details it needs. It may ask for them all at once or one at a time (depending on how you respond). Be ready with:

Property address (required)

Listing link (MLS or property website)

Price and status (e.g., “Just Listed,” “Active”)

Specs (beds, baths, square footage, lot size, year built, garage, etc.)

Top 3–5 features (bullet points)

A short, neutral neighborhood note (no school or crime claims)

Open house details (optional)

Photos: ⚠️ You can’t attach or upload images directly into Claude. Instead, you’ll need to provide links (URLs) to your photos.

Best practice: upload the photos into your email platform (Mailchimp, Constant Contact, etc.) and copy those links.

While you could use Dropbox or Google Drive links, many email clients (like Gmail) may block those images from displaying.

Video (optional): You don’t upload the video file. Instead, provide two links:

A link to a thumbnail image (a still picture that looks like a video preview).

A link to where the video plays online (YouTube, Vimeo, your property site, etc.). The thumbnail will display in the email, and when someone clicks it, they’ll be taken to the video.

Signature info (name, phone, email, website, license #, brokerage, logo)

Main call-to-action (like “View Full Listing” or “RSVP for Open House”)

Subject line and preview text preferences

Brand elements (colors, fonts, logo, header style, rounded corners)

Greeting style (with or without a personalized “Hi [First Name],” — if yes, provide your email platform’s merge-tag syntax or ask Claude to research it for you).

Pick your subject line and preview text. Claude will give you 5 subject line options and 5 preview text options. Choose one of each.

Get your finished email. Claude will return a complete announcement email in one block. It includes:

Hidden preview text

Header (logo or header image)

Main property photo

Highlight block (price, address, specs, features)

Open house section (if provided)

Gallery (if provided)

Main call-to-action button

Signature with your info and brokerage

Social links (optional)

Footer handled by your email platform

Paste into your email platform. Open a new “HTML” email in your provider (Mailchimp, Constant Contact, kvCORE, etc.). Paste the code. It will render as a professional campaign email.

Test before sending. Send yourself a test email to confirm:

All links work (listing, maps, CTA, etc.).

Photos show up properly (if not, re-upload them into your email platform and replace the links).

The main button is clear and clickable.

If Claude didn’t have certain details, you’ll see [INSERT LATER] in those spots — update those before sending.

Send your campaign. Once everything looks right, send it to your audience.

Prompt

Copy

# Email Listing Blast Builder

Role: Build a single, branded HTML email (table-based with inline CSS) announcing a new property listing. Follow this prompt for the entire chat. If uncertain, refer back to this message.

Design Rules
- Table-based layout only; all critical styles inline.
- Fixed 600px container; minimal mobile media query.
- Absolute HTTPS URLs for all images/links.
- Real alt text for images; no placeholders or empty src.
- Live text (price, address) — don’t bake text into images.
- One clear primary CTA button (secondary links allowed but de-emphasized).
- Keep HTML lean (target <80KB).

---

## Intake (batched by default; ask one-at-a-time only if user requests)

Provide in one message (bullets OK):

1) Property address (required)
2) Listing URL (MLS or property site)
3) Price & status (e.g., Just Listed, Active)
4) Specs (beds, baths, sq ft, lot, year built, parking/garage, HOA if any)
5) Top 3–5 features (bulleted)
6) Neighborhood highlight (1–2 neutral lines; avoid school/crime claims)
7) Open house (optional: date(s), time(s))
8) Media
- Hero image URL (1)
- Gallery image URLs (up to 6)
- Video tour URL (use thumbnail + link; no embeds)
9) Signature
- Name, title, phone, email, website, license # (if required), brokerage name & address, brokerage logo URL (optional)
10) Primary CTA (e.g., Book a Private Showing / View Full Listing / RSVP for Open House / Custom)
11) Subject preferences (tone/keywords; default: clean, no emojis)
12) Preheader text (40–100 chars) or ask to draft
13) UTM parameters (optional)
14) Social links (optional: IG, FB, YT, TikTok, LinkedIn)
15) Brand elements
- Fonts (headings, body)
- Colors (primary, secondary, accent in hex)
- Logo URL (optional)
- Header style (logo-only or full-width image?)
- Rounded corners (Yes/No; default: Yes) 16) Greeting style
- Do you want a merge-tag salutation? (e.g., “Hi [First Name],”)
- If yes, provide the correct merge-tag syntax for your ESP.
- If no, email will start without a personalized greeting.

Compliance (always on): No school ratings, crime stats, or protected-class language. Respect MLS “Coming Soon” rules. If details are missing, insert [INSERT LATER] (don’t fabricate).

---

## Subject & Preheader
- Draft 5 subject options (28–50 chars) tailored to inputs.
- Draft 5 preheaders (40–100 chars) that complement (not repeat) the subject.
- Ask user to confirm one subject + one preheader.

---

## Build the Email (Sections in this exact order)
1) Preheader (visually hidden)
2) Header (logo or header image)
3) Hero image
4) Listing highlight block (price, address, specs, 3–5 bullets)
5) Open House (optional)
6) Gallery (optional, 2–3 columns, 1–2 rows)
7) Primary CTA button
8) Signature (contact + brokerage + license #)
9) Social links (optional)
10) Footer note (unsubscribe handled by ESP; do not add manually)

Always output complete, ready-to-send HTML inside a single code block using the framework below.

---

## HTML Framework to Follow (replace tokens like [ADDRESS], [PRICE], etc.)

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8">
<meta name="x-apple-disable-message-reformatting">
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
<title>[SUBJECT_PLAIN]</title>
<style>
/* Minimal mobile adjustments */
@media only screen and (max-width:600px){
.container{width:100%!important;min-width:320px!important;margin:10px!important}
.content{padding:20px 16px!important}
.stack{display:block!important;width:100%!important}
.btn{display:block!important;width:80%!important;max-width:280px!important;margin:0 auto!important}
.gallery-col{display:block!important;width:100%!important;padding-right:0!important;margin-bottom:10px!important}
}
</style>
</head>
<body style="margin:0;padding:0;background:#F7F7F9;">
<!-- Visually hidden preheader -->
<div style="display:none!important;max-height:0;overflow:hidden;opacity:0;color:transparent;">

Prompts

Listing Appointment Deep Research & Audio Briefing

Generate a property and seller dossier with AI research, then turn it into a customized audio overview you can listen to on your way to the appointment.

View source →
A prompt-driven research workflow that helps you prepare for a listing appointment with professional, fact-based insights. Using deep research tools like Perplexity, Grok, or ChatGPT's deep research mode, the prompt generates a property and seller dossier in clean Markdown format. Then you take the output into NotebookLM to produce a structured audio briefing.

Listing Appointment Deep Research & Audio Briefing

What This Is

This resource is a prompt-driven research workflow that helps you prepare for a listing appointment with professional, fact-based insights. Using deep research tools like Perplexity, Grok, or ChatGPT’s deep research mode, the prompt generates a property and seller dossier in clean Markdown format.

The dossier includes:

Full chronology of the property (year built, builder, ownership history, zoning, architectural notes, renovations).

Market context (comparable sales, neighborhood trends, pricing patterns).

Seller dossier (career, community involvement, past real estate activity).

A summary with key opportunities and considerations for your appointment.

Once the dossier is created, you’ll take the output into NotebookLM (Google’s free AI notebook). There, you can paste the text and generate an audio overview — a spoken debrief you can listen to on the way to your appointment, keeping you sharp and confident.

How To Use It

  1. Copy the Prompt

Use the full research prompt provided.

Fill in the property address and seller name(s).

Prompt

Copy

## Role
You are a Real Estate Research Analyst tasked with preparing a comprehensive briefing for an agent’s upcoming listing appointment.
Your job is to research the property and its owners, compiling a thorough, well-organized dossier.

---

## Custom Inputs
- Property Address: [PROPERTY ADDRESS]
- Owner(s) Name(s): [SELLER NAME(S)]

---

## Research Scope

### Property Research
- Full chronology of the property: year built, original builder/developer, prior land use, notable renovations, additions, or past sales.
- Ownership history: timeline of past owners and transfers.
- Comparable sales and neighborhood trends: pricing patterns, turnover rate, appreciation.
- Architectural/style notes: any notable design history or unique features.
- Zoning, permits, and development context: relevant local planning or changes over time.
- Market positioning: how this property fits into current local supply/demand.

### Seller Research (non-invasive, professional context)
- General background that’s useful in a listing appointment (career/industry, community involvement, public mentions).
- Past real estate activity (e.g., has sold/bought multiple times in the area).
- Lifestyle/context clues that may shape motivation (downsizing, relocating, etc.) — but avoid anything private, speculative, or intrusive.
- Network or affiliations relevant for understanding credibility and rapport-building.

---

## Output Format (Markdown)

### 📍 Property Dossier
1. Basic Facts
- Address: [repeat input]
- Year Built:
- Builder/Developer:
- Style/Type:
- Lot/Size:

2. Chronology
- [Year]: Built by…
- [Year]: Major remodel…
- [Year]: Ownership transfer to…

3. Comparable Market Context
- Recent neighborhood sales:
- Price per sq ft trends:
- Inventory patterns:

4. Architectural / Historical Notes
- [Details]

5. Zoning / Development Context
- [Details]

---

### 👤 Seller Dossier
1. Background Overview
- Occupation/Industry:
- Community presence:
- Public records of note:

2. Real Estate History
- Past transactions in the region:
- Typical property type owned:

3. Possible Seller Motivation Context
- [Summarized carefully — relocation, lifestyle shift, etc.]

---

### ✅ Final Summary
- 3 key opportunities for positioning the property competitively.
- 3 potential considerations the agent should be aware of before meeting.

---

## Important Instructions
- Findings must be factual, well-sourced, and clearly presented.
- Avoid speculation, gossip, or personal/invasive details.
- When data is uncertain, note confidence level or say “No public data found.”
- Write clearly in professional, client-ready markdown.

Show more

  1. Run the Research

Paste the prompt into a deep research tool (Perplexity, Grok, or ChatGPT deep research).

Wait for the dossier output in Markdown format.

  1. Copy the Output

Copy the entirety of the deep research output — it’ll be an entire synopsis.

  1. Open NotebookLM

Go to notebooklm.google.com and sign in with your Google account.

Create a new notebook.

  1. Add the Research as a Source

Click Add Source → choose Copied Text.

Paste the dossier and click Submit.

  1. Generate the Audio Overview

In the right-hand panel, select Audio Overview.

Either play the default version or click the ⋮ (three-dot menu) to add a custom instruction.

  1. Customize Your Audio Overview (Optional)

Use the preformatted prompt below to guide the audio overview.

Tap the “…” action menu in the top corner of the Audio Overview box/button and paste the prompt below.

Prompt

Copy

### Audio Overview Customization

Focus: Deliver a listing appointment prep briefing for the agent.

Structure to follow:
1. Property Facts
- Year built, builder/developer, style, lot size.
- Renovations, ownership changes, notable history.

2. Market Context
- Comparable sales and price trends.
- Inventory and turnover patterns.
- Positioning in today’s market.

3. Seller Context
- General background (career, community involvement, prior real estate activity).
- Rapport-building details (non-invasive, professional).

4. Key Talking Points for Appointment
- 3 opportunities to emphasize in marketing/presentation.
- 3 considerations or potential challenges to be aware of.

Tone & Delivery:
- Professional but conversational, as if advising the agent directly.
- Prioritize clarity and actionability over raw data.
- Keep the flow logical: property → market → seller → talking points.
- Summarize insights as “what matters for this appointment.”

Show more

From the Session: This prompt was introduced in Exclusive Success Summit Debrief.

Prompts

MLS Price Cliff Analysis Video Script Generator

Turn MLS data into a powerful visual story about what happens when homes sit too long on the market.

View source →
The MLS Price Cliff Analyzer is a plug-and-play LLM prompt that helps you surface one of the most important seller-facing insights in your market: what happens to a home's value when it doesn't sell quickly. Analyzes your spreadsheet of closed sales to pinpoint the moment — based on actual data — when pricing power starts to slip.

MLS Price Cliff Analysis Video Script Generator

What This Is

The MLS Price Cliff Analyzer is a plug-and-play LLM prompt that helps you surface one of the most important seller-facing insights in your market: what happens to a home’s value when it doesn’t sell quickly.

This tool analyzes your spreadsheet of closed sales to pinpoint the moment—based on actual data—when pricing power starts to slip. It gives you a number you can talk about: “After 21 days, homes in your market sell for 6% less on average.”

Instead of just saying, “You’ve got to price it right from the start,” now you can back it up—with both data and language that hits.

Here’s a script you might use in a listing conversation to drivethe point home:

Your property is never more valuable to a buyer than on the day it’s listed, spurred by the buyer’s instinct to act quickly for fear of losing out to someone else. However, as days turn into weeks, this urgency fades—and, as a result, so does the property’s perceived value. Your property attracts the most attention on the day it’s listed because popular search platforms prioritize new listings. But as your listing ages, both visibility and buyer interest naturally decline. Understanding these factors, how critical do you believe it is to get everything right from the start?

With a single MLS upload and this prompt, you’ll get:

A sharp, stat-backed talking point about how value erodes with time on market

A script written for a short-form video like Instagram Reels

A zoomed-in graph that visually shows the cliff—dramatically and clearly

You’ll walk away with a data-backed Instagram Reel script—plus a visual chart—that helps you educate sellers, strengthen your listing presentation, and position yourself as the kind of agent who shows the strategy, not just talks about it.

How to Use It

Export your MLS data Start by downloading the data you want to analyze. Make sure it includes only closed (i.e., sold) listings. A spreadsheet—CSV or Excel—is best.

Copy the prompt and upload your MLS data Use the clipboard button in the top-right corner of the prompt below to copy it. Then paste it into your preferred LLM (like ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, or Grok), and in that same message, upload your MLS spreadsheet for analysis.

Press enter and let the AI run the analysis The model will:

Identify or infer key fields like sales price, list price, and days on market

Clean and organize the data

Detect the point where pricing power breaks down

Ask you for clarification if it can’t confidently map your columns

Review the three outputs You’ll get:

A cliff summary — when the drop happens, and how much value is lost

A short-form video script — written for Instagram Reels

A graph — clean and zoomed-in to visually highlight the pricing drop

Record your Reel using a green screen overlay

Open the ChatGPT conversation on your phone

Screen record the part showing the graph and summary

Save that video to your camera roll

Open Instagram → Reels → Tap Effects → Choose Green Screen

Set your screen recording as the background

Record yourself delivering the script on camera (or personalize it with your own take)

Prompt

Copy

# MLS “Price-Cliff” Analyzer & Insta-Reel Script
*This message includes MLS data and instructions. Analyze the data and follow the prompt as written.*

---

## 📥 1. Required Input

You have been provided a spreadsheet containing closed (i.e., sold) residential sales.

### Required fields (exact column names may vary):
- Days on Market – the number of days each property was actively listed before going under contract or closing. If not directly provided, infer it using listing and closing dates.
- Original List Price – the initial asking price when the property was first listed, *not* the final list price after reductions.
- Final Sales Price – the actual price the buyer paid at closing.

> Use best-effort reasoning to identify these fields, regardless of spacing, punctuation, or formatting.
> If any required field cannot be confidently identified or inferred, respond with:
> “⚠️ Unable to identify one or more required columns. Please clarify the column names, or re-export the spreadsheet with full listing and sale details.”

### Optional but helpful fields:
- Property location details (e.g., City, Zip Code)
- Date-related fields (e.g., List Date, Close Date)
- Price category fields (e.g., Price Range, Price Bracket)

---

## 🎯 2. Objective

Analyze how the average sale price (relative to original list price) changes as days on market increases.Your goal is to determine whether there’s a clear inflection point—a “cliff”—where pricing power drops significantly.

### Specifically:
1. Identify the earliest point in time where the average sale-to-original-list ratio drops by ≥ 5 percentage points compared to earlier listings.
- Use logical groupings (e.g., 7-day or 10-day buckets) based on what best fits the distribution.
- Ensure that each comparison group includes at least 5 closed sales to ensure statistical weight.
2. Filter for meaningful patterns:
- If the first significant drop occurs after 100 days on market, treat it as too late to be actionable. In that case, return:
“📊 No early price cliff detected. No meaningful drop within the first 100 days on market.”
3. If a valid price cliff is found, return all three outputs defined in §4.
- If no valid cliff is found, skip the Instagram script and graph, and only return the summary line above.

---

## ⚙️ 3. Method (internal)

1. Identify required fields:
- Determine which columns represent:
• *Final sales price* (what the home sold for)
• *Original list price* (the first asking price when listed)
• *Days on market* (or infer it using listing and closing dates)
- Use contextual clues, formatting variations, or common MLS phrasing. If any value cannot be reasonably determined, refer to the fallback in §1.

2. Clean the data:
- Drop rows with missing or non-numeric values in any of the three required fields.

3. Calculate pricing retention:
- For each property, calculate the ratio:
sale_to_olist_ratio = final_sales_price / original_list_price

4. Group by time on market:
- Organize listings into logical day-range groupings (e.g., 7-day or 10-day buckets) based on distribution.
- Only include buckets with 5 or more sales to avoid noise.

5. Detect the “cliff”:
- Identify the earliest bucket where the average ratio drops ≥ 5 percentage points compared to earlier groupings.
- If the first such drop occurs after 100 days, return the fallback summary and skip outputs in §4.

6. If valid cliff is found:
- Return the Markdown summary (4.1), Instagram script (4.2), and chart (4.3) with zoomed focus around the drop.

7. If no cliff is found:
- Return:
“📊 No early price cliff detected. No meaningful drop within the first 100 days on market.”
- Do not return a graph or script.

---

## 📤 4. Deliverables

### 4.1 Cliff Summary (Markdown)
Early Price Cliff Detected at: {DOM_bucket_start}–{+6} days
Avg. % of Original List Received: {ratio_cliff × 100} %
Typical Discount vs. Week 1: {delta_pct} pp

### 4.2 Instagram Reel Script (≈ 60 sec)
- Hook – A short, punchy opening (≤ 12 words) that includes the cliff stat.
- Build – 3–4 short sentences that explain what happens at the cliff and why it matters (buyer behavior, urgency, etc.).
- CTA – Prompt viewers to DM for pricing strategy or a local market breakdown.

### 4.3 Graph
- Generate a Matplotlib line chart:
• X-axis: days on market (bucketed)
• Y-axis: % of original list received (sale-to-list ratio × 100)
• Highlight the cliff bucket with a red marker and annotation
• Zoom the x-axis to ± 30 days around the cliff for visual clarity
- Show inline using python_user_visible
- Title the chart: YourTown Price Retention vs. Days on Market (Built ≤ YYYY)

---

## 🖋️ 5. Style & Guardrails

  • Quote exact percentag
Prompts

Marketing Plan Builder Prompt

Build a complete, professional real estate marketing plan in one ChatGPT conversation. This prompt turns ChatGPT into a guided planning session that walks you through…

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your Content Calendar — yours is the real strategic planner with live social performance + Search Console + Reddit + competitor data feeds.
The One-Shot Marketing Plan is a guided ChatGPT workflow that builds a complete, professional real estate marketing plan — in one conversation. Like a 1:1 strategy session, taking you from your current numbers to a clear, personalized action plan you can print or hand off to Canva, Claude, or any creative AI for design.

Marketing Plan Builder Prompt

What This Is

The One-Shot Marketing Plan is a guided ChatGPT workflow that builds a complete, professional real estate marketing plan — in one conversation.

It’s like a 1:1 strategy session with Jason Pantana, taking you from your current numbers to a clear, personalized action plan you can print or hand off to Canva, Claude, or any creative AI for design.

This prompt walks through five phases:

Audit – Review current production and spend.

Goals – Set measurable targets and budgets.

Sources – Identify where business will come from.

Channels – Map how you’ll market to reach those goals.

Plan – Generate a clean, branded report ready to export.

Everything is built into the prompt — no setup or templates required.

How To Use This

Copy the Prompt Click the clipboard icon in the prompt box below to copy it, then paste it into ChatGPT.

Personalize It ChatGPT will ask for:

Your name or team name

Plan period (e.g., “Full Year 2026”)

Whether to include a “Generated on [date]” label

Whether to customize colors and fonts or keep the default design style

Complete the Audit Enter quick stats: transaction count, GCI, marketing spend, lead sources, and whether you’re in Growth, Maintain, or Make-Money-Now mode. You can also define your target market if you want extra context.

Set Goals Choose whether your main goal is GCI, units, or both. Define your timeframe and ideal business mix, and the system will suggest a marketing budget (≈ 10 % of Adjusted GCI).

Define Sources & Channels Assign percentages of your total goal to each source (sphere, farming, social, ads, referrals, etc.). Then outline which channels will activate each source — with cadence, focus, and sample KPIs.

Generate Your Plan When ready, type “Generate my plan.” ChatGPT will build a full multi-page document with your data, tables, and a branded cover — ready for PDF or Canva export.

Tip: Keep your answers short and realistic. The assistant will only provide examples when you ask. You’ll finish with a clear, customized marketing plan you can print, share, or present to your team.

Prompt

Copy

# ONE-SHOT MARKETING PLAN

You are acting on behalf of Jason Pantana, an AI & marketing coach trained in Ai Marketing Academy’s One-Shot Planning Framework.
Your purpose is to guide a real estate professional through one structured conversation that produces a comprehensive, prescriptive marketing plan — whether for a full year, a single quarter, or another defined period — that they can confidently execute.

---

## CONTEXT: THE ONE-SHOT PHILOSOPHY

This framework helps real estate professionals *reverse-engineer* their marketing strategy from clear, numeric goals.
The process is logical and sequential — Goals → Sources → Channels → Plan — mirroring how real businesses generate production results.

  1. Goals define what the agent wants to achieve — expressed in units, volume, or GCI.
  2. Sources identify *where those deals will come from* (sphere of influence [SOI], geographic farming, online ads, SEO, partnerships, etc.).
  3. Channels outline *how those sources will be worked* through marketing and communication.
  4. Plan compiles everything into an actionable, tactical playbook.

> Clarification: Sources are where clients originate — the starting point of opportunity — while Channels are the marketing and communication tools used to reach, nurture, and convert those opportunities. Opportunities from any source will ultimately appear as *leads or referrals* — typically buyers or sellers, though they may also include leases or outgoing referrals — which are outcomes, not sources themselves.

Marketing activities may be relationship-based, lead-generation-based, or brand-building — often a mix — depending on the source being worked.
Every plan should balance education and execution: teaching the agent what to do *and* how to do it.
AI’s role is to guide, fill knowledge gaps, and prescribe actionable next steps grounded in real estate reality.

---

## ROLE

You are a coach, not a scribe.
Your tone is confident, professional, and conversational — like Jason Pantana coaching an agent one-on-one.

Responsibilities:
- Ask one focused question at a time and summarize each phase.
- Use clear, instructive language.
- If answers are vague, make reasonable, real-estate-specific assumptions.
- Follow the exact five-phase sequence.
- You may reference general frameworks (like SMART goals or KPIs) *only* to clarify — never to replace or reorder these five phases.
- Adapt to solo agents, teams, or brokerages by scaling recommendations.
- Focus on client acquisition (buyers & sellers); leases and outgoing referrals are optional ancillary revenue.

---

## PERSONALIZATION (ask first)

1) Agent or team name for the plan
2) Plan period (e.g., 2026 or Q1 2026)
3) Include “Generated on [today’s date]”?
4) Any color/font preferences for the final document?

---
## PHASE 1 — AUDIT *(establish baseline)*

Purpose: Understand the agent’s current production, market conditions, and marketing context.

Collect:

  • Total transactions — total closed sales; list counts for buy-side and list-side separately
  • Sales volume & GCI — total sales volume; GCI both gross and adjusted (after broker splits and referral fees)
  • Marketing spend (last 12 months) — approximate total invested in marketing
  • Primary sources of business — where closings originated last period (e.g., sphere/past clients, agent network, geographic farming, SEO/web, online ads & portals, partnerships, events/open houses)
  • Market served — core areas, ZIP codes, and typical price range

After collecting, summarize findings clearly — the agent’s production profile, marketing spend, and primary business drivers — to form the baseline for goal setting.

---

## PHASE 2 — GOALS *(begin with the end in mind)*

Purpose: Establish clear, measurable goals and determine the business mode that fits those ambitions.

Start by reinforcing the principle of reverse engineering:
> “We’ll begin with the end in mind. Once we define where you want to go, we’ll work backward to identify the sources and channels that will get you there.”

Ask:
- Which numeric goal feels most motivating — Units, Volume, GCI, or a combination?
- What’s your specific numeric target for this period (e.g., 36 units, $20 M volume, $500 K GCI)?
- Desired business mix (% listings / % buyers)
- Estimated average GCI per deal

Once goals are defined, evaluate alignment between past performance, marketing spend, and desired growth to determine the most realistic business mode.

Explain:
> “Your business mode reflects how aggressively you’re operating relative to your goals and marketing investment.”

  • Growth Mode — reinvesting to scale (≈ 10–15 % of Adjusted GCI in marketing).
  • Maintain Mode — steady operations (≈ 8–10 %).
  • Make-Money-Now Mode — lean, ROI-driven (≤ 7 %).

If the agent’s stated goal implies > 40 % growth but their spend < 8 %, flag the gap and offer coaching context:
> “That’s ambitious given your current spend level. Should we design a Growth-Mode plan to match, or rebalance expectations for sustainability?”

Let the user confirm or adjust their stance.

Then, calculate and display a concise summary table linking goals, mode, and recommended marketing budget.

| Metric | Target | Mode | Recommended Marketing Budget |
|-------------|-------------|-----------|----------------------------------|
| Units / Volume / GCI | 36 units / $20 M / $500 K GCI | Growth | $50 K (10 %) |

---

Budget Guide: Recommend approximately Adjusted GCI × 10 %, flexing by mode (higher for Growth, lower for Make-Money-Now).

Transition:
> “Great — now that we know what you’re aiming for and how you intend to operate, let’s pinpoint *where* your next deals will come from. That’s your next phase: Sources.”

---

## PHASE 3 — SOURCES *(workable origins of buyers/sellers)*

Purpose: Identify w

Prompts

Optimize Your Bio for AI Search Engines 📊

Boost your chances of showing up in AI search recommendations by rewriting your bio with credibility signals.

View source →
⚠️ GAP — BUILD Will be part of future LLM Listing Engine profile-engineering module. See Gaps to Build.
AI search engines like ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google's AI Overview are transforming how people find information online. Instead of searching by links, users are asking questions like: "Who's the top real estate agent in [neighborhood]?" Boost your chances of showing up by rewriting your bio with credibility signals.

Optimize Your Bio for AI Search Engines

What This Is

The way consumers search is changing fast—and real estate professionals need to keep up.

AI search engines like ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google’s AI Overview are transforming how people find information online. Instead of searching by links, users are asking questions like:

“Who’s the top real estate agent in [neighborhood]?”

“Which REALTOR® sells the most homes in [city]?”

“Best luxury buyer’s agent in [area]?”

But here’s the twist: AI doesn’t evaluate your website the same way Google used to.

It can’t see your reviews on Google, Zillow, Yelp, or other platforms—those are dynamic elements AI search doesn’t index.

It heavily prioritizes static, indexable text, especially your bio or profile content.

It favors websites that already carry strong domain authority, which means your placement on high-traffic directories matters.

It looks for clear authority signals in your transaction history—but sites like Zillow and FastExpert often display outdated or incomplete data.

It ranks content higher when it includes EEAT indicators—that is, expertise, experience, authoritativeness, and trustworthiness.

That means your bio is one of the most important ranking factors in AI search—and this resource will help you get it right.

💡 Did you know? Google’s AI Overview now shows in nearly half of all Google searches, and consumer adoption of tools like ChatGPT and Perplexity is exploding.

The way people find professionals like you has changed—your discoverability now depends on how well you show up in AI-generated answers.

How to Use It

This prompt is designed to help you rewrite your bio so it performs better in AI-driven search results. You don’t need to be technical—just follow these steps:

Copy the prompt below and paste it into ChatGPT (or your favorite AI assistant).

It will give you a quick summary of what it does, then ask:

For your current bio

For key details about your experience, production, reviews, specialties, and more

Answer each question honestly and thoroughly.

After gathering enough information, it will generate three versions of your bio:

A short (1–2 sentence) version

A medium paragraph version

A long, detailed version

Use these bios anywhere you want to boost AI visibility:

Your website

Your Google Business profile

Zillow, Realtor.com, FastExpert, Yelp

Email signature or online directories

Where AI Pulls Agent Information From

AI search tools like ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google’s AI Overview pull from websites that have three key attributes:

High domain authority

Static, indexable content

Structured data about real estate agents

These nine platforms show up most frequently in AI-generated answers:

FastExpert

RealTrends

Yelp – especially the “10 Best [City] Real Estate Agents” pages

HomeLight – which also powers USNews.com agent rankings

Zillow

Reddit

Realtor.com

Expertise.com

Homes.com

💡 Why it matters: AI favors structured data, like ranked lists, comparison tables, and consistent agent stats (e.g., reviews, transactions, specialties).

That’s why pages like “10 Best Agents in Nashville” on Yelp or FastExpert often get pulled into answers—they’re easy for AI to read and extract from.

Make sure your profiles on these platforms are:

Claimed and verified

Updated with reviews and transaction history

Written in third person using factual, localized details

That’s how you increase your chances of being listed when someone asks,

“Who’s the best real estate agent in [your city]?”

Prompt

Copy

## Real Estate Agent Bio Optimization for AI Search

> I’m a real estate agent looking to improve my bio specifically for AI-based search engines like ChatGPT, Google’s AI Overviews, and Perplexity.

> Here’s important context before you help me rewrite my bio:

  • AI search tools often can’t access dynamic content, including reviews or recent updates—so they rely heavily on static, indexable information.
  • That makes my bio a critical ranking factor, especially for queries like:
  • *“Who’s the top real estate agent in [city]?”*
  • *“Best REALTOR® for luxury homes in [city]”*
  • *“Top buyer’s agent in [city]”*
  • AI search systems prioritize profiles that demonstrate EEAT:
  • Expertise – years licensed, designations, specialties
  • Experience – production stats, transactions, types of clients served
  • Authoritativeness – awards, rankings, platform placement
  • Trustworthiness – review counts with platform references (Google, Zillow, etc.)

> Your job is to:
1. Ask me for my current bio.
2. Ask follow-up questions to extract missing EEAT data, including:
- What name should the bio be written under? (My full name or team/business name?)
_(Bio should be written in third person for better indexing and search visibility.)_
- Number of years licensed and markets served
- Total career production and average annual transactions
- Any team, brokerage, or brand affiliations that boost credibility
- Awards, achievements, and local or platform-based rankings
- Number of public reviews on:
- Google
- Zillow
- Realtor.com
- Yelp
- FastExpert
- Whether my Zillow and FastExpert profiles show updated transaction history
- Where I would rank locally based on MLS production (e.g., top 1%, top 10 agents, etc.)
- Specialties (luxury, relocation, first-time buyers, move-ups, etc.)
- Notable geographic areas, neighborhoods, or price points I focus on

> Then, rewrite my bio with these goals:
- Emphasize factual credibility using specific data points (not vague superlatives)
- Use clear, natural language that feels trustworthy and human
- Include local SEO keywords where appropriate (e.g., [city], REALTOR®, real estate agent, listing agent)
- Provide three versions of the bio:
- A short version (1–2 sentences)
- A medium version (1 paragraph, 75–125 words)
- A long version (2–3 paragraphs, fully detailed)

> Keep all versions concise but rich with *static* signals that improve AI visibility.

Show more

From the Session: This prompt was introduced in AI Recommendation Engine.

Prompts

Personalized Market Update Email Built from MLS Data

Generate personalized, data-driven market update emails using your MLS data and AI—fast, accurate, and tailored to your audience.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your Newsletter Generator — yours pulls live MLS data from mls-matrix-scraper and feeds it into The EPA Report's market-update section.
A strategy to help you create hyperlocal real estate market update emails using AI and your own MLS data. Why it matters: Sellers hire the knowledge broker — the agent who shows up with insights, not just opinions.

Personalized Market Update Email Built from MLS Data

What This Is

This is a strategy to help you create hyperlocal real estate market update emails using AI and your own MLS data.

Why does this matter? Because sellers hire the knowledge broker—the agent who shows up with insights, not just opinions. And a well-crafted market update email is one of the clearest ways to show you know your stuff.

But here’s the problem: most market reports are too broad. A citywide update or a generic monthly recap isn’t meaningful to most of your database.

What actually works? Getting specific:

A snapshot of what’s happening in a certain neighborhood

A report on listings over $1M in your area

An update tailored to investors looking at duplexes

A trend summary for homeowners who might want to downsize or move up

Those are the updates people pay attention to—but building them by hand every time is exhausting. With AI, you don’t have to. This system helps you generate high-impact, data-backed, hyperlocal market update email newsletters in a fraction of the time.

All you need is your MLS data and one prompt.

How to Use It

You’ll be using either ChatGPT, Gemini, Grok, or—my personal pick—Claude (your choice). One prompt handles everything: it’ll ask you the right questions, wait for your data, and build the newsletter.

STEP 1: Pull Your MLS Data

Start by exporting data from your MLS based on the audience or area you’re targeting.

Use filters like price range, property type, or ZIP code.

Include relevant columns like status (Closed, Expired, Withdrawn), list and close dates, price, and location.

Export your results as a CSV or Excel file.

Smaller, focused datasets work best. Example searches might include:

“Detached homes between $600K–$900K in [Area] over the past 60 days”“Expired listings over $1.2M in [ZIP] in the past quarter”“Condos with 2+ beds in [Neighborhood] sold year-to-date”

STEP 2: Paste the Prompt and Follow the Flow

Open a new chat in your AI tool. Copy and paste the prompt below. The AI will walk you through a short intake—your name, market area, brand colors, and what you want the email to accomplish. Once you’ve answered those questions, it’ll ask you to upload your spreadsheet. Then it builds your newsletter automatically.

You don’t need to run a second prompt or switch between pages. Everything happens in one conversation.

NOTE: If you’ve used the previous version of this resource, the two-prompt process has been replaced. This single prompt handles intake, data analysis, and HTML generation in one flow.

Prompt

Copy
You’re going to help me create a custom HTML email newsletter based on real estate market data I’ll provide in a spreadsheet.Before we get to the data, I need you to ask me the following questions one at a time. Wait for my answer to each before moving on:1. What is your business or brand name?2. What is the agent or sender name (for the signature and any references)?3. What market location does this update cover (e.g., “Franklin, Tennessee”)?4. What font(s) should we use in the design?5. What are your brand colors (color codes or descriptions)?6. What are your email signature details (phone, email, website, license number, etc.)?7. What is the primary goal of this email? Who are you trying to influence, and what do you want them to think, feel, or do?Once I’ve answered all seven questions, confirm my answers back to me in a summary. Then ask me to upload my MLS spreadsheet.—After I upload the spreadsheet, generate the email newsletter using these rules:PERSONALIZED GREETING– Add a personalized greeting at the top using standard merge tag syntax (e.g., {{FirstName}}).– If no name is available, default to a friendly salutation like “Friend” or “Homeowner.”– Ensure the name is converted to proper case (e.g., “john” becomes “John”; avoid all caps).DATA INTEGRITY– Use only the data provided in the spreadsheet.– Do not invent, infer, or hallucinate any additional information.– Infer all relevant market characteristics (property type, price range, sub-location) directly from the spreadsheet data without introducing anything not present.COMPELLING MARKET INSIGHT– Analyze the data to identify a single compelling insight aligned with the primary goal I specified.– Write a 3–4 sentence paragraph that clearly communicates this insight to the target audience.– Highlight a relevant, specific data point that would influence the intended behavior.– The tone should be persuasive but strictly factual—no exaggeration or speculation.OFFLINE FUNCTIONALITY– Ensure all infographic content—including charts, graphs, and imagery—renders properly in any modern web browser, even without an internet connection.CODING STANDARDS– Write clean, semantic HTML with inline CSS styling only.– Use table-based layouts and email-safe CSS properties.– Avoid external stylesheets, <style> blocks, and modern CSS features unsupported by email clients like Outlook.– The final product should be fully functional across major email clients.EXPERT DESIGN– Apply professional design principles to ensure the layout is clean, visually engaging, and easy to read.– Use the fonts and brand colors I specified.– Include clear section headers and concise supporting text to guide the reader through the content.DATA VISUALIZATION– Create charts and graphs that clearly display key trends and performance metrics using only the spreadsheet data.– Do not infer or supplement with outside information.– Keep the number of visual sections focused—typically 2 to 4 key visuals is appropriate.– The visualizations should help the audience quickly grasp what’s happening in the market and why it matters in the context of the stated goal.TITLE– Use the date range in the spreadsheet to generate a relevant and accurate title for the newsletter.OUTPUT– Return the complete HTML code in a single code block, ready to copy and paste into an email platform like Mailchimp or ActiveCampaign.– Ensure the final design reflects the branding and communication goal captured during intake.

Show more

STEP 3: Copy, Paste, and Send from Your Email Platform

Once the AI gives you the final HTML, copy the entire block of code and paste it into the HTML editor of your email platform (like Mailchimp, ActiveCampaign, etc.).

Before you send:

Carefully review for accuracy—make sure the data, insights, and language reflect what’s in your spreadsheet

Preview the email to check formatting, visuals, and mobile responsiveness

Replace any placeholder merge tags (e.g., {{FirstName}}) with the correct format for your platform

Confirm that links, buttons, brand colors, contact info, and CTAs are correct

Send a test email to yourself

Then send to your intended list

You don’t need to know code—just copy, paste, review, and send with confidence.

Tips

Smaller, more targeted datasets produce better newsletters than broad, everything-in-the-MLS exports. Think neighborhood-level, not county-level.

If the output doesn’t match your expectations, tell the AI what to change—tone, layout, which data points to emphasize. You’re in the same conversation, so it has full context to iterate.

Run this monthly or biweekly with fresh MLS pulls and you’ve got a repeatable content system that keeps you top-of-mind with your database.

DISCLAIMER: This resource provides prompts, instructions, and content to help professionals use AI tools more effectively. Because AI-generated outputs can vary, it’s your responsibility to review and refine them for accuracy, relevance, and alignment with applicable laws, industry standards, and your specific business objectives.

Market Update Email Newsletter

Prompts

Podcast Market Report from MLS Data

Analyze MLS data and generate a clear, hyperlocal market update you can use for AI-generated podcasts.

View source →
This prompt instructs language models like ChatGPT, Gemini, or Claude to analyze your attached MLS spreadsheet, highlighting key insights specifically tailored for a hyperlocal real estate market update podcast. The analysis reflects the type of MLS search you choose — specific property types, listing statuses, areas, neighborhoods, or price ranges.

Podcast Market Report from MLS Data

What This Is

This prompt instructs language models like ChatGPT, Gemini, or Claude to analyze your attached MLS spreadsheet, highlighting key insights specifically tailored for a hyperlocal real estate market update podcast. The analysis will reflect the type of MLS search you choose—whether it’s specific property types, listing statuses (e.g., sold, expired, canceled, etc.), areas or neighborhoods, property types, or price ranges.

This prompt instructs your preferred language model to analyze your attached MLS spreadsheet, highlighting key insights specifically tailored for a hyperlocal real estate market update podcast. The output will be a written synopsis of the market, so it can be easily copied and pasted into a platform like NotebookLM.

Since NotebookLM doesn’t accept spreadsheets as sources, this summary acts as a bridge—converting raw MLS data into a structured narrative that can be used by NotebookLM to generate the final podcast script or report.

How To Use It

Run the MLS search you want based on the focus of your podcast.

Download your MLS data to a spreadsheet.

Note: Procedures vary by MLS provider. Be mindful of their rules and regulations regarding data usage.

Start a new conversation in your language model (e.g., ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude) and attach/upload your spreadsheet.

Copy and paste the prompt below into your chat. The prompt already instructs the model to provide its answer using Markdown formatting, so the output will be clean, structured, and easy to read—even in platforms that don’t support manual text formatting.

Why Markdown Formatting?You may notice the use of extra characters like hashtags (#), asterisks (*), and dashes (-) in the prompt below. That’s called Markdown, a simple way to format text clearly, especially helpful when the input or messaging fields (like those used with AI chatbots such as ChatGPT) don’t support formatting such as headings, bullets, or numbered lists.

Before running the prompt, modify any portions that don’t match your MLS search, such as area, dates, property types, etc.

Prompt

Copy

## MLS Residential Real Estate Data Analysis Prompt

### Data Map (Complete before use)
– Location: [e.g. Franklin, TN]
– Listing Statuses: [e.g. Closed (Sold), Expired, Canceled]
– Date Range: [e.g. January 1, 2025 – July 13, 2025]

You are tasked with conducting an in-depth analysis of the provided MLS residential real estate data for site-built homes in {{Location}}. The dataset exclusively contains listings marked as {{Listing Statuses}}, from {{Date Range}}. Your analysis will be the foundation for insightful, impactful talking points in a podcast episode addressing current real estate market dynamics.

Important: You must not fabricate, amend, or append any data. Rely strictly on the provided MLS data to ensure the accuracy and integrity of your analysis.

Structure your analysis clearly and precisely, addressing the following key areas:

### 1. Pricing Trends
– Identify and describe the current pricing trends within the dataset. Are home prices in {{Location}} trending upward, downward, or stabilizing?
– Provide relevant statistical evidence and highlight any significant trends or changes compared to past periods.

### 2. Sold vs. Expired, Canceled, or Withdrawn Listings
– Conduct a detailed comparative analysis of homes that successfully sold versus those that expired or were canceled.
– Provide price segmentation to identify price ranges with higher success rates (sold listings) versus those more prone to expiration or cancellation.
– Examine whether certain agents or real estate firms have disproportionately higher expiration or cancellation rates.
– Highlight specific neighborhoods or areas within {{Location}} that consistently experience higher or lower rates of expired or canceled listings.

### 3. Impact of Overpricing
– Assess the effect of initial overpricing on both days on market and final sale prices.
– Clearly quantify the relationship between listing prices set above market value and their impact on eventual sales outcomes.
– Evaluate whether overpricing ultimately benefits or negatively impacts sellers.

### 4. Market Sweet Spots
– Identify and describe market “sweet spots,” including specific home types, sizes, features, or neighborhoods currently outperforming or underperforming.
– Highlight segments demonstrating quicker sales, fewer cancellations or expirations, or achieving above-average sale prices.

Ensure the output of your analysis is factual, actionable, and well-organized, suitable for direct use as discussion points during the podcast. Format the output in Markdown to ensure clear structure and readability when pasted into platforms that don’t support manual text formatting.

Show more

Prompts

Real Estate Listing Description Prompt

Create accurate, polished Public Remarks using confirmed property details and your preferred style.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your Listing Remarks Writer — production version, condition-adaptive (fixer/mid/move-in), noun-dense AEO-tuned for ChatGPT+Zillow.
A ready-to-use prompt designed to help you write professional real estate listing descriptions — specifically the Public Remarks that prospective buyers see on websites like Zillow, Realtor.com, and Redfin. The AI becomes your listing description assistant and will never invent details.

Real Estate Listing Description Prompt

What This Is

This is a ready-to-use prompt designed to help you write professional real estate listing descriptions—specifically the Public Remarks that prospective buyers see on websites like Zillow, Realtor.com, and Redfin.

When you use this prompt, the AI becomes your listing description assistant. It will:

Pull property details (beds, baths, square footage, lot size, year built, upgrades, etc.) from online sources.

Summarize themes and styles from past MLS remarks for the same property.

Ask you to confirm accuracy before writing anything.

Draft a polished description in your preferred style.

Offer revisions (shorter, longer, different tone, etc.) so you always have options.

The key thing to remember: the AI will never invent details. It only uses confirmed information and your input, so what you get back is both accurate and compelling.

How to Use It

Copy the Prompt Below Scroll down and click the little clipboard icon in the top-right corner of the prompt box. This copies the entire prompt.

Paste into ChatGPT (or Gemini, Claude, Grok, et al.) Open a new chat, paste the prompt, and hit enter. The AI will now follow these instructions for the entire conversation.

Start with the Address The very first question the AI will ask is: “What’s the property address?” Give it the full address of your listing.

Review Pulled Information The AI will search online and present two things:

Specs Found Online (all property details it could gather)

Themes / Remarks from Past Listings (a summary of how the home has been described before) It will then ask you: “Are these specs still accurate, or have there been changes?” Confirm or correct as needed.

Choose a Writing Style You can either describe the style you want (casual, professional, luxury, etc.) or share a past description as a sample. The AI will match your preferred style.

Get Your Draft The AI will write a draft listing description that:

Starts with a short, attention-grabbing headline.

Highlights standout features in the first few sentences.

Uses short, scannable paragraphs for mobile readers.

Ends with a strong call to action (like scheduling a tour).

Refine As Needed You can ask for revisions:

Shorter or longer versions

Different tones (more formal, more energetic, etc.)

Adjusted details or emphasis

That’s it—you’ll end up with a polished, accurate, and buyer-friendly listing description you can drop straight into your MLS.

Prompt

Copy

# Real Estate Listing Description Assistant Prompt

Role: You are a real estate listing description assistant.
Goal: Create an accurate, compelling property listing description that reflects verified property specs and my preferred writing style.
Important: You are writing Public Remarks only (consumer-facing listing description). Do not include Agent Remarks, showing instructions, or internal notes.

---

## Core Instructions
- Always follow these instructions for the entire conversation. If you’re ever unsure, refer back to this message.
- Begin every new workflow by asking for the property address.
- After I provide the address, search the web (MLS syndicators like Zillow, Realtor.com, Redfin, etc.) for past or current listings of that property.
- Pull back all major specifications (beds, baths, sq ft, lot size, year built, garage, basement, notable features, updates, etc.).
- Also pull back, if available:
- Tax info (parcel number, recent tax amounts).
- Community features (HOA amenities, neighborhood perks).
- Outstanding exterior features (porches, patios, landscaping, architectural style).
- Nearby establishments (schools, parks, shopping, dining, recreation).
- Summarize the themes or wording styles used in previous listing remarks.
- Present the findings back to me in two sections:
1. Specs Found Online
2. Themes / Remarks from Past Listings
- Confirm accuracy with me: “Are these specs still accurate, or have there been changes?”
- Ask me how I’d like the description to sound:
- Option A: I describe the style in my own words.
- Option B: I share a sample of past descriptions so you can infer my style.
- Write a draft Public Remarks description:
- Use only confirmed specs.
- Apply the chosen style.
- Assume no character limit unless I ask otherwise.
- After showing me the draft, always offer revisions (shorter, longer, different tone, change details, etc.).
- Never fabricate specs. Always confirm anything unclear.

---

## Formatting & Style Guidelines (Public Remarks)
- Length: Target ~200–250 words total. If character limits exist, aim for brevity.
- Structure:
1. Headline (5–10 words) that hooks readers.
2. 2–3 sentence pitch highlighting the property’s standout features.
3. Short paragraphs thereafter—easy to scan on mobile.
4. Strong call to action at the end (e.g., “Schedule your private tour today.”)
- Tone & Content:
- Begin with a compelling hook.
- Tell the story—invite buyers to imagine life in the home.
- Be honest and transparent; avoid overhype or misleading phrases.
- Spotlight real value drivers—renovations, premium finishes, energy-efficient features, unique design elements.
- Use clear, vivid, but truthful language. Avoid clichés (“cozy,” “fixer-upper”) unless intentionally reframed.

---

## Initial Intake
To get started, ask me this exact question:

“What’s the property address?”

Show more

From the Session: This prompt was introduced in AI Listing Content Kit.

Prompts

Sales Scoreboard: Listing Agent Comparison Dashboard

Generate an anonymized leaderboard that visualizes listing agent performance using your MLS data.

View source →
This prompt builds an interactive Artifact inside Claude that analyzes and visualizes how listing agents are performing in your market — without naming names. Anonymizes each agent as "Agent A," "Agent B," etc., while showcasing key stats like number of listings sold, list-to-close success rate, and price reductions.

Sales Scoreboard: Listing Agent Comparison Dashboard

What This Is

This prompt builds an interactive Artifact inside Claude that analyzes and visualizes how listing agents are performing in your market—without naming names. It anonymizes each agent as “Agent A,” “Agent B,” etc., while showcasing key stats like number of listings sold, list-to-close success rate, and price reductions.

It’s ideal for competitive research or internal presentations where you want the data to speak for itself, not stir up drama. The output is a portable HTML file that works offline and can be easily shared, printed, or embedded.

How To Use It

Export your MLS data for the time frame and market you want to analyze. Make sure it includes agent names, listing statuses (like closed, expired, cancelled), list prices, and relevant performance data.

Save the file as a .csv or .txt, depending on your MLS export options.

If you’d like your name to appear unmasked in the scorecard (and even highlighted), be sure to tell Claude in a follow-up prompt. For example, say: “Please keep my name, Jane Doe, visible in the report.” All other agents will be anonymized.

Create a simple brand kit with your logo, hex color codes, and font preferences. If you don’t have a formal one, just list the info in a doc and upload it with the prompt.

Paste the full prompt (below) into Claude. Upload your data file and brand kit when prompted. Claude will return a fully interactive .html file that works offline, includes sortable tables, and highlights agent performance anonymously—except yours, if you choose.

Use this scorecard to evaluate trends, performance gaps, or pricing strategies across your market, all while keeping the data private and insightful.

Prompt

Copy

You are a top-tier data visualization expert and web developer. Your task is to create a fully self-contained HTML Artifact called the “Listing Agent Sales Scorecard.” The market or geographic region should be inferred directly from the dataset. Follow these rules:

Data Embedding:
- Use only the MLS data provided in the attached spreadsheet. Embed this data inline (e.g., JSON or JavaScript arrays) to ensure the Artifact is self-contained.
- Avoid relying on external APIs, databases, or other data sources.

Libraries and Dependencies:
- Use embedded JavaScript libraries (e.g., D3.js) or write custom JavaScript to create interactive visualizations like leaderboards, pie charts, or bar graphs.
- Ensure all dependencies are included in the file, so it functions offline.

Interactive Features:
- Include sortable tables or dropdown menus that let users filter agents by metrics such as total listings sold, listing success rates, and price reductions.
- When analyzing listing success rates, use the column in the dataset that specifies listing status (e.g., closed, sold, expired, cancelled, withdrawn). Any status other than “closed” or “sold” means the agent did not succeed in selling the property and should be treated accordingly.
- Anonymize all agent names in the dataset by replacing them with generic identifiers (e.g., “Agent A,” “Agent B”) to maintain privacy—except for the user’s own name, if provided separately. If the user specifies their name (e.g., “Keep ‘Jane Doe’ visible”), do not anonymize it. Instead, visually highlight their results within the interface.
- Add visual highlights to showcase market leaders and their performance.

Branding and Styling:
- Apply consistent visual styling using the attached brand kit, which includes colors (hex codes), font names, and a logo file.
- If no formal brand kit is provided, use clean, modern design defaults and prompt the user for key visual preferences if needed.

Output:
- Provide a downloadable .html file that functions fully offline and opens in any browser.

The final result should be visually compelling, data-rich, and completely portable for offline use.

Show more

Prompts

Schema Markup for Real Estate Listings

Add the right data behind your listings so they appear accurately in buyers' AI searches and conversations.

View source →
⚠️ GAP — BUILD No personal skill yet. Planned: Schema Builder — auto-generates valid JSON-LD for RealEstateListing, FAQPage, VideoObject, Person+E-E-A-T, LocalBusiness, Article. ~3-4 hrs to build. See Gaps to Build.
Add schema markup (JSON-LD) to your property listings so AI systems like ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overview, and Gemini can better understand and surface your listings in conversational home searches. According to Realtor.com research, 82% of Americans are already using AI tools to explore the housing market.

Schema Markup for Real Estate Listings

What This Is

This resource explains how to add schema markup (JSON-LD) to your property listings so AI systems like ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overview, and Gemini can better understand and surface your listings in conversational home searches.

Why this matters: According to Realtor.com research, 82% of Americans are already using AI tools to explore the housing market. And they’re not searching the way they used to.

Cornell University found that AI users don’t type short keyword phrases like “homes in Brentwood.” Instead, they write full wish lists in long, detailed prompts—things like:

“Homes under $1.2M near Starbucks, with a fenced yard, sunset views, and a primary suite on the main level.”

If your listing page doesn’t label those details in structured data, AI systems like ChatGPT may not realize your property matches the buyer’s search. Today’s buyers give AI every detail up front—price, features, location, and proximity to specific places—so without schema, your listing can be skipped entirely.

You may also remember Zillow’s ChatGPT integration, which allows direct home searches inside ChatGPT via Zillow. There are several resources in the Academy on this topic:

Zillow–ChatGPT Apps SDK webinar replay

Zillow ChatGPT listing optimization prompt

Zillow ChatGPT branded email announcement

Here’s the catch: most users won’t realize they must manually enable the Zillow app. So while this feature drives more people to use AI for home search, manyof those searches will still pull from the open web—not Zillow alone. That’s the opportunity: by adding schema markup, your listings can appear more frequently when buyers use AI to search for homes, even if the Zillow app isn’t connected.

How to Use This

Skim the prompt below. It’s already written for you — no edits needed.

Open a new ChatGPT chat. Paste the entire prompt as-is.

Follow the questions. Follow the questions. If you’ve already used the Zillow ChatGPT Listing Optimization Prompt to create keyword-optimized public remarks or a detailed feature list, paste that here to skip ahead. Otherwise, just answer the short intake questions (platform, URL, property details, and features).

ChatGPT will generate your schema code. You’ll receive a ready-to-paste JSON-LD block and step-by-step instructions for installing it on your site.

Add it to your website. The prompt includes built-in guidance for different website platforms—whether you’re using Sierra Interactive, Real Estate Webmasters, Ylopo, CINC, Real Geeks, Lofty, or another provider. It will walk you through exactly how to add the code in your specific setup.

Validate your schema: Use Google’s Rich Results Test and Schema Markup Validator.

Keep it current: If beds, baths, price, or features change, re-run the prompt and replace the code.

Tip: If someone else manages your website, forward them the code and instructions that ChatGPT provides — they’ll know exactly what to do.

Copy and paste the prompt below into ChatGPT to generate your listing schema.

Prompt

Copy

# 🏡 Schema Optimization Assistant for Real Estate Listings

You are a schema optimization assistant for real estate listing agents.

AI systems like ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overview, Gemini, and others now retrieve property information through web crawling and structured data (called *schema*).
Even if Zillow’s ChatGPT integration isn’t used, these systems interpret listing pages through schema markup to understand a home’s features, location, and relevance to buyer searches.

Your job is to help the agent generate and install JSON-LD schema markup that accurately describes their property so that AI systems can discover and rank it in conversational searches.

---

### ⚙️ OBJECTIVE

Create a property-specific schema markup that:

  1. Uses @type: RealEstateListing with a nested itemOffered of type SingleFamilyResidence (or another subtype if applicable).
  2. Embeds key, factual details: address, beds, baths, square footage, price, photos, geo coordinates, and descriptive features.
  3. Converts human-friendly phrasing into structured data fields (amenityFeature, keywords, description).
  4. Returns clear, plain-English instructions for how to add the markup to the user’s website (Sierra Interactive, Real Geeks, Lofty, or via Google Tag Manager).

---

### 🧱 COMPLIANCE GUARDRAILS

  • Follow Fair Housing and RESPA standards (no demographic or lifestyle bias).
  • Use factual, place-based statements only.
  • Avoid words like *walkable* or *family-friendly*; use distance or neutral phrasing (e.g., “0.4 mi to Sevier Park”).
  • Exclude all agent or brokerage info.
  • Keep tone factual and compliant.

---

### 🧭 PHASE 1: INTAKE (Branching)

Start naturally:
> “I’ll help you create schema markup so AI systems and search engines can understand your listing.
> If you already have a feature list or optimized remarks, we can skip ahead.”

#### A) Skip-the-Line (Preferred)
Ask:
- “Do you already have a feature list or keyword-optimized remarks for this property?”
- If yes:
- Paste them here.
- I’ll reuse those for amenityFeature and keywords.
- Then I’ll confirm any missing details (price, beds, baths, sqft, images).
- If no: Move to section B.

#### B) Standard Intake
Ask these questions:
1. “What’s your website platform (Sierra Interactive, Real Geeks, Lofty, or other)?”
- *(Default to Sierra Interactive for instructions if unspecified.)*
2. “What’s the property address or a listing URL?”
- If the URL is accessible, I’ll pull any visible facts and nearby places.
- Otherwise, I’ll rely on what you paste.
3. “Provide the core property facts — price, beds, baths, square footage, lot size (optional), and neighborhood.”
4. “List 5–10 key features (comma-separated):
e.g., primary on main, near Starbucks, sunset views, fenced yard, EV outlet, chef’s kitchen.”
5. “Optional: Upload 3–5 listing photos so I can spot visual features. If you can’t upload, no problem.”

Then:
- Consolidate all confirmed features.
- Map them into amenityFeature and keywords.
- Confirm any uncertainties before generating the schema.

---

### 🧩 PHASE 2: DATA STRUCTURE + MAPPING

Build a JSON-LD object with:

  • "@context": "https://schema.org"
  • "@type": "RealEstateListing"
  • Nested "itemOffered" of type SingleFamilyResidence (or other if applicable)
  • Address, price, beds, baths, square footage, images, listing URL
  • geo (lat/long if provided)
  • amenityFeature array (@type: LocationFeatureSpecification, each with name and value: true)
  • keywords array for searchable phrases
  • description written in plain language summarizing the property.

---

### ✍️ PHASE 3: GENERATE + EXPLAIN THE SCHEMA (SIMPLY)

After collecting all the details, generate the schema and explain it clearly, assuming the user has never seen code before.

Say something like:

> “Below is a short piece of code that tells AI systems what this home is and what it offers — similar to how a caption tells a photo what’s in the picture.
> You’ll copy and paste this into your website (or add it with Google Tag Manager if your site doesn’t let you paste scripts).
> You may be asked if you can help with how to do this or offer to help — that’s totally fine. Just walk them through the same steps explained below.”

Then:

1. Show the complete schema inside:
```html
<script type="application/ld+json">
{ … }
</script>

Prompts

Seller Update: Detailed Listing Performance Report

Generate a branded, interactive HTML report that shows sellers exactly how their listing is performing.

View source →
ENHANCED BY Your Weekly Listing Update — automated weekly-cadence version of this report.
This prompt powers a dynamic report you can generate inside Claude using its Artifact feature. The result is a beautifully branded HTML file that gives sellers a clear, interactive snapshot of their home's performance — online views, open houses, showing activity, and more. Built for transparency.

Seller Update: Detailed Listing Performance Report

What This Is

This prompt powers a dynamic report you can generate inside Claude using its Artifact feature. The result is a beautifully branded HTML file that gives sellers a clear, interactive snapshot of their home’s performance—online views, open houses, showing activity, and more.

It’s built for transparency. When homes take longer to sell, this report helps you show the work, reinforce trust, and guide sellers with confidence. Plus, it’s completely self-contained—offline ready and printable with just one click.

How To Use It

Start by grabbing the sample spreadsheet provided here: Seller Insights Widget Report Template. You can use it as-is or customize it with your own metrics.

Upload that spreadsheet into Claude when you run the prompt. That’s the data source the Artifact will pull from—nothing else.

Next, upload your brand kit. This can include your logo, fonts, and any color guidelines. Claude will apply your branding directly into the HTML design. If you don’t have one, just list them in a doc and save it as a PDF or Word file.

Then, paste the full prompt (below) into Claude. It will generate a downloadable HTML file that works offline and looks great in any browser.

Open the file and review it. You’ll find dynamic charts, filters, and a print-friendly layout already built in.

From there, you can send the report to your seller, print it out for meetings, or screen share it during calls. It’s built to instill trust and show that you’ve been doing the work—even when days on market start to climb.

Prompt

Copy

You are a world-class web developer and designer tasked with creating a fully self-contained HTML Artifact called the “[Property Address] Market Performance Review.” This tool will provide home sellers with an interactive, visually appealing summary of their property’s performance on the market. Follow these rules:

Data Embedding:
- Use only the data provided in the attached spreadsheet. Embed this data directly into the HTML file using inline JSON or JavaScript.  
- Do not use external data sources or make API calls.

Libraries and Dependencies:
- Use pure HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. If you need a library (e.g., Chart.js), embed its minified version directly in the file.  
- Ensure no reliance on external links or resources.

Interactive Features:
- Include dropdown menus or filters for data visualization, allowing users to view trends (e.g., weekly changes in online views, showings, and/or offers).  
- Add dynamic charts and graphs to display key performance metrics, such as a line graph for showings or a pie chart for offers.  
- Include a “Print/Export PDF” button that generates a printer-friendly version optimized for standard 8.5×11 inch paper, with appropriate margins, scaling, and page breaks.

Branding and Styling:
- Use the attached brand kit to ensure the Artifact aligns with branding guidelines (fonts, colors, and design aesthetics). Use judgement about incorporating gradient color patterns.  
- Implement print-specific styling that maintains brand consistency while optimizing for print output.

Output:
- Provide a downloadable .html file that works offline and can be opened directly in any browser. Verify its functionality without internet access.  
- Ensure the print/export functionality produces well-formatted output with centered content, proper spacing, and readable font sizes.

The final result should be visually stunning, user-friendly, and entirely portable for offline use.

Show more

DISCLAIMER: This resource provides prompts, instructions, and content to help professionals use AI tools more effectively. Because AI-generated outputs can vary, it’s your responsibility to review and refine them for accuracy, relevance, and alignment with applicable laws, industry standards, and your specific business objectives.
