Personal Custom Prompts (3)

Prompts and skills curated by Graeham from external sources. Each is also installed as a real Claude Skill in Documents/Claude/Skills/skills/ — invoke by name in Cowork or paste the full prompt into a Claude Project.

Personal Prompt ★

Personal Language Tutor 📊

Adaptive AI tutor that teaches any language through conversation, not lectures. 10-question onboarding (saved forever), live level assessment, structured daily lesson loop (warm-up → new material → practice → corrections → wrap-up), vocabulary tracking with NEW/LEARNING/KNOWN states, immersive role-plays with difficulty scaling, line-by-line writing review for handwritten image uploads, weekly CEFR progress reports (A0→C1), cultural context, and graceful handling of frustration / forgetting / trips / "explain in English."

Source: mavgpt.ai — Claude Language Tutor Setup Guide, 2026 Edition. Curated 2026-05-14. Also installed as a real Claude Skill at Documents/Claude/Skills/skills/language-tutor/SKILL.md — auto-fires on phrases like "teach me Spanish", "let's role-play in French", "quiz me on this week's vocab", "I have a trip to [country]", or "check my handwriting" with an image upload.

How it works

9-step structured system. Step 1 runs once (10 onboarding questions, saved forever). Steps 2-9 cycle every session: live level check → daily lesson loop → vocab + grammar progression with NEW/LEARNING/KNOWN states → role-plays → writing review → weekly CEFR check → cultural context → graceful situation handling. Hard rules: never more than 7 new words per session, never a grammar lecture longer than 3 sentences, always celebrate small wins.

When to use it

  • Learning any new language (Spanish, French, Mandarin, Italian, etc.)
  • Polishing fluency in a language you already partially speak
  • Preparing for a specific event (trip in 3 months, exam, business meeting)
  • Daily structured practice that adapts to your time (5 min to 1 hour)
  • Heritage language reconnection
  • Image-uploading handwritten practice for line-by-line review

How to activate it (3 setup options)

Option 1 — Claude Project: claude.ai → Projects → Create Project → name "Language Tutor" → paste full prompt below into custom instructions → start a chat. Requires Claude Pro/Max.

Option 2 — Cowork skill: say run the language-tutor skill or just "teach me Spanish" — auto-fires from the trigger phrases. Canonical SKILL.md at Documents/Claude/Skills/skills/language-tutor/SKILL.md.

Option 3 — Daily check-in: Cowork → Schedule a Task → daily at your preferred time → prompt: "Run today's language lesson — warm-up review then 5 new words about [topic]."

▶ Show full prompt (paste into Claude Project) 
You are a personal language tutor who adapts to each learner's level, goals, and pace.

You teach through conversation, not lectures. You correct mistakes without killing momentum. You make the language feel usable from day one, not academic. Every session is built for THIS person at THEIR level with THEIR goals.

--- STEP 1: LEARN THE STUDENT (FIRST TIME ONLY) ---
The first time someone uses this, ask these questions. Save every answer permanently. Never ask again unless they say something changed.
1. What language do you want to learn?
2. What's your current level? (Complete beginner = zero knowledge. Beginner = know some basics like greetings. Intermediate = can hold simple conversations. Advanced = conversational but want to polish grammar/fluency.)
3. Why are you learning this language?
4. Do you have a specific timeline?
5. How much time can you practice per day?
6. Do you learn better through reading, listening, writing, or speaking practice?
7. Any specific topics you want to focus on?
8. Do you speak any other languages?
9. Have you tried learning this language before? What worked and what didn't?
10. Do you prefer to be corrected immediately, or after?

[Full prompt in sources/personal-prompts/language-tutor.md and SKILL.md]

Full text saved at Documents/Claude/AI-Library/sources/personal-prompts/language-tutor.md and the canonical Claude Skill at Documents/Claude/Skills/skills/language-tutor/SKILL.md.

Related

  • No direct AiM equivalent — Pantana doesn't cover language learning.
  • Pairs with Schedule for daily check-in automation.
Personal Prompt ★

Blader Humanizer 📊

Removes 29 signs of AI-generated writing from text. Based on Wikipedia's "Signs of AI writing" guide maintained by WikiProject AI Cleanup. 18.8k stars · MIT licensed. Detects significance inflation, AI vocabulary ("delve", "leverage"), em dash overuse, copula avoidance, rule of three, signposting, sycophantic tone, generic conclusions, and 22 more patterns. Includes voice-calibration mode where you paste 2-3 paragraphs of your own writing and it rewrites in YOUR style instead of generic "clean" output.

Source: github.com/blader/humanizer by blader, MIT licensed. Cloned to Documents/Claude/Skills/skills/humanizer/ on 2026-05-14. Auto-fires on /humanizer command in Claude Code, or in Cowork by saying "humanize this text" with content to clean. Includes a final "obviously AI-generated" audit pass + second-pass rewrite to catch lingering AI-isms.

How it works

Two-pass rewrite system. First pass detects and rewrites the 29 known AI-writing patterns (content patterns, language patterns, style patterns, communication patterns, filler/hedging, persuasive tropes). Second pass runs an "obviously AI-generated" audit on the first draft and catches anything that slipped through. Voice calibration mode: paste a sample of your own writing first, and the rewrite matches your sentence rhythm, word choices, and quirks instead of generic "clean" output.

When to use it

  • Right before publishing any AI-assisted blog post, listing remarks, email, LinkedIn post
  • Cleaning up content from content-creation-engine before posting
  • Listing remarks from listing-remarks-writer that need a final humanization pass
  • Newsletter sections from newsletter-generator (The EPA Report)
  • Any time the writing sounds "AI-ish" — em dashes, "delve," "in today's fast-paced world"
  • Brand voice consistency across multiple agents (use voice calibration mode)

How to activate it

In Claude Code or OpenCode: /humanizer then paste your text. (Note: the slash command is /humanizer NOT /human.)

In Cowork: say run the humanizer skill or "humanize this text: [paste]" — canonical SKILL.md at Documents/Claude/Skills/skills/humanizer/SKILL.md.

Voice calibration: /humanizer\n\nHere's a sample of my writing for voice matching: [paste 2-3 paragraphs]\n\nNow humanize this text: [paste AI text]

▶ Show before / after example

BEFORE (AI-sounding):

"AI-assisted coding serves as an enduring testament to the transformative potential of large language models, marking a pivotal moment in the evolution of software development. In today's rapidly evolving technological landscape, these groundbreaking tools—nestled at the intersection of research and practice—are reshaping how engineers ideate, iterate, and deliver, underscoring their vital role in modern workflows. At its core, the value proposition is clear: streamlining processes, enhancing collaboration, and fostering alignment."

AFTER (humanized):

"AI coding assistants can speed up the boring parts of the job. They're great at boilerplate: config files and the little glue code you don't want to write. They can also help you sketch a test, but you still have to read it. The dangerous part is how confident the suggestions look. I've accepted code that compiled and passed lint, then discovered later it missed the point because I stopped paying attention. If you treat it like autocomplete and review every line, it's useful. If you use it to avoid thinking, it will help you ship bugs faster. The only real backstop is tests. Without them, you're mostly judging vibes."

Pairs with

  • Content Creation Engine — run humanizer on the Phase 5 script output before posting
  • Listing Remarks Writer — final humanization pass before MLS submission
  • Newsletter Generator — clean each section before sending
  • Copywriter — produces, then humanizer polishes
Personal Prompt ★

Identity Mirror — 5-Year Self-Audit 📊

A four-prompt sequence run inside one ChatGPT chat that already has long memory of you. It turns the model into a mirror: build a complete profile of who you are → name the contradictions between your words and actions → project the exact version of you in five years if nothing changes → then flip it to the five-year version if you fix your single biggest blind spot starting today. The leverage is in running all four in order in the same thread — each answer feeds the next.

Source: Personal — saved by Graeham Watts, 2026-05-31. Run in ChatGPT on an account with memory enabled and real conversation history (otherwise it has nothing to mirror). Paste the prompts one at a time, in order, in the same chat. Full text saved at Documents/Claude/AI-Library/sources/personal-prompts/identity-mirror.md.

The prompts (run in order, same chat)

Prompt 1 — Profile:

Using only things I've actually said or done across our conversations, build a profile of me: communication style, values, what I'm insecure about, what I'm working toward. Cite the specific behavior behind each claim. Tag every claim as fact / strong inference / weak guess. Where you have no data for a section, say so instead of filling it in. No flattery — if the evidence is unflattering, say it plainly.

Prompt 2 — Contradictions:

Where do my words not match my actions? Give me the specific quote or behavior on each side of the gap. Rank them by how much each is actually costing me, and separate real contradictions from things that are just normal human tension. End with the one that matters most and why.

Prompt 3 — Dark projection:

If I keep operating exactly as I do now, give me the most likely version of me in five years — not the worst case — across career, relationships, health, mindset. Ground each prediction in a current behavior. Flag which domains you have real data on and which are pattern-inference. Give me your honest odds, not a dramatized story.

Prompt 4 — Fixed projection:

Name my single highest-leverage blind spot and justify why that one over the others. Then map the five-year fixed version with dated milestones — including what the fix costs me and what would make it fail. Keep the same evidence and confidence discipline.

When to use it

  • Annual or quarterly self-review / goal reset
  • When you feel stuck and can't name why
  • Before a big life or career decision
  • New Year / birthday reflection
